hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify reconnaissance traffic that fans out across services or hosts with low-byte, low-packet conversations.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface unmanaged sources performing scan-like fan-out across many destinations or services.
prerequisites: Arkime session metadata is indexed for the scoped traffic and time window.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can isolate scan fan-out directly through session count, port spread, and surrounding traffic pivots.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Scan Fan-Out Hunt for T1595 Active Scanning
Technique Context
- Look for short, low-byte sessions from one source to many destinations or service ports.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && packets <= <LOW_PACKET_THRESHOLD> && bytes <= <SCAN_BYTES_THRESHOLD> && (port.dst == 21 || port.dst == 22 || port.dst == 23 || port.dst == 25 || port.dst == 53 || port.dst == 80 || port.dst == 443 || port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 3389 || port.dst == 5985 || port.dst == 5986)
What the search isolates: Candidate scan traffic where the source is fanning out instead of maintaining normal business sessions.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by ip.src and then port.dst to find sources touching many targets.
2. Pivot into adjacent sessions from the same source to separate recon from follow-on exploitation.
Validation steps:
- Confirm destination or service-port fan-out with consistently small sessions.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Vulnerability scanners, inventory appliances, and approved testing.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when an unmanaged source scans broadly or the sweep is followed by access or payload retrieval.
hunt_methods.title: T1595 Scan Fan-Out Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify broad probing across unusual or ephemeral service ports.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface broad high-port probing that often precedes service identification or exploit selection.
prerequisites: Arkime session metadata is indexed for the scoped traffic and time window.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime is good at showing high-port probe spread, timing, and the pivot from recon into follow-on traffic.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime High-Port Sweep Hunt for T1595 Active Scanning
Technique Context
- Find sources touching many high or unusual ports with tiny sessions that resemble broad probing rather than user traffic.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && packets <= <LOW_PACKET_THRESHOLD> && bytes <= <SCAN_BYTES_THRESHOLD> && port.dst >= 1024
What the search isolates: Sources that are sampling many high ports and may be looking for exposed services beyond the common admin set.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by ip.src and port.dst to find wide high-port coverage.
2. Check whether the same source later returns to a smaller set of ports with longer sessions.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source is probing many high ports or multiple peers in a compressed window.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Research scans, inventory jobs, or approved red-team sweeps.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a new or unmanaged source moves from broad high-port probing into persistent sessions or payload transfer.
hunt_methods.title: T1595 High-Port Sweep Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify broad address-space discovery through repeated short sessions to many destinations.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface sources enumerating address space rather than talking to a normal peer set.
prerequisites: Arkime sees the relevant east-west or north-south segments in scope.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show source-to-many-destination patterns cleanly in session metadata.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime IP Block Sweep Hunt for T1595.001 Scanning IP Blocks
Technique Context
- Focus on one source touching many destinations inside the same range with short sessions and little payload.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && packets <= <LOW_PACKET_THRESHOLD> && bytes <= <SCAN_BYTES_THRESHOLD>
What the search isolates: Low-volume sessions that can be grouped by source and destination range to reveal broad IP-space enumeration.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by ip.src and then review destination concentration by subnet or range.
2. Pivot into later sessions from the same source to see whether any discovered hosts receive longer follow-on conversations.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source traverses a wide destination set rather than a normal peer list.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Asset inventory, network mapping, and approved scanning infrastructure.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a non-scanner host sweeps ranges and then returns to selected systems for access or transfer.
hunt_methods.title: T1595.001 IP Block Sweep Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify vulnerability assessment and exploit-preparation activity aimed at exposed web applications.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface web sessions that resemble scanner-driven exploit preparation rather than normal browsing.
prerequisites: HTTP parser fields and user-agent metadata are available for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can isolate exploit-surface probing through HTTP metadata and follow-on session review.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Exploit Surface Probe Hunt for T1595.002 Vulnerability Scanning
Technique Context
- Look for web requests and user-agents that resemble exploit preparation, panel discovery, or automated assessment.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((http.uri == /(?i)(wp-login|owa|autodiscover|\\.git|cgi-bin|phpmyadmin|manager\\/html|jenkins|actuator|api-docs)/) || (http.user-agent == /(?i)(nessus|openvas|nikto|nuclei|acunetix|qualys|burp)/))
What the search isolates: HTTP sessions that line up with known vulnerability assessment behavior or exploit-relevant paths.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, http.user-agent, bytes, packets
Session-review workflow:
1. Separate approved testing infrastructure from opportunistic external probing.
2. Review nearby sessions from the same source for uploads, auth attempts, or shell access.
Validation steps:
- Confirm exploit-relevant paths or scanner identifiers are repeated against one or more targets.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Approved vuln scanning, attack-surface management, or QA testing.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when the source is not approved and the probe is followed by successful application interaction.
hunt_methods.title: T1595.002 Exploit Surface Probe Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify path and content enumeration that looks like wordlist-driven web recon.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface dictionary-like path enumeration against web applications.
prerequisites: HTTP parser fields are available for the scoped application traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show repeated exploit-path and admin-path discovery against the same application target.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Wordlist Path Enumeration Hunt for T1595.003 Wordlist Scanning
Technique Context
- Look for one source requesting many candidate admin, backup, and hidden paths in quick succession.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && http.uri == /(?i)(admin|login|backup|old|test|dev|config|dump|git|svn|env)/
What the search isolates: Requests that resemble dictionary-driven path discovery rather than normal user navigation.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, http.user-agent, http.method
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by ip.src and host.http to find repeated path probing against the same application.
2. Check whether the same source later accesses one discovered path with a longer or more interactive session.
Validation steps:
- Confirm many candidate paths are touched in a compressed time window.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- QA tests, monitoring checks, or vulnerability assessment tooling.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when an unmanaged source enumerates many candidate paths and later settles on one with sustained interaction.
hunt_methods.title: T1595.003 Wordlist Path Enumeration Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify internal service enumeration that can precede remote access, exploitation, or lateral movement.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface internal service-discovery behavior from sources that should not be mapping management and application ports.
prerequisites: Arkime is capturing the east-west traffic segments relevant to the hunt.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can directly show low-byte internal port fan-out and the transition from discovery into access or transfer.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Internal Service Census Hunt for T1046 Network Service Discovery
Technique Context
- Find internal hosts enumerating management and application services across east-west paths with short, low-payload sessions.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && packets <= <LOW_PACKET_THRESHOLD> && bytes <= <SCAN_BYTES_THRESHOLD> && (port.dst == 22 || port.dst == 80 || port.dst == 443 || port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 135 || port.dst == 139 || port.dst == 3389 || port.dst == 5985 || port.dst == 5986 || port.dst == 1433 || port.dst == 1521 || port.dst == 3306 || port.dst == 5432 || port.dst == 6379 || port.dst == 9200 || port.dst == 27017)
What the search isolates: Short internal service probes that can indicate service discovery rather than normal peer communication.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by ip.src and port.dst to find hosts sweeping many peers or many services.
2. Compare the source against approved scanners, management servers, patching systems, and monitoring tools.
3. Pivot into later sessions from the same source to see whether discovery leads to SMB, RDP, SSH, WinRM, payload transfer, or outbound tasking.
Validation steps:
- Confirm unusual internal destination breadth, service-port fan-out, or a new source performing census activity.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Monitoring, patching, backup, vulnerability scanning, and management infrastructure.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when an unmanaged or user workstation source enumerates internal services and then pivots into remote access, staging, or external communications.
hunt_methods.title: T1046 Internal Service Census Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify external remote-service use that can support initial footholds, persistence, or unauthorized remote access.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface unusual use of external remote access surfaces from suspicious, novel, or off-hours sources.
prerequisites: The relevant remote access gateway traffic is visible in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show who is reaching external remote access services, when, and what surrounding traffic follows.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime External Remote Service Gate Hunt for T1133 External Remote Services
Technique Context
- Review outbound or inbound remote-service access to externally reachable admin, VPN, bastion, and gateway surfaces that stand out for the source host or time window.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_EXTERNAL_SCOPE>) && ((port.dst == 22 || port.dst == 3389 || port.dst == 8443 || port.dst == 4443 || port.dst == 500 || port.dst == 4500 || port.dst == 1194 || port.dst == 51820) || host.http == /(?i)(vpn|ssl-vpn|globalprotect|fortinet|fortigate|pulse|citrix|netscaler|rdweb|bastion|jump|guacamole)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(vpn|ssl-vpn|globalprotect|fortinet|fortigate|pulse|citrix|netscaler|rdweb|bastion|jump|guacamole)/) && packets > <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS>
What the search isolates: Interactive sessions to externally reachable remote access surfaces that deserve comparison against approved access paths.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen, host.http, dns.host, ja3, cert.subject.cn
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the source, destination, protocol, and time window match approved VPN, bastion, or helpdesk workflows.
2. Pivot into nearby sessions from the same source for staging, cloud admin activity, remote support tooling, or external data transfer.
3. Pivot by destination certificate, JA3, and hostnames to find other endpoints using the same remote-service path.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the path is new, rare, off-hours, or inconsistent with the source host and identity context.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Approved VPN, bastion, helpdesk, vendor support, and emergency administrative access.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a new or rare external remote-service path is tied to scanning, payload retrieval, cloud administration, or egress behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1133 External Remote Service Gate Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify browser-driven delivery chains that transition into suspicious object retrieval and post-delivery communications.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface browser sessions that look more like exploit delivery or fake-update flows than normal browsing.
prerequisites: HTTP metadata is available for the scoped browsing traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show the chain from page retrieval into suspicious object transfer and adjacent follow-on activity.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Browser To Payload Chain Hunt for T1189 Drive-by Compromise
Technique Context
- Find browser-like web sessions that pivot quickly from page retrieval into large object downloads or suspicious follow-on traffic.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && http.method == GET && http.user-agent == /(?i)(mozilla|chrome|safari|edge|firefox)/ && (http.uri == /(?i)\.(zip|rar|7z|iso|img|dll|exe|msi|jar|ps1|vbs|js|hta)$/ || bytes >= <PAYLOAD_RETRIEVAL_BYTES>)
What the search isolates: Browser-driven sessions that may reflect exploit delivery, fake update flows, or drive-by payload retrieval.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, http.user-agent, bytes, packets
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the host normally browses to the destination or retrieves executables from it.
2. Pivot into later sessions from the same host for C2, discovery, or remote access.
Validation steps:
- Confirm browser-like traffic is followed by file-bearing transfers or suspicious outbound sessions.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Legitimate software downloads, browser updates, or admin-driven file retrieval.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when browser traffic to a rare destination is quickly followed by payload retrieval and post-delivery network activity.
hunt_methods.title: T1189 Browser To Payload Chain Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify suspicious application-layer requests that commonly precede exploitation or web-shell placement.

hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.

# Arkime Exploit Surface Abuse Hunt for T1190 Exploit Public-Facing Application
Technique Context
- Review HTTP methods, exploit-relevant paths, and abnormal client behavior against exposed applications.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((http.method == POST || http.method == PUT || http.method == PROPFIND) || (http.uri == /(?i)(wp-admin|wp-login|\/owa\/|autodiscover|jmx-console|manager\/html|api\/jsonws|\.git\/|cgi-bin|struts|ecp\/)/))
What the search isolates: Sessions that look like exploit staging, panel abuse, or shell placement attempts against exposed services.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, http.method, http.user-agent, bytes, packets
Session-review workflow:
1. Review targeted paths and methods to decide whether the traffic reflects exploit staging or routine admin activity.
2. Pivot into adjacent sessions from the same source for uploads, command execution, or callbacks.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source hits exploit-relevant paths or unusual write-capable methods.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Approved app testing, admin operations, or vulnerability assessment.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when suspicious path access is followed by shell access, payload retrieval, or outbound tasking from the target.
hunt_methods.title: T1190 Exploit Surface Abuse Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify staging, upload, and callback patterns that follow suspicious public-facing application abuse.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface likely post-exploit staging and follow-on callbacks after suspicious application abuse.
prerequisites: HTTP parser fields are retained for the scoped public-facing traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because it ties exploit-surface abuse to the traffic patterns that matter most after likely exploitation succeeds.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Web Shell Staging Follow On Hunt for T1190 Exploit Public-Facing Application
Technique Context
- Focus on what happens immediately after likely exploit traffic: uploads, rare admin paths, and new outbound sessions from the target.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((http.uri == /(?i)(cmd|shell|upload|filemanager|aspnet_client|webroot|tmp|temp)/) || bytes >= <PAYLOAD_RETRIEVAL_BYTES>)
What the search isolates: Follow-on application sessions that may indicate successful staging, shell placement, or operator-controlled post-exploit activity.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, http.method, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review suspicious follow-on paths and uploads from the same source after exploit-prep traffic.
2. Pivot into new outbound sessions from the target to see whether compromise led to tasking or payload retrieval.
Validation steps:
- Confirm exploit-like requests are closely followed by staging or callback behavior.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Legitimate file management panels, application upload paths, or admin workflows.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when likely exploit traffic is followed by upload-like sessions and new outbound communications from the target.
hunt_methods.title: T1190 Web Shell Staging Follow On Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify remote service abuse that moves from probing or failed sessions into interactive or sustained access.

# Arkime Remote Service Exploit Transition Hunt for T1210 Exploitation of Remote Services
Technique Context
- Look for repeated short service sessions that quickly transition into longer or more interactive conversations to the same target.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 22 || port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 3389 || port.dst == 5985 || port.dst == 5986)
What the search isolates: Candidate remote-service abuse where a target first receives probe-like traffic and then sustained sessions.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Review sequence changes from short probes into longer sessions against the same host.
2. Pivot into nearby traffic for payload transfer, remote admin protocols, or new outbound sessions from the target.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source-target pair is not a standard admin path and shows a clear transition into sustained traffic.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Legitimate retries, load balancer health checks, or approved administrative access.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when probe-like traffic to a remote service is followed by sustained sessions and additional post-access network behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1210 Remote Service Exploit Transition Hunt

# Arkime Multi Protocol Remote Service Hunt for T1021 Remote Services
Technique Context
- Start broad and look for unusual interactive sessions across the common remote-service protocols used for administration and lateral movement.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 22 || port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 3389 || port.dst == 5900 || port.dst == 5985 || port.dst == 5986) && packets > <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS>

2. Pivot into preceding discovery or follow-on transfer sessions from the same source.

Escalation guidance: Escalate when rare WinRM traffic appears from new sources or is paired with staging and egress.
hunt_methods.title: T1021.006 WinRM Remote Management Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify remote-service use through cloud administration surfaces from hosts that do not normally manage cloud resources.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface unusual access to cloud admin surfaces that may support remote service abuse.
prerequisites: HTTP hostnames or DNS lookups for cloud admin traffic are retained in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because cloud admin-plane access is network-visible and meaningful when it appears from the wrong endpoints.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Cloud Admin Plane Access Hunt for T1021.007 Cloud Services
Technique Context
- Review access to cloud admin and shell surfaces from hosts that do not normally administer cloud resources.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (host.http == /(?i)(portal\.azure\.com|login\.microsoftonline\.com|console\.aws\.amazon\.com|signin\.aws\.amazon\.com|shell\.azure\.com|admin\.google\.com|console\.cloud\.google\.com)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(portal\.azure\.com|console\.aws\.amazon\.com|shell\.azure\.com|admin\.google\.com|console\.cloud\.google\.com)/)
What the search isolates: Sessions to cloud admin and shell surfaces that may support remote service abuse through cloud consoles.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, dns.host, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Separate known cloud admin workstations from unexpected endpoints.
2. Pivot into surrounding sessions for token abuse, cloud storage transfers, or remote support tooling.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source host normally should not access cloud admin portals or shells.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Approved cloud administration, break-glass access, or engineering operations.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when cloud admin-plane sessions originate from unusual endpoints or sit next to suspicious storage or tasking traffic.
hunt_methods.title: T1021.007 Cloud Admin Plane Access Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify internal large-object transfers that look more like lateral staging than routine file movement.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface suspicious internal file or tool transfer sessions that support lateral movement.
prerequisites: The relevant internal transfer paths are visible in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show the path, size, timing, and neighboring sessions for lateral transfer activity.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Lateral Tool Transfer Session Hunt for T1570 Lateral Tool Transfer
Technique Context
- Review internal transfer sessions that look like tooling, archives, installers, scripts, or operator staging between hosts rather than normal file sharing.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 22 || port.dst == 80 || port.dst == 443 || port.dst == 5985 || port.dst == 5986) && bytes >= <PAYLOAD_RETRIEVAL_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Large internal transfers over common admin and transfer paths that may represent lateral tooling, archive movement, or post-access staging.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, host.http, http.uri, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the source normally transfers large objects to the destination over the protocol in use.
2. Pivot into prior discovery and later remote-service sessions from the same source.
3. For HTTP-visible sessions, inspect URI and object naming for archives, installers, scripts, or temp paths.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the transfer is unusual for the source host, occurs after discovery, or is followed by remote-service use.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Software deployment, admin copy jobs, backup, package mirrors, and file distribution.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when unusual internal transfers are paired with remote-service access, immediate tasking, or outbound communications from the destination.
hunt_methods.title: T1570 Lateral Tool Transfer Session Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify remote access tool traffic that implies hands-on access through commercial remote support services.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface remote access tool infrastructure use that does not align to approved support workflows.
prerequisites: HTTP hostnames or DNS lookups for remote access tooling are retained in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show vendor-tool infrastructure use and tie it to the rest of the session story.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Remote Access Vendor Session Hunt for T1219 Remote Access Tools
Technique Context
- Review connections to remote support and remote control service infrastructure from hosts that do not normally use those tools.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (host.http == /(?i)(teamviewer|anydesk|screenconnect|connectwise|logmein|gotomypc|splashtop|bomgar|beyondtrust|rustdesk)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(teamviewer|anydesk|screenconnect|connectwise|logmein|gotomypc|splashtop|bomgar|beyondtrust|rustdesk)/)
What the search isolates: Sessions to remote-access-tool infrastructure that can represent hands-on-keyboard access through commercial services.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, dns.host, ja3, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Separate approved support tooling from novel or unmanaged use.
2. Pivot into preceding discovery or later transfer sessions from the same source.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the host, user segment, or time window does not fit approved support use.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Approved helpdesk or vendor remote support tools.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when remote access vendor traffic appears from unmanaged hosts or sits next to suspicious staging, discovery, or egress.
hunt_methods.title: T1219 Remote Access Vendor Session Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify persistent remote access tool sessions that can sustain hands-on-keyboard activity.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface unusually persistent remote access tool sessions that deserve operator review.
prerequisites: HTTP hostnames or DNS lookups for remote access tooling are retained in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can distinguish quick vendor check-ins from persistent interactive support channels by session volume and reuse.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Long Lived Support Channel Hunt for T1219 Remote Access Tools
Technique Context
- Focus on long-lived or repeated remote support sessions that are unusual for the host and may reflect persistent operator access.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && bytes >= <PROXY_SESSION_BYTES> && (host.http == /(?i)(teamviewer|anydesk|screenconnect|connectwise|logmein|gotomypc|splashtop|bomgar|beyondtrust|rustdesk)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(teamviewer|anydesk|screenconnect|connectwise|logmein|gotomypc|splashtop|bomgar|beyondtrust|rustdesk)/)
What the search isolates: Larger or more persistent remote support sessions that may indicate active remote control rather than a brief check-in.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, dns.host, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Compare the session length and volume to known helpdesk norms for the environment.
2. Pivot into surrounding transfer or admin sessions to see whether the remote support path is used to move tools or data.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source host is not part of expected support workflows and the session is unusually persistent.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Extended support cases or remote vendor troubleshooting.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when persistent remote access tool sessions appear on endpoints that should not require interactive support or that immediately stage tools or exfiltrate data.
hunt_methods.title: T1219 Long Lived Support Channel Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify application channels that look shaped to reduce visibility rather than to match normal service behavior.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface common protocols used in ways that conceal operator traffic through mismatched payload expectations, ports, and service context.
prerequisites: HTTP, DNS, or TLS metadata is retained for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can combine protocol, port, byte profile, destination reuse, and parser fields into a stronger obfuscation lead than keywords alone.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Opaque Channel Camouflage Hunt for T1001 Data Obfuscation
Technique Context
- Review common application protocols used with odd byte profiles, protocol-port drift, or repeated low-signal tasking channels that reduce visibility.
Role baseline: <EXPECTED_HOST_ROLE>
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && ((protocols == dns && bytes >= <DNS_INFLATED_BYTES>) || (protocols == http && packets <= <LOW_PACKET_THRESHOLD> && bytes >= <HTTP_INFLATED_BYTES>) || (protocols == tls && bytes <= <SMALL_BEACON_BYTES> && port.dst != 443) || ((protocols == http || protocols == http2) && port.dst != 80 && port.dst != 443 && port.dst != 8080 && port.dst != 8443))
What the search isolates: Common protocols used in byte or port patterns that deserve review for concealed tasking, junk/padding, or protocol impersonation.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, protocols, port.dst, bytes, packets, host.http, dns.host, http.uri, ja3, cert.subject.cn, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the byte profile, protocol-port pairing, destination, and host role fit normal service behavior.
2. Pivot into repeated use of the same destination, JA3, hostname family, or adjacent staging, exfiltration, and tasking sessions.
3. Inspect packet detail when the byte profile is stable, inflated, or inconsistent with the visible protocol metadata.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the channel is rare for the host role, recurs with stable infrastructure, and does not fit normal service use.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Proxies, API gateways, EDR telemetry, software updates, SaaS APIs, and bespoke enterprise applications.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when the opaque-looking channel is rare for the host role, repeatedly reused, and sits next to suspicious tasking or transfer behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1001 Opaque Channel Camouflage Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify rare repeated application channels that mismatch the source host's expected role.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface rare repeated application channels that become suspicious because they do not fit the host role.
prerequisites: Protocol parser fields are retained and the analyst understands the expected role of the source host.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can combine rarity, repetition, and role mismatch into a stronger camouflage lead than bytes alone.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Rarity And Role Mismatch Hunt for T1001 Data Obfuscation
Technique Context
- Treat obfuscation as a behavior problem: the strongest leads are channels that are rare for the host role, repeatedly reused, and operationally adjacent to tasking or transfer behavior.
Role baseline: <EXPECTED_HOST_ROLE>
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((protocols == dns || protocols == http || protocols == tls) && bytes <= <SMALL_BEACON_BYTES>)
What the search isolates: Small repeated application channels that only become meaningful once rarity, destination reuse, and host-role mismatch are considered together.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, host.http, dns.host, ja3, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by destination family and review whether the same source keeps returning in a stable cadence.
2. Compare the channel against the expected host role and pivot into neighboring staging, exfiltration, or fallback-channel behavior.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the destination is rare for the host and the same low-signal channel recurs instead of appearing once.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Telemetry beacons, SaaS APIs, cloud services, update checks, and management agents.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a rare low-signal channel is repeatedly reused by a host that should not need it and it sits next to operator-like follow-on activity.
hunt_methods.title: T1001 Rarity And Role Mismatch Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify DNS sessions carrying unexpectedly large payloads consistent with padding or junk data.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface DNS channels that look padded or inflated compared to normal DNS exchanges.
prerequisites: DNS parser data is available for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can approximate DNS padding behavior through low-packet inflated exchanges and repeated infrastructure reuse.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime DNS Inflated Payload Padding Hunt for T1001.001 Junk Data
Technique Context
- DNS normally stays extremely small. Low-packet DNS sessions that still carry inflated byte volume are more interesting than the same byte count over HTTP.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && protocols == dns && packets <= 3 && bytes >= <DNS_INFLATED_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Low-packet DNS sessions with inflated payload volume that can reflect junk data or padding added to hinder inspection.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, dns.host, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Review destinations where very small DNS exchanges still carry inflated byte counts.
2. Check entropy and structure in queried names, then pivot into later sessions to the same infrastructure to see whether the channel supports T1071.004, T1041, or T1048-style behavior.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the same source reuses the domain or destination family and the byte pattern persists over time.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- CDN-backed APIs, security/EDR telemetry, software updates, and enterprise software with generated domains.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when inflated DNS exchanges recur to rare infrastructure, show structured or high-entropy labels, and sit next to suspicious tasking or exfiltration behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1001.001 DNS Inflated Payload Padding Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify HTTP sessions carrying unexpectedly large payloads consistent with padding or junk data.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface HTTP channels that look padded or inflated relative to the apparent request/response pattern.
prerequisites: HTTP parser data is available for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can approximate HTTP junk-data behavior through low-packet inflated exchanges plus repetition and role mismatch.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime HTTP Inflated Low-Packet Hunt for T1001.001 Junk Data
Technique Context
- HTTP can legitimately be larger than DNS, so the hunt should focus on low-packet HTTP sessions whose byte volume is still inflated relative to the apparent interaction.
Role baseline: <EXPECTED_HOST_ROLE>
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && protocols == http && packets <= <LOW_PACKET_THRESHOLD> && bytes >= <HTTP_INFLATED_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Low-packet HTTP sessions with inflated payload volume that may reflect padding or junk data instead of a normal application exchange.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the destination, path family, and payload size fit the host role and expected application behavior.
2. Inspect structure in URIs or parameters, then pivot into later sessions to the same destination to see whether the channel becomes T1041, T1048, or T1071.001 behavior.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the same source reuses the destination and the low-packet/high-byte pattern is persistent instead of one-off.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- REST APIs, cloud services, EDR telemetry, software update checks, CDN-backed APIs, and normal application tokens or structured parameters.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when inflated low-packet HTTP sessions recur to rare infrastructure, do not fit the host role, and sit next to suspicious tasking or transfer behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1001.001 HTTP Inflated Low-Packet Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify repeated low-packet sessions with stable inflated payload profiles.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface repeated low-packet padded channels whose stability makes them more suspicious than one isolated session.
prerequisites: HTTP and DNS parser data are available and the hunt window is wide enough to compare repeated sessions.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because repetition and stable byte-to-packet ratios make padding behavior far more convincing than a one-off inflated session.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Stable Byte-Packet Ratio Hunt for T1001.001 Junk Data
Technique Context
- One inflated session is ambiguous. The stronger signal is when the same host keeps sending low-packet sessions with a similar byte profile to the same destination family.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((protocols == dns && packets <= 3 && bytes >= <DNS_INFLATED_BYTES>) || (protocols == http && packets <= <LOW_PACKET_THRESHOLD> && bytes >= <HTTP_INFLATED_BYTES>))
What the search isolates: Candidate padded channels whose value comes from repetition, stable ratios, and destination reuse rather than one noisy session.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, protocols, dns.host, host.http, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and destination family to find repeated low-packet sessions with similar byte ranges.
2. Compare the repetition pattern against host role and pivot into adjacent staging, tasking, or transfer behavior before escalating.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the same destination family is reused and the byte-to-packet relationship is stable across several sessions.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Repetitive APIs, telemetry bursts, software updates, and structured application calls.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when the repeated inflated pattern is rare for the host, structurally consistent, and sits next to T1071.*, T1041, or T1048 follow-on behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1001.001 Stable Byte-Packet Ratio Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify image or media transfers that do not fit the host and may conceal payload or data movement.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface suspicious media-object transfer patterns that can conceal payloads or data movement.
prerequisites: HTTP parser fields are retained for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show unusual media-object traffic and the surrounding behavior that makes it suspicious.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Media Object Camouflage Hunt for T1001.002 Steganography
Technique Context
- Review image and media object transfers to rare destinations when the host does not normally exchange those objects at scale.
Role baseline: <EXPECTED_HOST_ROLE>
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && http.uri == /(?i)\.(png|jpg|jpeg|gif|bmp|svg|ico|webp|mp3|mp4)$/ && bytes >= <MID_TRANSFER_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Media-object sessions that can support hidden payloads, data transfer, or staged tasking.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, bytes, packets, http.user-agent, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the destination, object type, and transfer size are normal for the host role.
2. Pivot into surrounding sessions for repeated media object fetches or uploads to the same infrastructure.
Validation steps:
- Confirm unusual object types or repetitive transfers to rare destinations.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- CDN content, media browsing, software branding assets, or application downloads.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when repeated media-object sessions to rare destinations line up with tasking, staging, or egress behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1001.002 Media Object Camouflage Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify repeated media or blob traffic that looks operational rather than normal content delivery.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface repeated media/blob traffic whose role mismatch or destination reuse makes it more suspicious than ordinary content delivery.
prerequisites: HTTP parser fields are retained for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can tie media/blob object movement to repeated destination use and role mismatch rather than one large file alone.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Blob Retrieval Upload Mismatch Hunt for T1001.002 Steganography
Technique Context
- A stronger lead appears when media or blob objects are repeatedly retrieved or uploaded in ways that do not fit the host role or the destination's normal service pattern.
Role baseline: <EXPECTED_HOST_ROLE>
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((http.uri == /(?i)\.(png|jpg|jpeg|gif|bmp|svg|ico|webp|mp3|mp4)$/) || (host.http == /(?i)(cdn|img|media|static|content)/)) && bytes >= <MID_TRANSFER_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Blob-heavy media transactions where destination reuse, role mismatch, or repeated object movement make the traffic stand out.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, bytes, packets, http.user-agent, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the same destination family repeatedly carries large media or blob traffic for a host that should not rely on it.
2. Pivot into adjacent tasking, archive movement, or exfiltration sessions to see whether the object traffic is operationally meaningful.
Validation steps:
- Confirm media/blob transfers recur or the host-role mismatch is strong enough to make the channel suspicious.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Content delivery, image-heavy applications, normal uploads, or software asset retrieval.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when repeated media/blob movement to rare infrastructure does not fit the host role and sits next to other suspicious communications.
hunt_methods.title: T1001.002 Blob Retrieval Upload Mismatch Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify channels that mimic expected services while using protocol behavior or ports that do not fit.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface channels that appear to impersonate expected services or protocols.
prerequisites: Protocol parser fields are retained for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show protocol-port mismatches and repeated misuse across the same infrastructure.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Protocol Impersonation Hunt for T1001.003 Protocol or Service Impersonation
Technique Context
- Look for protocols appearing on ports or destinations that do not fit the service they claim to be.
Role baseline: <EXPECTED_HOST_ROLE>
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((protocols == http && port.dst != 80 && port.dst != 443 && port.dst != 8080 && port.dst != 8443) || (protocols == tls && port.dst != 443 && port.dst != 8443) || (protocols == dns && port.dst != 53))
What the search isolates: Sessions where the observed protocol does not align well with the service port or service context.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, protocols, port.dst, host.http, dns.host, cert.subject.cn, ja3, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the port and protocol mismatch is expected for the application, destination, and host role.
2. Pivot into repeated use of the same mismatch to see whether it behaves like a stable operator channel.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the mismatch is unusual for the environment and not just a custom internal service.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Nonstandard internal apps, proxies, load balancers, and application gateways.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when protocol-port mismatches recur to rare infrastructure and sit next to suspicious tasking, staging, or exfiltration activity.
hunt_methods.title: T1001.003 Protocol Impersonation Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify stable destination reuse of protocol-service mismatches that do not fit the source host or destination role.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface repeated service-impersonating traffic where the destination family and host role do not fit.
prerequisites: Protocol parser fields are retained and the analyst understands the expected host role in scope.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because repeated destination reuse and role mismatch make protocol impersonation far more credible than one odd port choice.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Destination Role Mismatch Hunt for T1001.003 Protocol or Service Impersonation
Technique Context
- The mismatch gets stronger when one host repeatedly uses a protocol-service pairing that does not fit either the destination class or the source host role.
Role baseline: <EXPECTED_HOST_ROLE>
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((protocols == http && port.dst != 80 && port.dst != 443 && port.dst != 8080 && port.dst != 8443) || (protocols == tls && port.dst != 443 && port.dst != 8443) || (protocols == dns && port.dst != 53))
What the search isolates: Stable destination reuse of service-impersonating traffic where the destination family, role expectation, or surrounding behavior do not fit.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, protocols, port.dst, host.http, dns.host, cert.subject.cn, ja3, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by destination family and review whether the same mismatch recurs to the same infrastructure.
2. Compare the channel against host role and pivot into surrounding tasking, tunneling, or transfer behavior before escalating.
Validation steps:
- Confirm repeated destination reuse or a strong source/destination role mismatch rather than one odd session.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Bespoke enterprise apps, alternate listener configs, or proxies.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a repeated protocol-service mismatch does not fit the host role or destination class and sits next to other operator-like activity.
hunt_methods.title: T1001.003 Destination Role Mismatch Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify repeated small application-layer channels that resemble operator tasking rather than normal service traffic.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface repeated low-volume application-layer channels that may carry C2 tasking.
prerequisites: Protocol parser fields are retained for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show application-layer channel reuse, parser metadata, byte profile, and adjacent payload or exfil behavior.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Application Layer Tasking Hunt for T1071 Application Layer Protocol
Technique Context
- Review repeated low-volume application-layer channels that could carry command and control tasking over allowed protocols.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && ((protocols == http || protocols == http2 || protocols == tls || protocols == dns || protocols == ftp) || (port.dst == 25 || port.dst == 465 || port.dst == 587 || port.dst == 993 || port.dst == 995)) && bytes <= <SMALL_BEACON_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Low-volume application-layer sessions that become meaningful when grouped by repeated source, destination, protocol, JA3, and timing.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, host.http, dns.host, http.uri, http.user-agent, ja3, cert.subject.cn, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and destination to identify repeated tasking-like channels rather than one-off browsing.
2. Compare destination rarity, JA3 reuse, URI structure, DNS labels, user-agent, and timing cadence against the host role.
3. Pivot into adjacent payload retrieval, remote service, or exfiltration sessions from the same host.
Validation steps:
- Confirm repeated low-volume sessions to rare infrastructure or unusual application services for the source host.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- SaaS APIs, cloud agents, EDR telemetry, software update checks, and monitoring agents.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a rare application-layer channel recurs with stable infrastructure and is adjacent to staging, discovery, or data movement.
hunt_methods.title: T1071 Application Layer Tasking Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify repeated small web-protocol channels consistent with C2 tasking.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface web-protocol beacon and tasking candidates that become meaningful through repetition, rarity, and surrounding sessions.
prerequisites: HTTP and TLS parser fields are available for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime is excellent at showing repeated low-volume web sessions, reuse, and TLS artifacts.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Web Beacon Tasking Hunt for T1071.001 Web Protocols
Technique Context
- Review repeated low-volume HTTP, HTTP/2, or TLS sessions to rare web destinations that may carry tasking or beacon responses.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (protocols == http || protocols == http2 || protocols == tls) && bytes <= <SMALL_BEACON_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Web-protocol sessions that are small enough to be tasking-like and should be grouped by destination, JA3, URI family, and timing.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, dns.host, http.uri, http.method, http.user-agent, ja3, cert.subject.cn, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and destination to find repeated small sessions instead of normal browsing bursts.
2. Review hostnames, URI structure, user-agent stability, JA3, certificate values, and timing cadence.
3. Pivot into larger transfers, new remote services, or alternate C2 channels from the same source.
Validation steps:
- Confirm repeated low-volume web sessions to rare infrastructure or suspicious URI patterns for the source host.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Cloud APIs, telemetry agents, software update checks, browser sync, and normal SaaS polling.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when the same host repeatedly contacts rare web infrastructure with stable metadata and adjacent staging or exfiltration behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1071.001 Web Beacon Tasking Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify web C2 channels that also move objects, payloads, or staged data.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface rare web-object and upload style traffic that may be part of web-protocol C2.
prerequisites: HTTP parser fields are available for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because it expands beyond simple beacon cadence into the web object and path patterns many operators actually abuse.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Rare Web Object Transfer Hunt for T1071.001 Web Protocols
Technique Context
- Review web-protocol sessions where tasking infrastructure also handles object transfer, upload paths, or blob-like API traffic.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_EXTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (protocols == http || protocols == http2 || protocols == tls) && ((http.method == POST || http.method == PUT) || http.uri == /(?i)(api|graphql|upload|submit|content|cdn|static|obj|blob|media|sync|checkin)/ || bytes >= <MID_TRANSFER_BYTES>)
What the search isolates: Web sessions that may combine C2 tasking with object upload, download, or staging behavior.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, dns.host, http.uri, http.method, http.user-agent, ja3, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether POST/PUT, blob-like URI paths, or larger web transfers are normal for the source host.
2. Pivot backward to small tasking sessions and forward to larger transfer or alternate-channel activity.
3. Compare the destination against common SaaS/cloud baselines and known application workflows.
Validation steps:
- Confirm rare destination reuse, unexpected method/path behavior, or object transfer adjacent to small tasking sessions.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- SaaS APIs, cloud sync tools, browsers, patching, and enterprise application uploads.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when rare web-object traffic is tied to stable tasking metadata or is followed by additional staging or exfiltration sessions.
hunt_methods.title: T1071.001 Rare Web Object Transfer Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify unusual FTP control and data sessions tied to staging, tool transfer, or egress.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface rare FTP paths that may support operator control, transfer, or exfiltration.
prerequisites: FTP traffic is visible in Arkime for the scoped segment.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because FTP stands out clearly in session metadata and is often meaningful when it appears from the wrong systems.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime FTP Control And Data Channel Hunt for T1071.002 File Transfer Protocols
Technique Context
- Review FTP sessions where control and data traffic appear from hosts that do not normally exchange files that way.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 20 || port.dst == 21 || protocols == ftp)
What the search isolates: FTP sessions that can support tasking, payload retrieval, or data movement outside normal enterprise patterns.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Separate expected legacy integrations from rare or first-time FTP paths.
2. Pivot into neighboring sessions for staging, remote service use, or large follow-on transfers.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source does not normally use FTP to the destination and that data volume is meaningful.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Legacy integrations, batch file exchange, or partner transfers.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when rare FTP paths appear from unexpected hosts or lead directly into staging, remote access, or egress.
hunt_methods.title: T1071.002 FTP Control And Data Channel Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify unusual mail-protocol sessions that may support tasking, delivery, or data movement.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface direct mail-protocol use from hosts that do not normally speak SMTP, IMAP, or POP externally.
prerequisites: Mail protocol traffic is visible in Arkime for the scoped segment.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because direct mail-protocol use from non-mail systems stands out well in Arkime session data.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Mail Relay Tasking Hunt for T1071.003 Mail Protocols
Technique Context
- Review SMTP, IMAP, or POP sessions to rare external relays from hosts that should not use mail protocols directly.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 25 || port.dst == 465 || port.dst == 587 || port.dst == 993 || port.dst == 995)
What the search isolates: Mail-protocol sessions that may support operator communications, staging, or alternate delivery channels.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Separate expected mail servers and appliances from endpoints or servers that should not use mail protocols directly.
2. Pivot into adjacent DNS, web, and transfer sessions to understand the wider infrastructure pattern.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source role and destination make the mail path unusual.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Mail servers, secure gateways, printers, scanners, and enterprise apps.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when direct mail-protocol use appears from non-mail hosts and is paired with suspicious transfer or staging behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1071.003 Mail Relay Tasking Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify DNS patterns that stand out from normal resolution and support controlled operator communications.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface DNS behavior that could reflect tasking, lookup-driven rendezvous, or data movement over DNS.
prerequisites: DNS parser fields are indexed for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can pair suspicious DNS structure with the later sessions that matter operationally.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime DNS Payload Beacon Hunt for T1071.004 DNS
Technique Context
- DNS tasking becomes more interesting when the same source repeatedly sends oversized or highly structured queries to a stable destination family instead of performing ordinary scattered resolution.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && protocols == dns && ((bytes >= <DNS_INFLATED_BYTES>) || (dns.host == /(?i)([a-z0-9-]{12,}\\.){2,}/))
What the search isolates: DNS sessions whose payload size or label structure suggests operational content, especially when the pattern repeats to the same resolver or infrastructure family.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, dns.host, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and destination family to find repeated oversized or deeply structured DNS sessions instead of one isolated lookup.
2. Review label structure, query repetition, and later sessions to the same infrastructure before deciding the traffic is tasking.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the same host reuses the same domain family or resolver path and the oversized or structured pattern persists over time.
- Pivot into later HTTP, TLS, tunneling, or transfer behavior that reuses the same infrastructure.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- CDN-backed applications, EDR telemetry, cloud services, and enterprise software that generates long labels or larger DNS responses.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when repeated oversized or structured DNS sessions are rare for the host, reuse the same infrastructure, and bridge into later control or transfer behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1071.004 DNS Payload Beacon Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify DNS lookups that act as rendezvous points for later operator communications.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface suspicious DNS lookups that clearly bridge into later communications.
prerequisites: DNS parser fields are indexed and surrounding sessions are retained in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime excels when DNS is treated as the pivot into the next sessions, not as an isolated event.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime DNS To Session Chain Hunt for T1071.004 DNS
Technique Context
- Treat DNS as a rendezvous layer only when the lookup is followed by consistent later communication to the resolved infrastructure, not as a standalone suspicious event.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && protocols == dns && ((bytes >= <DNS_INFLATED_BYTES>) || (dns.host == /(?i)([a-z0-9-]{12,}\\.){2,}/))
What the search isolates: Suspicious DNS sessions that are strong mainly because they become the pivot point into later HTTP, TLS, FTP, or alternate-protocol communications.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, dns.host, ip.dst, communityId, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Start with repeated structured or oversized DNS traffic and pivot into the next sessions from the same source within the same window.
2. Check whether the same destination or infrastructure family is reused for web, TLS, tunneling, or data transfer instead of one-off resolution.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the DNS activity consistently precedes later communications to the same infrastructure family.
- Separate ordinary SaaS resolution from cases where DNS is a stable precursor to tasking, staging, or egress.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Normal application resolution before SaaS, CDN, or API use.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when structured or oversized DNS lookups repeatedly bridge into the same follow-on infrastructure and the resulting traffic looks operational rather than user-driven.
hunt_methods.title: T1071.004 DNS To Session Chain Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify proxy or relay use that masks later tasking, staging, or egress.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface proxy-like relay use that can hide later operator traffic while reducing pure alt-port noise.
prerequisites: Session metadata and HTTP or TLS fields are retained for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can isolate proxy ports, HTTP CONNECT behavior, destination reuse, and the later traffic the relay fronts.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Proxy Relay Hunt for T1090 Proxy
Technique Context
- Review proxy-like relay paths that can hide later operator traffic, especially HTTP CONNECT and classic proxy ports used by hosts that do not normally proxy.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (((port.dst == 1080 || port.dst == 3128 || port.dst == 8080) && bytes >= <PROXY_SESSION_BYTES>) || (protocols == http && http.method == CONNECT))
What the search isolates: Candidate proxy or relay sessions that can support hidden tasking, egress, or staging.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, host.http, dns.host, http.method, ja3, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the destination is an approved proxy, web gateway, remote support relay, or lab service.
2. Pivot into later sessions from the same source to see whether the proxy path fronts additional suspicious tasking, staging, or exfiltration.
3. Group by destination and source role to find endpoints that should not originate proxy-style traffic.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source does not normally use the relay and the path is not an expected egress route.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Corporate proxies, web gateways, dev relays, lab proxies, and sanctioned privacy gateways.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a rare relay path is used by endpoints that should not proxy traffic and it fronts later tasking or egress.
hunt_methods.title: T1090 Proxy Relay Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify relay use that precedes or fronts later suspicious external communications.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface candidate relay entry points that can be expanded into chained operator traffic.
prerequisites: Session metadata is retained for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show one host traversing multiple relay-like destinations across the same time window.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Relay Chaining Hunt for T1090 Proxy
Technique Context
- Look for a source using one relay-like destination and then quickly shifting to additional external services or repeated destinations through the same operational window.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && ((port.dst == 1080 || port.dst == 3128 || port.dst == 8080 || port.dst == 8443) || (protocols == http && http.method == CONNECT))
What the search isolates: Candidate relay entry sessions that should be expanded into surrounding traffic to determine whether the host is chaining communications.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, host.http, dns.host, http.method, ja3, cert.subject.cn, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and relay-like destination to identify repeated relay use.
2. Pivot to the same source across adjacent minutes or hours and review later external destinations, JA3 reuse, and object transfer.
3. Confirm whether the relay path is an approved enterprise proxy or a rare path introduced during suspicious activity.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the relay is uncommon for the source host and appears before or during tasking, staging, or egress behavior.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Enterprise proxies, developer tunnels, test relays, and cloud gateways.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when relay-like traffic is new for the source and temporally fronts additional suspicious external sessions.
hunt_methods.title: T1090 Relay Chaining Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify proxy or relay paths that obscure the true destination or source of later communications.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface internal proxy transit paths that can relay traffic through compromised or unauthorized internal systems.
prerequisites: The relevant east-west relay paths are visible in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can isolate proxy-like paths through ports, HTTP CONNECT metadata, destination reuse, and surrounding-session pivots.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime T1090.001 Internal Proxy Transit Hunt for T1090.001
Technique Context
- Review relay-like paths that match the sub-technique and then expand into surrounding sessions to confirm whether traffic is being intermediated.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && ((port.dst == 1080 || port.dst == 3128 || port.dst == 8080) || (protocols == http && http.method == CONNECT))
What the search isolates: Candidate proxy paths where port choice, HTTP CONNECT behavior, destination reuse, or host-role mismatch suggests relay use.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, host.http, dns.host, http.method, ja3, cert.subject.cn, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Determine whether the proxy-like path is approved for the source host and network segment.
2. Pivot into adjacent sessions from the same source and destination to identify follow-on tasking, staging, or egress.
3. Group by source, destination, JA3, and hostnames to find repeated relay behavior or multiple affected hosts.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the path is rare or newly introduced and is adjacent to suspicious operator activity.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Enterprise proxies, web gateways, developer tunnels, test relays, and remote support infrastructure.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when rare proxy-like traffic is used by endpoints that should not relay traffic or when it fronts suspicious follow-on sessions.
hunt_methods.title: T1090.001 Internal Proxy Transit Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify proxy or relay paths that obscure the true destination or source of later communications.

Technique Context
- Review relay-like paths that match the sub-technique and then expand into surrounding sessions to confirm whether traffic is being intermediated.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_EXTERNAL_SCOPE>) && ((port.dst == 1080 || port.dst == 3128 || port.dst == 8080 || port.dst == 8443) || (protocols == http && http.method == CONNECT))
What the search isolates: Candidate proxy paths where port choice, HTTP CONNECT behavior, destination reuse, or host-role mismatch suggests relay use.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, host.http, dns.host, http.method, ja3, cert.subject.cn, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Determine whether the proxy-like path is approved for the source host and network segment.
2. Pivot into adjacent sessions from the same source and destination to identify follow-on tasking, staging, or egress.

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify proxy or relay paths that obscure the true destination or source of later communications.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface proxy-like entry points that may participate in multi-hop traffic chains.

# Arkime T1090.003 Multi Hop Proxy Path Hunt for T1090.003
Technique Context
- Review relay-like paths that match the sub-technique and then expand into surrounding sessions to confirm whether traffic is being intermediated.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && ((port.dst == 1080 || port.dst == 3128 || port.dst == 8080 || port.dst == 8443) || (protocols == http && http.method == CONNECT))
What the search isolates: Candidate proxy paths where port choice, HTTP CONNECT behavior, destination reuse, or host-role mismatch suggests relay use.
