hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify ICMP, GRE, or unusual UDP channels that can carry operator traffic.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface unusual non-application-layer transport channels that deserve operator review.
prerequisites: The relevant transport-layer traffic is visible in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because non-application-layer transport stands out in session metadata when endpoints use it unexpectedly.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Non Application Transport Hunt for T1095 Non-Application Layer Protocol
Technique Context
- Review ICMP, GRE, or unusual UDP-based channels that are not normal for the host and can transport tasking or data.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (protocols == icmp || protocols == gre || port.dst == 500 || port.dst == 4500)
What the search isolates: Non-application-layer transport channels that can conceal tasking or data movement.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the protocol is expected for the host role and path.
2. Pivot into neighboring sessions to see whether the same host also uses proxy, DNS, or web tasking channels.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the host rarely or never uses the protocol in normal operations.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- VPNs, tunnel interfaces, monitoring, or network infrastructure protocols.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when unusual transport-layer channels appear from endpoints and sit next to suspicious staging or egress behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1095 Non Application Transport Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify hosts pulling probable tooling or payloads from infrastructure that does not fit normal software distribution patterns.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface suspicious inbound retrieval of tools or archives that can support an intrusion workflow.
prerequisites: Arkime is capturing HTTP metadata or file-transfer protocol sessions for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can directly show tool retrieval, the hosting infrastructure, and the sessions that follow it.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Staged Payload Retrieval Hunt for T1105 Ingress Tool Transfer
Technique Context
- Isolate suspicious download paths, file-bearing sessions, or protocol changes that introduce tooling after initial access.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((http.uri == /(?i)\.(zip|rar|7z|iso|img|dll|exe|msi|ps1|vbs|js|hta)$/) || protocols == ftp || (http.method == GET && bytes >= <PAYLOAD_RETRIEVAL_BYTES>))
What the search isolates: Sessions that look like a host is retrieving tools, archives, or scripts that support follow-on execution or lateral movement.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, http.method, protocols, bytes, packets
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the host normally fetches tooling from the destination.
2. Pivot into earlier exploit or phishing sessions and later remote-access or beaconing traffic.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the host and destination pair does not fit normal software distribution.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Software deployment, patching, content distribution, and sanctioned admin downloads.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when suspicious retrieval is paired with exploit traffic, unusual destinations, or rapid follow-on admin or beaconing sessions.
hunt_methods.title: T1105 Staged Payload Retrieval Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify tool retrieval that rapidly turns into post-download operator activity.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface downloads that are quickly followed by suspicious network use from the same endpoint.
prerequisites: Arkime is capturing the relevant retrieval traffic and later sessions from the same host.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because it uses Arkime's biggest strength here: tying one suspicious retrieval to the sessions that immediately follow it.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Follow On Tool Staging Hunt for T1105 Ingress Tool Transfer
Technique Context
- Treat suspicious downloads as the first step and then review whether the same host immediately reaches new destinations or admin protocols.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && bytes >= <PAYLOAD_RETRIEVAL_BYTES> && (protocols == http || protocols == tls || protocols == ftp)
What the search isolates: Large inbound retrieval sessions that can be pivoted into immediate post-download staging and use.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, protocols, host.http, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Start with the large retrieval and pivot into the next sessions from the same host.
2. Look for a move into remote service use, cloud storage, relay traffic, or beaconing after the download.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the retrieval is followed by a meaningful change in the host's network behavior.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Large installer downloads or normal update activity.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a suspicious download is immediately followed by new tasking, staging, or admin-path traffic.
hunt_methods.title: T1105 Follow On Tool Staging Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify very small trigger-like sessions that precede a change in network behavior.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface tiny high-port signaling candidates that can trigger later access or C2 behavior.
prerequisites: Arkime session metadata is retained for the scoped time window.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt when used as a sequence pivot: Arkime can show tiny trigger sessions and the later session transition they precede.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Low Volume Signaling Hunt for T1205 Traffic Signaling
Technique Context
- Review tiny sessions to uncommon or high ports that can act as trigger signals before a service opens, changes behavior, or initiates a larger session.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && bytes <= <TINY_SIGNAL_BYTES> && packets <= <LOW_PACKET_THRESHOLD> && port.dst >= 1024
What the search isolates: Very small high-port sessions that only become meaningful when followed by a service transition, callback, or sustained connection.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by destination and review whether tiny probes recur in a pattern rather than random internet noise.
2. Pivot forward from the same source and destination to identify newly opened sessions, protocol changes, or sustained traffic.
3. Compare the destination port set against known applications and approved health checks.
Validation steps:
- Confirm a repeated tiny signal is temporally linked to a later session transition or callback.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Health checks, load balancers, monitoring probes, scanners, and NAT/firewall keepalives.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when tiny high-port signals are rare, patterned, and immediately precede new tasking or access sessions.
hunt_methods.title: T1205 Low Volume Signaling Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify knock-style signaling before a new service or channel becomes active.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface knock-like sequences that precede a newly reachable service or channel.
prerequisites: Arkime session metadata is retained for the scoped time window.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show the small pre-access sessions and the service that appears afterward.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Port Knock Pattern Hunt for T1205.001 Port Knocking
Technique Context
- Review repeated tiny sessions to high or unusual ports that may be used to signal a service to open.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && bytes <= <TINY_SIGNAL_BYTES> && packets <= <LOW_PACKET_THRESHOLD> && port.dst >= 1024
What the search isolates: Tiny high-port sessions that can be grouped by source and destination to reveal knock-like patterns.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by ip.src and ip.dst to find clusters of tiny high-port connections in a short period.
2. Pivot into later sessions to the same destination to see whether a new service becomes reachable.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the same source touches several unusual ports before a meaningful session appears.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Scanning, transient health checks, or misconfigured clients.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when tiny high-port sequences are followed by new remote access or transfer sessions to the same destination.
hunt_methods.title: T1205.001 Port Knock Pattern Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify DNS resolution patterns that support resilient or rotating infrastructure.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface dynamic-resolution candidates based on generated domain structure, dynamic DNS providers, and follow-on infrastructure churn.
prerequisites: DNS parser fields are retained in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can pivot from DNS into the follow-on destination set that matters operationally.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Dynamic Resolution Drift Hunt for T1568 Dynamic Resolution
Technique Context
- Review DNS activity where domain structure or provider choice suggests dynamic resolution rather than stable enterprise destinations.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && protocols == dns && (dns.host == /(?i)([a-z0-9-]{12,}\.){2,}/ || dns.host == /(?i)(dyndns|duckdns|no-ip|hopto|zapto|servehttp|serveftp|ddns|dynamicdns)/)
What the search isolates: DNS queries that may represent dynamic infrastructure, high-entropy generated names, or dynamic DNS provider use.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, dns.host, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and domain family to identify repeated dynamic-resolution behavior.
2. Pivot from DNS to follow-on sessions to resolved infrastructure and review destination churn.
3. Separate approved dynamic DNS or SaaS infrastructure from rare host-specific resolution.
Validation steps:
- Confirm repeated use by the same host and follow-on connections to changing or rare infrastructure.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Dynamic DNS for labs, home networks, vendor appliances, CDN names, and SaaS-generated hostnames.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a host repeatedly resolves dynamic-looking names and then connects to rare infrastructure for tasking or transfer.
hunt_methods.title: T1568 Dynamic Resolution Drift Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify DNS-backed infrastructure rotation that supports resilient C2.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface DNS domain families that can be pivoted into fast-flux-like answer and destination churn.
prerequisites: DNS lookups and later sessions are retained in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can approximate fast-flux behavior by pivoting from DNS into changing downstream sessions.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Fast Flux Resolution Review for T1568.001 Fast Flux DNS
Technique Context
- Use DNS sessions as the entry point, then group by queried domain to review answer-set expansion, short-lived infrastructure, and many follow-on destination IPs.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && protocols == dns && dns.host == /(?i)([a-z0-9-]{8,}\.){1,}/
What the search isolates: DNS activity that should be grouped by domain family to identify fast-flux-like answer churn and many resolved endpoints.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, dns.host, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by dns.host and source to find domain families repeatedly queried by the same host.
2. Pivot to follow-on connections and review whether one domain fans out to many destination IPs or ASNs in a short window.
3. Compare against CDN and SaaS baselines before escalating.
Validation steps:
- Confirm rapid answer or destination churn for one domain family and follow-on connections to many rare endpoints.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- CDNs, load balancers, cloud APIs, and SaaS platforms.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a non-CDN domain shows fast destination rotation and the source follows those answers with tasking-like or transfer sessions.
hunt_methods.title: T1568.001 Fast Flux Resolution Review

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify algorithmic-looking domain lookups that can act as rendezvous points for malware infrastructure.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface candidate DGA activity where domain churn and structure justify follow-on review.

hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can connect algorithmic-looking domains to the sessions that make them operationally meaningful.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime DGA Candidate Hunt for T1568.002 Domain Generation Algorithms
Technique Context
- Focus on DNS labels and domain churn patterns that are more consistent with algorithmic generation than with human-selected infrastructure.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && protocols == dns && dns.host == /(?i)([a-z0-9]{12,}\\.){2,}/
What the search isolates: DNS sessions where the hostnames look algorithmic enough to justify deeper review for domain churn and follow-on communication.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, dns.host, ip.dst, firstSeen, lastSeen, bytes, packets
Session-review workflow:
1. Start with ip.src to find hosts querying many similarly shaped domains.
2. Pivot into later web or TLS sessions to the queried infrastructure.
Validation steps:
- Confirm domain churn or repeated failed lookups across the same host.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Randomized telemetry, CDN sharding, or disposable cloud infrastructure.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a host queries many algorithmic-looking domains and one or more turn into active communications.
hunt_methods.title: T1568.002 DGA Candidate Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify common protocols running on non-standard ports as potential covert channels.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface common protocols shifted to unusual ports that may support evasion or covert transport.
prerequisites: Protocol parser fields are retained for the scoped traffic.

hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Non Standard Port Channel Hunt for T1571 Non-Standard Port
Technique Context
- Review common protocols operating on ports that are unusual for the service and the destination.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((protocols == http && port.dst != 80 && port.dst != 443 && port.dst != 8080 && port.dst != 8443) || (protocols == tls && port.dst != 443 && port.dst != 8443) || (protocols == dns && port.dst != 53))
What the search isolates: Common protocols shifted onto unusual ports where they can evade simple controls or blend with other traffic.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, protocols, port.dst, host.http, dns.host, ja3, bytes, packets
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the protocol-port pairing is expected for the destination and host role.
2. Pivot into repeated use of the same pairing to see whether it forms a stable channel.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the port choice is not explained by an approved application or service.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Custom enterprise apps, proxies, or alternate listener configurations.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a rare protocol-port pairing recurs to unusual infrastructure and is tied to tasking or data movement.
hunt_methods.title: T1571 Non Standard Port Channel Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify protocol misuse where normal DNS, HTTP, or TLS semantics are bent into covert transport.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface channels that may be repurposed to carry tunneled traffic or hidden operator communications.
prerequisites: Arkime is retaining DNS, HTTP, and TLS parser fields for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can reveal protocol misuse, byte asymmetry, and the follow-on traffic that makes a tunnel suspicious.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Protocol Tunnel Candidate Hunt for T1572 Protocol Tunneling
Technique Context
- Isolate protocol misuse, long-lived sessions, or unexpected data volume inside channels that normally carry modest traffic.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((protocols == dns && bytes >= <DNS_TUNNEL_BYTES>) || (http.uri == /(?i)(connect|tunnel|websocket|socket\\.io)/) || (port.dst == 53 && protocols == tls))
What the search isolates: DNS, HTTP, or TLS sessions that look like they are carrying tunneled traffic or protocol behavior outside normal service expectations.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, protocols, port.dst, dns.host, host.http, http.uri, bytes, packets, ja3
Session-review workflow:
1. Review candidate sessions for long duration, repeated reuse, or unexpected data volume relative to the protocol in use.
2. Check whether the same infrastructure is reused with similar byte and packet patterns over time instead of one odd session.
3. Pivot into surrounding sessions from the same host to see whether the tunnel sits next to staging, remote access, or exfiltration.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the session carries more data or more persistence than the protocol usually should.
- Confirm repetition, structure, or destination reuse strong enough to suggest a tunnel rather than one noisy session.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Websocket apps, remote support tools, security tooling, DNS-heavy services, and sanctioned overlay traffic.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when the same host shows repeated protocol misuse plus surrounding signs of tasking, staging, or alternate-channel egress.
hunt_methods.title: T1572 Protocol Tunnel Candidate Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify mixed DNS and HTTP tunnel patterns that support sustained covert transport.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface mixed DNS and HTTP behaviors that together form a more convincing tunnel story.
prerequisites: DNS and HTTP parser fields are retained for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because it combines several tunnel indicators into one reviewer workflow instead of relying on a single weak signal.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime HTTP And DNS Tunnel Review for T1572 Protocol Tunneling
Technique Context
- Review tunnel-like HTTP upgrade paths, DNS-heavy exchanges, and mixed protocol behavior to the same infrastructure.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((protocols == dns && bytes > <DNS_TUNNEL_BYTES>) || http.uri == /(?i)(websocket|socket\\.io|upgrade|connect)/)
What the search isolates: Sessions that can be correlated into HTTP or DNS tunnel workflows instead of ordinary application use.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, dns.host, host.http, http.uri, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the same source uses both DNS-heavy and web upgrade traffic to the same infrastructure family.
2. Pivot into later remote access or exfiltration sessions to see whether the suspected tunnel becomes an operational backbone.
Validation steps:
- Confirm persistent use of the same destination or family across more than one tunnel-like pattern.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Collaboration apps, remote support, and modern web services with upgrade flows.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when the same host uses tunnel-like HTTP or DNS behavior as the stable path into later operator activity.
hunt_methods.title: T1572 HTTP And DNS Tunnel Review

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify encrypted sessions that are rare for the source and reused like a command channel.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface rare encrypted channels that may conceal C2 or operator traffic.
prerequisites: TLS parser fields are retained for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show encrypted destination rarity, TLS reuse, and later traffic growth even when payload is opaque.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Rare Encrypted Channel Hunt for T1573 Encrypted Channel
Technique Context
- Review encrypted channels that are rare for the host, use non-standard ports, or have stable low-volume beacon-like profiles.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && ((protocols == tls && port.dst != 443) || (port.dst == 22 || port.dst == 1194 || port.dst == 51820 || port.dst == 4500 || port.dst == 500)) && bytes <= <SMALL_BEACON_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Encrypted sessions that may hide C2 content while standing out by port, protocol, destination, or host-role mismatch.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, ja3, cert.subject.cn, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source, destination, port, and JA3 to find repeated encrypted channels rather than one-off sessions.
2. Compare against approved VPN, SSH, remote admin, and application baselines for the host role.
3. Pivot into adjacent transfer, tasking, or remote-service sessions from the same source.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the encrypted path is rare, repeated, and not explained by approved administration or VPN use.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- VPN clients, SSH administration, cloud agents, remote support tools, and non-standard enterprise applications.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a rare encrypted channel recurs with stable JA3 or destination metadata and is adjacent to staging or exfiltration.
hunt_methods.title: T1573 Rare Encrypted Channel Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify unexpected SSH or VPN-like encrypted channels that can carry operator traffic.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface unexpected SSH or VPN-style encrypted channels that deserve operator review.

hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime SSH And VPN Symmetric Channel Hunt for T1573.001 Symmetric Cryptography
Technique Context
- Network metadata cannot prove the exact crypto primitive, but Arkime can still isolate SSH and VPN-style encrypted channels that often rely on session-key encryption after setup.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 22 || port.dst == 1194 || port.dst == 51820 || port.dst == 4500)
What the search isolates: SSH and VPN-like encrypted transport channels that can support tasking, lateral movement, or protected transfer.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the host normally establishes SSH or VPN-style channels to the destination.
2. Pivot into surrounding sessions to see whether the channel sits next to transfer or remote access behavior.

Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Admin SSH, VPN clients, and legitimate tunnels.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when unexpected SSH or VPN-style channels appear on endpoints and support operator activity.
hunt_methods.title: T1573.001 SSH And VPN Symmetric Channel Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify TLS channels that may hide asymmetric encrypted C2 or tasking.

# Arkime TLS Certificate Channel Hunt for T1573.002 Asymmetric Cryptography
Technique Context
- Review TLS channels with suspicious certificate metadata, rare JA3 values, or stable low-volume behavior that may hide asymmetric encrypted C2.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && protocols == tls && bytes <= <SMALL_BEACON_BYTES> && (cert.subject.cn == /(?i)(localhost|default|test|unknown|self|client|server|update|cdn|cloud)/ || port.dst != 443)
What the search isolates: Low-volume TLS sessions where certificate metadata or port choice makes the encrypted channel worth review.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, ja3, cert.subject.cn, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by JA3, certificate common name, and destination to find repeated encrypted channels.
2. Compare against known SaaS, EDR, VPN, and enterprise application TLS baselines.
3. Pivot into earlier payload retrieval and later exfiltration or fallback-channel behavior.
Validation steps:
- Confirm certificate/JA3 rarity and repeated source reuse rather than a single normal TLS session.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Internal PKI, appliances, VPNs, development systems, and enterprise agents with generic certificates.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when rare TLS metadata recurs to uncommon infrastructure and is adjacent to staging or data movement.
hunt_methods.title: T1573.002 TLS Certificate Channel Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify large egress over rare external paths that may represent an alternate transfer route.

# Arkime Alternate-Medium Egress Hunt for T1011 Exfiltration Over Other Network Medium
Technique Context
- Arkime cannot prove the physical medium changed without environment context, so use this as a supporting pivot for large egress over rare external paths that differ from normal business and C2 channels.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_EXTERNAL_SCOPE>) && bytes >= <LARGE_TRANSFER_BYTES> && protocols != http && protocols != http2 && protocols != tls && protocols != dns && protocols != smtp && protocols != imap && protocols != pop3
What the search isolates: Large outbound transfers over rare external paths that require validation against routing, host role, and known egress channels.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, protocols, port.dst, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, host.http, dns.host, ja3
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by protocol and destination to find rare egress paths that differ from normal web, DNS, mail, and approved transfer channels.
2. Validate whether the path represents an alternate network medium or merely an alternate protocol.
3. Pivot into earlier collection or staging and later repeated use of the same destination.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the route, interface, destination, or provider path is unusual for the source and operational window.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Partner transfers, VPNs, backups, legacy integrations, and sanctioned out-of-band administration.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when rare large egress follows suspicious staging and uses a route or medium not expected for the host.
hunt_methods.title: T1011 Alternate-Medium Egress Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify rare protocol volume shifts that may indicate a change in egress path or medium.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface rare-protocol volume shifts that may indicate early alternate-medium data transfer.

hunt_methods.strength_reason: Supporting pivot because Arkime sees the session path and byte shift, but separate context is needed to prove exfiltration over another network medium.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Rare Protocol Volume Shift Hunt for T1011 Exfiltration Over Other Network Medium
Technique Context
- Use session metadata to find hosts whose egress suddenly shifts to rare external transfer paths; validate medium change with routing or endpoint context.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_EXTERNAL_SCOPE>) && bytes >= <MID_TRANSFER_BYTES> && (protocols == ftp || protocols == ssh || protocols == smb || protocols == nfs || port.dst == 21 || port.dst == 22 || port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 873 || port.dst == 1194 || port.dst == 51820)
What the search isolates: Mid-to-large outbound sessions over rare transfer or tunnel-like paths that may indicate a shift away from normal egress.

Session-review workflow:
1. Compare the protocol and destination to the host's normal egress profile.
2. Pivot into route, VPN, proxy, or gateway context where available to determine whether the network medium changed.
3. Review earlier staging and repeated transfer sessions to the same infrastructure.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the protocol/path is rare for the host and coincides with staging, archiving, or suspicious collection.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Partner transfers, VPNs, backups, storage appliances, and sanctioned remote administration.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when rare protocol volume appears from a host that should not use that path and follows suspicious staging behavior.

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify automated-looking outbound transfer reuse from a suspect source.

# Arkime Automated Egress Reuse Hunt for T1020 Automated Exfiltration
Technique Context
- Review repeated outbound transfer sessions from the same source to the same external destination where volume and timing suggest automation rather than manual browsing.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_EXTERNAL_SCOPE>) && bytes >= <MID_TRANSFER_BYTES>
What the search isolates: External transfer sessions that should be grouped by source, destination, size, and cadence to identify automated egress.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, protocols, port.dst, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, host.http, dns.host, ja3
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and destination to identify repeated transfers or stable destination reuse.
2. Sort by firstSeen/lastSeen and compare transfer sizes for cadence or automation.
3. Pivot backward into collection/staging and forward into additional destination reuse.
Validation steps:
- Confirm repetition, consistent timing or sizes, and lack of an approved business transfer workflow.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Backups, cloud sync, package mirrors, partner transfers, and scheduled integrations.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when repeated external transfer sessions follow staging activity and do not match approved automation.

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify scheduled or repeated transfer cadence to external infrastructure.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface outbound transfers that can be validated as scheduled exfiltration by timing, size, and destination reuse.

# Arkime Scheduled Transfer Cadence Hunt for T1029 Scheduled Transfer

- Review outbound transfers whose timing, destination reuse, and similar byte sizes suggest scheduled exfiltration.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_EXTERNAL_SCOPE>) && bytes >= <MID_TRANSFER_BYTES>
What the search isolates: External transfers that should be sorted and grouped to identify repeated time-window or same-size cadence.

Session-review workflow:

2. Compare transfer sizes and durations to find same-size or same-time behavior.
3. Pivot into earlier staging and tasking sessions that precede each transfer window.
Validation steps:

Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Scheduled backups, reporting exports, ETL jobs, cloud sync, and partner file feeds.

# Arkime Sized Chunk Transfer Hunt for T1030 Data Transfer Size Limits

- Review repeated mid-sized outbound sessions to the same destination where byte sizes appear capped or chunked to avoid large-transfer detection.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_EXTERNAL_SCOPE>) && bytes >= <MID_TRANSFER_BYTES> && bytes < <LARGE_TRANSFER_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Mid-sized external transfers that should be grouped by source, destination, and size similarity to reveal chunking.

# Arkime C2 Channel Volume Shift Hunt for T1041 Exfiltration Over C2 Channel

- Review established tasking channels that suddenly carry much more outbound volume than their earlier small beacon profile.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_EXTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (protocols == http || protocols == http2 || protocols == tls || protocols == dns) && bytes >= <LARGE_TRANSFER_BYTES>

3. Check for prior staging, compression, or archive movement on the source host where host telemetry is available.

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify stable infrastructure that evolves from tasking into exfiltration.

hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.

hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Alternate Protocol Exfil Hunt for T1048 Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol
Technique Context
- Review large outbound transfers over protocols that are not the host's normal web, DNS, mail, or business egress channels.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_EXTERNAL_SCOPE>) && bytes >= <LARGE_TRANSFER_BYTES> && (protocols == ftp || protocols == ssh || protocols == smb || protocols == nfs || port.dst == 21 || port.dst == 22 || port.dst == 25 || port.dst == 587 || port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 873 || port.dst == 1194 || port.dst == 51820)
What the search isolates: Candidate alternate-protocol egress where protocol choice, destination, and source role make the transfer stand out.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, protocols, port.dst, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, host.http, dns.host, ja3
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by protocol and destination to find rare alternate egress paths.
2. Compare against approved file transfer, VPN, partner exchange, backup, and admin workflows.
3. Pivot into earlier staging, proxy, remote-service, or archive-transfer sessions from the same host.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the protocol and destination are not normal for the source host and that the transfer is outbound to external infrastructure.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- File exchange, partner integrations, backups, VPNs, and sanctioned admin transfer paths.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when alternate-protocol egress follows suspicious collection or staging activity and targets rare infrastructure.
hunt_methods.title: T1048 Alternate Protocol Exfil Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify large outbound encrypted non-C2 transfers over SSH or VPN-like channels.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface large encrypted non-C2 transfers over SSH or VPN-style channels that do not fit the source host.
prerequisites: SSH or VPN-style traffic is visible in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can still strongly isolate large SSH and VPN-style egress even without decrypting the payload.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime SSH Symmetric Channel Exfil Hunt for T1048.001 Exfiltration Over Symmetric Encrypted Non-C2 Protocol
Technique Context
- Network metadata cannot prove the exact crypto primitive, but Arkime can isolate large SSH or VPN-style outbound channels that are not normal for the host.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && bytes >= <LARGE_TRANSFER_BYTES> && (port.dst == 22 || port.dst == 1194 || port.dst == 51820 || port.dst == 4500)
What the search isolates: Large encrypted non-C2 channels consistent with SSH- or VPN-style transport rather than ordinary web traffic.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the host normally transfers large volumes over SSH or VPN-style channels.
2. Pivot into earlier staging or later relay sessions from the same source.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the path is unusual for the host and transfer volume is meaningful.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Admin tunnels, backups, VPN clients, and approved secure file transfer.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when endpoints that should not use SSH or VPN-style egress suddenly send large encrypted transfers after suspicious preparation.
hunt_methods.title: T1048.001 SSH Symmetric Channel Exfil Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify large outbound encrypted non-C2 transfers over TLS-like channels.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface large encrypted non-C2 TLS channels that do not fit the source host.
prerequisites: TLS parser fields are retained for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can differentiate large TLS transfer channels by destination, certificate reuse, and surrounding network context.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime TLS Certificate Channel Exfil Hunt for T1048.002 Exfiltration Over Asymmetric Encrypted Non-C2 Protocol
Technique Context
- Network metadata cannot prove the exact key exchange details, but Arkime can isolate large TLS-based non-C2 transfer channels with suspicious certificate or destination reuse.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && protocols == tls && bytes >= <LARGE_TRANSFER_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Large encrypted non-C2 TLS channels that can be grouped by certificate, JA3, and destination reuse.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, ja3, cert.subject.cn, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the TLS destination and certificate fit the host's normal business profile.
2. Pivot into earlier staging and later cloud or proxy sessions from the same host.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the same destination or certificate is reused and the byte volume is meaningful.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- SaaS uploads, backups, sync tools, and cloud services.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when large TLS transfers to rare infrastructure follow suspicious preparation or use uncommon TLS profiles.
hunt_methods.title: T1048.002 TLS Certificate Channel Exfil Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify suspicious clear-text data movement that does not belong to normal application or administrative traffic.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface large or repeated clear-text egress over alternate protocols that may support exfiltration.
prerequisites: Arkime is retaining HTTP metadata or clear-text file-transfer sessions for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can directly expose clear-text protocol choice, object paths, and large outbound sessions.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Clear Text Alternate Protocol Exfil Hunt for T1048.003 Exfiltration Over Unencrypted Non-C2 Protocol
Technique Context
- Review large or repeated outbound egress over clear-text protocols that are not normal command-and-control channels.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_EXTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (protocols == ftp || port.dst == 21 || port.dst == 80 || port.dst == 8080) && bytes >= <LARGE_TRANSFER_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Large outbound sessions over clear-text protocols that can reveal alternate-protocol exfiltration or suspicious upload activity.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, host.http, http.uri, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Sort by bytes and destination to find sources sending significant clear-text volume to rare destinations.
2. Check whether the same destination receives repeated medium or large clear-text transfers rather than one explainable upload.
3. Review HTTP URIs or FTP usage to decide whether the traffic is business file movement or suspicious egress.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the host and destination pair is unusual and the protocol choice does not fit approved workflows.
- Confirm directionality and destination reuse so the lead is not just one noisy clear-text session.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Legacy business integrations, web uploads, sanctioned file exchange, and approved partner portals.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when large or repeated clear-text egress goes to rare external destinations and is tightly coupled to staging or post-compromise activity.
hunt_methods.title: T1048.003 Clear Text Alternate Protocol Exfil Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify unusual clear-text upload semantics and object paths that support deliberate data transfer.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface unusual clear-text uploads that look like deliberate object transfer rather than normal application use.
prerequisites: HTTP parser fields are retained for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because it pushes beyond large transfer size alone into the upload semantics visible in Arkime HTTP fields.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Clear Text Object Upload Hunt for T1048.003 Exfiltration Over Unencrypted Non-C2 Protocol
Technique Context
- Review clear-text upload paths and object-like URIs to rare external destinations where the source host is not expected to post data.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_EXTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (((http.method == POST || http.method == PUT) || http.uri == /(?i)(upload|submit|attachment|multipart|api\/.*file)/) && bytes >= <MID_TRANSFER_BYTES>)
What the search isolates: Clear-text or weakly protected object uploads that can reflect deliberate data movement.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, http.method, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the source normally uploads to the destination or URI pattern.
2. Check whether the host repeats the same upload path or destination across several medium-sized sessions.
3. Pivot into earlier staging or archive retrieval sessions from the same host.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the upload path and destination are rare for the host.
- Confirm the behavior is outward-facing and repeated enough to look operational rather than incidental.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Legitimate forms, API uploads, content management, web publishing, and sanctioned browser-based file exchange.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when unusual clear-text object uploads recur after suspicious staging or collection activity.
hunt_methods.title: T1048.003 Clear Text Object Upload Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify cloud-account egress from hosts or workflows that do not normally upload significant data to those providers.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface unusual large transfers from endpoints into external cloud storage or sync accounts.
prerequisites: Arkime captures HTTP hostnames or DNS lookups for the cloud traffic in scope.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show cloud storage destination use, transfer size, and the endpoint context that makes it suspicious.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Cloud Account Egress Hunt for T1537 Transfer Data to Cloud Account
Technique Context
- Separate likely cloud-account exfiltration from normal SaaS traffic when large outbound sessions hit storage-oriented or sync-oriented providers from unusual hosts.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((host.http == /(?i)(s3\\.amazonaws\\.com|blob\\.core\\.windows\\.net|storage\\.googleapis\\.com|dropbox\\.com|box\\.com|onedrive|sharepoint|mega\\.nz)/) || (dns.host == /(?i)(s3\\.amazonaws\\.com|blob\\.core\\.windows\\.net|storage\\.googleapis\\.com|dropbox\\.com|box\\.com|onedrive|sharepoint|mega\\.nz)/)) && bytes >= <LARGE_TRANSFER_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Large sessions to storage-oriented cloud providers that may reflect a host pushing data into an external account.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, dns.host, http.uri, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Start with rare source hosts rather than the cloud provider itself.
2. Review URI patterns and timing to distinguish sanctioned sync clients from suspicious transfers.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source host is unusual for the provider and the outbound volume is meaningful.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- OneDrive, SharePoint, Box, Dropbox, backup agents, and business integrations.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a host not normally associated with storage sync sends large data volumes to cloud storage after suspicious preparation or remote access.
hunt_methods.title: T1537 Cloud Account Egress Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify collaboration or sync uploads that do not fit the source endpoint and may represent data transfer to an external account.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface suspicious large uploads into collaboration or sync services from unusual endpoints.

hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because it narrows cloud-account transfer review to collaboration and sync platforms seasoned defenders often investigate directly.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Collaboration And Sync Upload Hunt for T1537 Transfer Data to Cloud Account
Technique Context
- Review large outbound traffic to collaboration and sync services when the endpoint is not a normal content owner or sync participant.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((host.http == /(?i)(sharepoint|onedrive|box\\.com|dropbox\\.com|drive\\.google\\.com|docs\\.google\\.com)/) || (dns.host == /(?i)(sharepoint|onedrive|box\\.com|dropbox\\.com|drive\\.google\\.com|docs\\.google\\.com)/)) && bytes >= <MID_TRANSFER_BYTES>

1. Review whether the endpoint normally uses the collaboration platform for uploads.
2. Pivot into earlier archive retrieval, remote access, or staging sessions from the same host.
Validation steps:
- Confirm unusual upload volume or destination use for the host role.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Legitimate collaboration, sync clients, and enterprise content workflows.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when unusual collaboration-platform uploads appear from hosts tied to suspicious staging or remote access behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1537 Collaboration And Sync Upload Hunt

# Arkime Web Service Egress Hunt for T1567 Exfiltration Over Web Service
Technique Context
- Review outbound transfers to legitimate web services that can provide cover for exfiltration because the service is commonly allowed.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_EXTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (protocols == http || protocols == http2 || protocols == tls) && bytes >= <LARGE_TRANSFER_BYTES> && (host.http == /(?i)(dropbox|box\.com|onedrive|sharepoint|drive\.google|docs\.google|storage\.googleapis|s3\.amazonaws|blob\.core\.windows\.net|mega\.nz|mediafire|file\.io|anonfiles|telegram|discord|slack)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(dropbox|box\.com|onedrive|sharepoint|drive\.google|docs\.google|storage\.googleapis|s3\.amazonaws|blob\.core\.windows\.net|mega\.nz|mediafire|file\.io|anonfiles|telegram|discord|slack)/)
What the search isolates: Large outbound web-service transfers that should be validated against approved cloud storage, collaboration, and API workflows.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, dns.host, http.uri, http.method, bytes, packets, ja3, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Separate sanctioned sync clients and business workflows from unusual hosts or off-hours uploads.
2. Pivot backward into local staging, archive movement, or C2 tasking from the same source.
3. Group by service family and source to identify repeated exfiltration candidates.
Validation steps:
- Confirm large outbound web-service use is unusual for the source host or follows suspicious collection/staging.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Cloud storage sync, collaboration uploads, backups, file sharing, and normal SaaS activity.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when rare or unauthorized web-service transfers follow suspicious staging and recur to the same service or account path.

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Use known scan infrastructure to expand into the surrounding reconnaissance behavior.
hunt_methods.expectation: Pivot from known scanning infrastructure into the surrounding Arkime session story.

hunt_methods.surface_details: Use known infrastructure as the starting point and pivot outward into the related Arkime sessions.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime IOC Pivot for T1595 Active Scanning
Technique Context
- Use this when you already have scanning infrastructure and want Arkime to expand the surrounding sessions.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (ip.src == <IP_IOC> || ip.dst == <IP_IOC> || dns.host == '<DOMAIN_IOC>' || host.http == '<DOMAIN_IOC>' || http.uri == '<URL_IOC>' || cert.subject.cn == '<HOSTNAME_IOC>')
What the pivot isolates: Direct touches to known scan infrastructure so you can pivot into the wider scan set and later follow-on sessions.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Start at the IOC hit and expand to the same source across adjacent time windows.
2. Review how the IOC-linked traffic fans out across ports and destinations.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the IOC hit sits in the same operational window as the suspected scan.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Shared hosting, cloud edges, or previously known benign scanners.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when IOC-linked sessions expand into broad scan fan-out or lead directly to exploitation.

# Arkime IOC Pivot for T1090 Proxy
Technique Context
- Use this when another source gives proxy, relay, redirector, or CDN-fronting infrastructure and you need Arkime to enumerate affected sources and surrounding sessions.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((ip.dst == <IP_IOC>) || dns.host == '<DOMAIN_IOC>' || host.http == '<DOMAIN_IOC>' || cert.subject.cn == '<HOSTNAME_IOC>' || (protocols == http && http.method == CONNECT))
What the pivot isolates: Sessions touching known relay infrastructure or proxy-style traffic that may front later tasking, staging, or egress.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, host.http, dns.host, http.method, ja3, cert.subject.cn, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Start at the direct IOC hit and identify all internal sources using the relay infrastructure.
2. Pivot forward and backward from each source to see whether the relay fronts C2, staging, or exfiltration.
3. Compare against approved proxy, CDN, and web gateway infrastructure.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the IOC is used as an intermediary path rather than a normal website or shared cloud endpoint.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Shared hosting, CDNs, corporate proxies, cloud gateways, and security inspection infrastructure.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when relay IOC use is rare for the source and fronts repeated tasking, staging, or egress sessions.

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify channel switching and destination reuse that suggest fallback transport logic.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface hosts that appear to maintain more than one candidate tasking path to the same infrastructure family.
prerequisites: Arkime retains parser fields for the outbound protocols in scope.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can reveal protocol switching and destination reuse that often exposes fallback behavior.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Fallback Channel Shift Hunt for T1008 Fallback Channels
Technique Context
- Review hosts that reuse the same suspicious destination family but shift between DNS, HTTP, TLS, or non-application transport as if one channel is backing up another.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && ((protocols == http || protocols == tls || protocols == dns || protocols == icmp || protocols == gre) && bytes <= <SMALL_BEACON_BYTES>)
What the search isolates: Small repeated outbound sessions that can be grouped by host and time window to reveal multi-protocol fallback behavior.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, protocols, host.http, dns.host, ja3, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and review whether the same host shifts protocols or destinations after a channel goes quiet.
2. Pivot into staging, tunneling, or later transfer sessions that reuse the same destination family.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the same host uses more than one candidate control path in the same operational window.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Multi-homed applications, failover logic, or products that support several transport options.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when the host rotates between small repeated channels and one path later becomes a stable tasking or transfer route.
hunt_methods.title: T1008 Fallback Channel Shift Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify fallback behavior where one tasking path gives way to another.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface hosts that recover command traffic over alternate paths instead of losing contact completely.
prerequisites: The hunt scope covers enough time to compare one control path against a later fallback path.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime is strong at timeline-based comparison of small repeated sessions across protocols and ports.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Alternate Path Recovery Hunt for T1008 Fallback Channels
Technique Context
- Look for a host that resumes low-volume tasking through a different protocol or port family after a prior channel drops away.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && ((protocols == http || protocols == tls || protocols == dns || port.dst == 443 || port.dst == 53 || port.dst == 8080 || port.dst == 8443) && bytes <= <SMALL_BEACON_BYTES>)
What the search isolates: Candidate recovery behavior where a host continues small outbound control traffic but no longer on the original path.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review the timeline for one host and note whether a small repeated channel stops and a new one appears shortly after.
2. Compare infrastructure overlap, domain family, and later transfer behavior before treating the shift as malicious.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the same host transitions quickly between candidate control paths instead of maintaining one stable service pattern.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Client failover, CDN changes, or applications testing alternate endpoints.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when the new path is rare for the host and preserves the same operator-like cadence as the earlier channel.
hunt_methods.title: T1008 Alternate Path Recovery Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify suspicious public web-service use as a control or rendezvous path.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface public web-service use that behaves like rendezvous or light tasking rather than normal browsing.
prerequisites: HTTP host and URI metadata are retained for the scoped outbound traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can tie public web-service reuse to cadence, destination family, and adjacent transfer behavior.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Web Service Rendezvous Hunt for T1102 Web Service
Technique Context
- Public web services matter here when they behave like a repeated rendezvous layer, not simply because the host contacted a familiar brand or platform.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (host.http == /(?i)(pastebin|gist|discord|slack|telegram|githubusercontent|dropbox|blogspot|wordpress|notion)/) && bytes <= <SMALL_BEACON_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Small repeated outbound sessions to public web platforms where cadence, reuse, and later behavioral drift make the service family look like rendezvous or control infrastructure.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, http.method, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and service family to find repeated small conversations to the same platform rather than one browser-driven visit.
2. Review whether the same platform later becomes retrieval, upload, or fallback-channel infrastructure for the same host.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the service family is rare for the host role and the sessions recur in a stable operational cadence.
- Check whether the host reuses the same platform as part of adjacent tasking, retrieval, or exfiltration behavior.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Developer workflows, collaboration tooling, browser tabs, sanctioned integrations, and normal API use.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when one public service family is reused in a stable pattern and becomes the pivot point into other operator-like network behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1102 Web Service Rendezvous Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify dead-drop-style content retrieval that triggers later network behavior.

# Arkime Dead Drop Resolver Review for T1102.001 Dead Drop Resolver
Technique Context
- Dead-drop retrieval becomes interesting when a read-only fetch to a public text or raw-content surface is quickly followed by new destinations, protocol changes, or stable operator cadence.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && http.method == GET && (host.http == /(?i)(pastebin|paste|gist|rentry|githubusercontent|raw)/)
What the search isolates: Candidate dead-drop retrieval sessions that gain value when they immediately bridge into later control, retrieval, or transfer activity.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, http.user-agent, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Start with the retrieval and pivot to the same source in the next few minutes rather than judging the one GET in isolation.
2. Review whether the host immediately contacts new destinations, changes protocols, or begins a repeated cadence after the read-only fetch.
Validation steps:
- Confirm a dead-drop-like fetch is followed by new destination use, tasking behavior, or retrieval that reuses the same infrastructure family.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Developer documentation, public code retrieval, package fetches, or normal browser use of raw content sites.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a small read-only fetch repeatedly acts as the precursor to new infrastructure contact or later operator-controlled behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1102.001 Dead Drop Resolver Review

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify public web-service API paths used like recurring bidirectional command channels.

prerequisites: HTTP host, method, and URI metadata are retained for the scoped outbound traffic.

hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Bidirectional Web Service Tasking Hunt for T1102.002 Bidirectional Communication
Technique Context
- Bidirectional web-service C2 is strongest when one API surface shows repeated small request and response pairs, then later drives retrieval, upload, or destination changes.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (host.http == /(?i)(discord|slack|telegram|github|gitlab|notion|dropbox|googleapis|graph)/) && (http.method == POST || http.method == PUT || http.method == GET) && bytes <= <SMALL_BEACON_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Candidate bidirectional API conversations that are suspicious because one service family behaves like a stable control path rather than ordinary user or app traffic.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, http.method, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and service family to find repeated small requests and responses to the same API surface over time.
2. Review whether that same service family later carries larger uploads, retrievals, or infrastructure pivots that make the earlier small traffic meaningful.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the service family supports repeated check-ins and then drifts into tasking, retrieval, or upload behavior.
- Compare the cadence and method mix against the host role so normal integrations are not overcalled.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Collaboration clients, developer tooling, sanctioned workflow integrations, and normal SaaS API chatter.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a rare API surface for the host becomes a stable conversation path and later drives materially different behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1102.002 Bidirectional Web Service Tasking Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify bidirectional API conversations that evolve from lightweight check-ins into operator-driven action.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface public web-service conversations that drift from simple check-ins into action-oriented activity.
prerequisites: Arkime retains enough timeline depth to compare earlier and later API sessions for the same host.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime is strong at showing behavioral drift in one service family over time.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime API Conversation Drift Hunt for T1102.002 Bidirectional Communication
Technique Context
- The useful signal is drift over time: a service family that begins as small repetitive API traffic and then changes in size, timing, or destination behavior as operations progress.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (host.http == /(?i)(discord|slack|telegram|github|gitlab|notion|dropbox|googleapis|graph)/)
What the search isolates: Public web-service API conversations whose size, cadence, or surrounding behavior changes enough to suggest tasking has turned into action.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, http.method, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review the timeline for one host and service family instead of one isolated session.
2. Separate benign API chatter from service families that evolve into downloads, uploads, fallback channels, or new destination use.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the same service family shows a clear behavioral shift from light tasking into materially different activity.
- Check whether the drift is rare for the host role and repeated enough to be operational rather than incidental.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Feature changes in collaboration clients, SaaS sync behavior, or API-heavy developer workflows.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when one service family becomes the transition point into retrieval, upload, fallback-channel use, or new infrastructure contact.
hunt_methods.title: T1102.002 API Conversation Drift Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify one-way web polling used as a broadcast-style control path.

hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime One-Way Web Broadcast Hunt for T1102.003 One-Way Communication

Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && http.method == GET && (host.http == /(?i)(blogspot|wordpress|tumblr|medium|paste|gist|githubusercontent|raw)/) && bytes <= <SMALL_BEACON_BYTES>
