hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify repeated auth attempts through short, concentrated network sessions.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface repeated short auth attempts that resemble brute-force behavior rather than ordinary access.
prerequisites: Auth-facing ports or login paths are visible in the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can reveal retry-heavy auth session patterns and the pivot into later access.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Repeated Auth Session Hunt for T1110 Brute Force
Technique Context
- Review repeated short auth-oriented sessions where the same source keeps returning to the same service or login path.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((port.dst == 22 || port.dst == 3389 || port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 21 || port.dst == 25 || port.dst == 587 || port.dst == 993 || port.dst == 995) || (http.method == POST && http.uri == /(?i)(login|signin|auth|oauth|token|session)/)) && packets <= <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS>
What the search isolates: Candidate auth attempts that are short enough to reflect repeated tries rather than full interactive sessions.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, http.uri, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and destination to find concentrated retry behavior.
2. Pivot into nearby successes, follow-on interactive sessions, or destination fan-out before escalating.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the same source repeatedly touches the same auth surface with short-lived sessions.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Legitimate retries, password managers, scanners, or health checks.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when repeated auth attempts are followed by successful interactive access or lateral movement.
hunt_methods.title: T1110 Repeated Auth Session Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify one-source-to-one-target login retry bursts.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface concentrated retry bursts against one login target.
prerequisites: Auth-facing ports or login paths are visible in the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show concentrated one-target retry bursts and their follow-on access.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Password Guessing Burst Hunt for T1110.001 Password Guessing
Technique Context
- Focus on one source repeatedly returning to one destination and service in a compressed time window.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((port.dst == 22 || port.dst == 3389 || port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 21 || port.dst == 5985 || port.dst == 5986) || (http.method == POST && http.uri == /(?i)(login|signin|auth|session)/)) && packets <= <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS>
What the search isolates: Candidate password guessing where one source concentrates on one login target instead of spraying broadly.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, http.uri, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and destination to spot tight retry bursts.
2. Review whether the same source eventually succeeds and becomes interactive.
Validation steps:
- Confirm repeated short auth sessions to one target within a narrow window.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- User lockouts, automation retries, or monitoring.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when the burst ends in a sustained session or is paired with reconnaissance against the same host.
hunt_methods.title: T1110.001 Password Guessing Burst Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify one-source-to-many-target auth attempts consistent with password spraying.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface one-to-many login fan-out that resembles password spraying rather than guessing.
prerequisites: The hunt scope includes the auth-facing segments or login paths being abused.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show one-source auth fan-out across many targets cleanly in session metadata.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Password Spray Fan-Out Hunt for T1110.003 Password Spraying
Technique Context
- Focus on one source touching many login targets or auth services with small repeated sessions instead of concentrating on one system.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((port.dst == 22 || port.dst == 3389 || port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 993 || port.dst == 995 || port.dst == 587) || (http.method == POST && http.uri == /(?i)(login|signin|auth|session)/)) && packets <= <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS>
What the search isolates: Candidate spray behavior where the same source fans out across multiple auth targets with short session attempts.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, http.uri, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source first, then inspect how many distinct targets or login surfaces are touched.
2. Pivot into any later successful or interactive sessions from the same source.
Validation steps:
- Confirm one source fans out across many auth targets with short-lived attempts.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Health checks, scanners, account-management automation, or SSO testing.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when wide auth fan-out is followed by a small set of successful interactive sessions.
hunt_methods.title: T1110.003 Password Spray Fan-Out Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify distributed password-spray attempts over a wider window.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface low-and-slow auth fan-out that would be missed by burst-only review.
prerequisites: The Arkime scope retains enough timeline depth to examine spaced auth attempts.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime timeline review can expose spaced password-spray behavior that simple burst logic misses.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Low-And-Slow Spray Window Hunt for T1110.003 Password Spraying
Technique Context
- Review auth attempts that are sparse enough to evade obvious burst logic but still cover many targets over one operational window.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((port.dst == 22 || port.dst == 3389 || port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 993 || port.dst == 995 || port.dst == 587) || (http.method == POST && http.uri == /(?i)(login|signin|auth|session)/)) && packets <= <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS>
What the search isolates: Small auth sessions that can be regrouped over a larger window to reveal low-and-slow spray behavior.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, firstSeen, lastSeen, packets, bytes
Session-review workflow:
1. Expand the time window and look for the same source returning to many auth targets without a tight burst.
2. Review whether one or two targets later receive longer successful sessions.
Validation steps:
- Confirm broad auth coverage by one source even when the attempts are spaced out.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Scheduled maintenance, management agents, or scattered user retries.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when spaced auth attempts across many targets culminate in later successful access from the same source.
hunt_methods.title: T1110.003 Low-And-Slow Spray Window Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify repeated external web-login attempts consistent with credential stuffing.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface repeated web-login POST behavior that resembles credential stuffing rather than ordinary use.
prerequisites: HTTP method and URI metadata are retained for the relevant login surface.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show repeated external login POST behavior and the pivot into later access.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Credential Stuffing Login Burst Hunt for T1110.004 Credential Stuffing
Technique Context
- Review external web login traffic where one source or infrastructure family drives repeated POSTs into the same auth paths.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_EXTERNAL_SCOPE>) && http.method == POST && http.uri == /(?i)(login|signin|auth|session|oauth|token)/ && packets <= <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS>
What the search isolates: Candidate credential-stuffing attempts against web login surfaces where repeated POSTs replace ordinary user behavior.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, http.user-agent, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and login path to find repeated POST activity.
2. Review user-agent stability, destination concentration, and later interactive sessions from the same infrastructure.
Validation steps:
- Confirm repeated login POSTs from the same source or source family into the same auth surface.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- SSO retries, bots, QA testing, or vulnerability scanning.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when the same login surface receives repeated POST attempts and later successful sessions or token use.
hunt_methods.title: T1110.004 Credential Stuffing Login Burst Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify encoded-looking DNS and HTTP elements used to carry control or payload data.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface long encoded-looking elements that may carry hidden tasking or payload data.
prerequisites: HTTP URI and DNS host metadata are retained for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can expose structured encoded-looking fields and tie them to later operational behavior.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Encoded Payload Parameter Hunt for T1132 Data Encoding
Technique Context
- Encoded payload carriage matters when long structured values recur to one destination family and behave like a stable tasking or transfer path, not when one long token appears once.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((http.uri == /[A-Za-z0-9_\/-]{40,}/) || (dns.host == /([A-Za-z0-9-]{25,}\.){2,}/))
What the search isolates: Candidate encoded parameter carriage in URIs or DNS labels where repeated structure, destination reuse, and later operational pivots matter more than literal content.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, http.uri, dns.host, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the structured values recur to the same destination family or sit inside one stable tasking path.
2. Pivot into later sessions that use the same infrastructure for tunneling, retrieval, or transfer instead of escalating one isolated long token.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the long values are rare for the application and are reused or patterned over time.
- Separate one-off signed URLs or tokens from cases where the same structured field family persists across several sessions.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Tracking tokens, long API keys, CDN paths, legitimate signed URLs, and structured application parameters.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when encoded-looking elements recur with stable cadence, destination reuse, and later control or exfiltration behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1132 Encoded Payload Parameter Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify recurring base64, base32, or hex-like fields that may carry operator-controlled data.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface standard-encoding candidates that act like operator-controlled structured content.
prerequisites: HTTP URI and DNS host metadata are retained for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can reveal recurring encoded field use to stable infrastructure without relying on English keywords.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Standard Encoding Review Hunt for T1132.001 Standard Encoding
Technique Context
- Standard encoding becomes useful here when base64, base32, or hex-like values recur to stable infrastructure in an operational cadence instead of appearing as one ordinary application token.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((http.uri == /(?i)([A-Za-z0-9+\/]{32,}=*)/) || (dns.host == /(?i)([a-z2-7]{24,}\.){2,}/) || (http.uri == /(?i)[a-f0-9]{32,}/))
What the search isolates: Standard-encoding candidates embedded in network-visible fields that may support staged tasking or payload fragments when the same destination family persists.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, http.uri, dns.host, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by destination and review whether the encoded-looking values change while the infrastructure remains stable.
2. Pivot into neighboring sessions to see whether the same destination later becomes a transfer, tunnel, or retrieval path.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the encoding-like strings are rare for the application and recur operationally to the same infrastructure family.
- Avoid overcalling one-off signed URLs, telemetry identifiers, or legitimate object keys.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Signed URLs, tokens, telemetry identifiers, object keys, and normal structured application values.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when standard-encoded-looking values recur on rare infrastructure, show stable cadence, and align with later control or exfiltration behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1132.001 Standard Encoding Review Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify long structured values that suggest custom encoding or transformation.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface long structured values that look custom-encoded rather than ordinary application parameters.
prerequisites: HTTP URI and DNS host metadata are retained for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show repeated long structured fields even when the analyst cannot decode them immediately.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Non-Standard Encoding Marker Hunt for T1132.002 Non-Standard Encoding
Technique Context
- Custom or transformed content becomes interesting when long structured values repeatedly hit the same destination family and do not fit the application's normal parameter style.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((http.uri == /(?i)[A-Za-z0-9._-]{50,}/) || (dns.host == /([A-Za-z0-9-]{20,}\.){3,}/))
What the search isolates: Candidate custom-encoding or transformed-content markers where destination reuse and recurrent structure matter more than one strange value.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, http.uri, dns.host, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the same destination repeatedly receives long structured values that change from session to session.
2. Pivot into later tunneling, staging, or exfiltration behavior to decide whether the destination is more than an application endpoint.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the value pattern is operationally structured, repeated, and unusual for the application.
- Avoid overcalling one-off GUID-heavy APIs, object keys, or app-specific identifiers.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Long object keys, GUID-heavy APIs, application-specific identifiers, and telemetry routing values.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when the same destination repeatedly carries long structured values that do not fit the app and align with later operator-like behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1132.002 Non-Standard Encoding Marker Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify segmented custom-encoded payload patterns inside DNS or web traffic.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface repeated structured values that look more like encoded payload chunks than normal app content.
prerequisites: DNS and HTTP metadata are retained for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can highlight repeated segmented payload structure even without decoding the content.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Custom Alphabet Payload Hunt for T1132.002 Non-Standard Encoding
Technique Context
- Focus on DNS or web elements that appear to carry chunked payloads with unusual character distribution or repeated segmented structure to one stable destination family.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((protocols == dns && bytes >= <DNS_INFLATED_BYTES>) || (http.uri == /(?i)([_-][A-Za-z0-9]{20,}){2,}/))
What the search isolates: Candidate custom alphabet or segmented payload carriage where repetition, destination reuse, and recurring structure are more suspicious than the literal value.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, dns.host, http.uri, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the same source sends a series of similarly structured values to one destination family instead of one isolated odd string.
2. Pivot into later tunneling, alternate-protocol egress, or web-service control to see whether the values sit inside a larger operator workflow.
Validation steps:
- Confirm repeated segmented values or inflated DNS sessions to the same destination family.
- Separate recurring structure from one-off telemetry identifiers or object keys.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Telemetry identifiers, segmented object keys, application-specific routing strings, and normal structured parameters.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when the structured values recur to rare infrastructure and coincide with other operator-like network behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1132.002 Custom Alphabet Payload Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify unusual SMB or HTTP callbacks that may reflect forced authentication.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface unusual internal SMB or HTTP callbacks that may reflect coerced authentication.
prerequisites: The hunt scope includes east-west traffic and short internal callbacks are visible in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show unexpected callback concentration and its relationship to surrounding relay-prerequisite traffic.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Forced Auth Callback Hunt for T1187 Forced Authentication
Technique Context
- Review internal SMB and HTTP callbacks to unusual peers that may reflect coerced authentication rather than user-driven access.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && ((port.dst == 445) || (port.dst == 80) || (port.dst == 443)) && packets <= <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS>
What the search isolates: Short internal callback sessions that can be pivoted against earlier name-resolution or remote procedure activity.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether one internal host suddenly receives many short SMB or HTTP callbacks from peers that do not normally contact it.
2. Pivot into nearby name-resolution, RPC, or relay-prerequisite traffic involving the same systems.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the callback target is unusual for the source hosts and the activity clusters in a narrow window.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- File sharing, software distribution, web health checks, or management tooling.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when unusual callbacks line up with relay-prerequisite traffic or later successful authentication use.
hunt_methods.title: T1187 Forced Auth Callback Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify concentrated packet pressure consistent with network flood activity.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface targets under unusual packet pressure that may reflect deliberate network flooding.
prerequisites: Arkime sees the target service path and packet counts are retained accurately.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can highlight packet concentration and target focus without requiring payload inspection.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Network Flood Concentration Hunt for T1498 Network Denial of Service
Technique Context
- Review targets receiving extremely high packet volume or session concentration from one source or one source family.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && packets >= <FLOOD_PACKET_THRESHOLD>
What the search isolates: Candidate network flood behavior where concentration and packet volume matter more than payload content.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, protocols, port.dst, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by destination to identify the systems taking the highest packet pressure.
2. Review whether one source dominates the traffic or whether the event broadens into many-source concentration.
Validation steps:
- Confirm packet volume is materially above normal for the target and service.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Backups, data replication, scanning, or legitimate throughput tests.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when packet concentration degrades a target service or clearly departs from normal operational throughput.
hunt_methods.title: T1498 Network Flood Concentration Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify likely reflection or amplification flood behavior against a target.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface reflection-style network floods against one destination or service family.
prerequisites: Arkime sees the flood path and service ports used by likely reflector protocols.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can expose amplification-oriented source-port concentration and flood timing.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Reflection-Oriented Throughput Hunt for T1498 Network Denial of Service
Technique Context
- Review whether a target receives unusually large response-oriented traffic from protocols often abused for reflection or amplification.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (port.src == 53 || port.src == 123 || port.src == 1900 || port.src == 11211 || port.src == 389) && packets >= <FLOOD_PACKET_THRESHOLD>
What the search isolates: Candidate reflection or amplification traffic where the target is receiving unusually large volumes from classic reflector services.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.src, protocols, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by destination and source port to see whether the target is receiving flood traffic from reflector-like services.
2. Pivot into the apparent reflector population and timing to separate widespread unsolicited traffic from expected service use.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the destination receives traffic from reflector-style ports at unusual scale.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Misconfigured service scans, testing, or legitimate service traffic at smaller scale.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a target receives large unsolicited traffic from classic reflector ports across many sources.
hunt_methods.title: T1498 Reflection-Oriented Throughput Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify direct source-to-target flood traffic.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface direct source-to-target flood behavior rather than reflector-driven volume.
prerequisites: The flood path is visible in Arkime and packet counts are retained accurately.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can differentiate direct sender concentration from reflection-style patterns.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Direct Flood Session Storm Hunt for T1498.001 Direct Network Flood
Technique Context
- Review one source or one source family overwhelming one target directly rather than through reflector services.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && packets >= <FLOOD_PACKET_THRESHOLD> && port.src != 53 && port.src != 123 && port.src != 1900 && port.src != 11211
What the search isolates: Candidate direct flood traffic where one source or one source family sends the storm itself.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, protocols, port.dst, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and destination to find dominant direct senders.
2. Review whether the target service or port family is the same across the event.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the traffic is concentrated and direct rather than a reflection pattern.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Load tests, replication bursts, or aggressive scanning.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when direct packet storms target one host or service and are not part of an approved test.
hunt_methods.title: T1498.001 Direct Flood Session Storm Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify reflection-amplification traffic patterns converging on one victim.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface reflection fan-in behavior against one destination.
prerequisites: Arkime sees the reflected traffic path and service ports used by likely reflectors.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can reveal many-source reflector fan-in to one target clearly in session metadata.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Reflection Amplification Fan-In Hunt for T1498.002 Reflection Amplification
Technique Context
- Focus on one target receiving response-heavy traffic from many apparent reflector systems using the same service ports.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (port.src == 53 || port.src == 123 || port.src == 1900 || port.src == 11211 || port.src == 389)
What the search isolates: Candidate reflection fan-in where many reflector-like systems appear to send traffic into one victim.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.src, protocols, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by destination first and then examine how many distinct sources share the same reflector-like source ports.
2. Compare the event against expected service traffic or known stress tests before escalating.
Validation steps:
- Confirm many sources converge on one target through classic reflector service ports.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Large but legitimate service traffic or approved test events.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when many sources fan into one target through reflector-style services at abnormal scale.
hunt_methods.title: T1498.002 Reflection Amplification Fan-In Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify endpoint service exhaustion through concentrated session pressure.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface service-focused denial activity against one endpoint or admin surface.
prerequisites: Arkime sees the endpoint service path and packet counts are retained accurately.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show service-specific session concentration against one endpoint cleanly.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Endpoint Service Exhaustion Hunt for T1499 Endpoint Denial of Service
Technique Context
- Review targets receiving abnormally dense session pressure against user-facing or admin-facing services where the effect is local service exhaustion rather than broad link saturation.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && packets >= <FLOOD_PACKET_THRESHOLD> && (port.dst == 80 || port.dst == 443 || port.dst == 3389 || port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 5985 || port.dst == 5986)
What the search isolates: Candidate service exhaustion where session pressure is concentrated on one endpoint or service rather than one network segment.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by destination and port to identify the service under the heaviest pressure.
2. Review whether the pressure comes from one source, many sources, or exploit-like request paths.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the volume is abnormal for the endpoint and service.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Load testing, software rollout spikes, or bulk administrative activity.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when service-focused session pressure is clearly degrading one endpoint or service path.
hunt_methods.title: T1499 Endpoint Service Exhaustion Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify application-layer request storms against a single endpoint or URI family.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface application-focused request storms that can exhaust one endpoint.
prerequisites: HTTP host and URI metadata are retained for the targeted application traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can isolate one application surface receiving repeated heavy request pressure.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Application Request Storm Hunt for T1499 Endpoint Denial of Service
Technique Context
- Focus on dense repeated web requests or protocol operations against one endpoint where the application itself appears to be the bottleneck.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((port.dst == 80 || port.dst == 443) && packets >= <FLOOD_PACKET_THRESHOLD>)
What the search isolates: Candidate application-layer request storms against one web-facing service.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by destination and URI family to see whether the traffic is hammering one application surface.
2. Review whether the pattern is broad and repetitive rather than normal client behavior.
Validation steps:
- Confirm one endpoint or URI family is receiving extreme repeated request pressure.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Flash crowds, load tests, or expected business spikes.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when one application surface receives extreme repeated pressure from one or many sources without an approved operational reason.
hunt_methods.title: T1499 Application Request Storm Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify broad multi-service flood behavior consistent with host resource exhaustion.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface host-wide session pressure more consistent with OS exhaustion than one application bottleneck.
prerequisites: Arkime sees the relevant traffic path and packet counts are retained accurately.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can distinguish broad host-level pressure from one-service storms through destination and port spread.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime OS Exhaustion Flood Hunt for T1499.001 OS Exhaustion Flood
Technique Context
- Review hosts receiving broad packet or session pressure across multiple service ports where the goal may be host resource exhaustion rather than one application path.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && packets >= <FLOOD_PACKET_THRESHOLD>
What the search isolates: Candidate host-level flood activity that is less service-specific and more consistent with overall resource exhaustion.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by destination and inspect whether multiple ports or services are affected together.
2. Separate host-wide pressure from one-service request storms before escalating.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the target sees broad packet pressure across more than one service path.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Throughput tests, replication bursts, or expected maintenance windows.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when one endpoint receives broad multi-service pressure that does not fit an approved operational event.
hunt_methods.title: T1499.001 OS Exhaustion Flood Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify exploit-like request pressure that may crash or destabilize one service.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface repeated exploit-looking pressure against one target that may degrade the service.
prerequisites: The targeted service path is visible in Arkime and includes parser fields for the suspicious requests.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can reveal repeated suspicious request concentration against a service and the resulting behavior change.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Exploit Driven Service Crash Hunt for T1499.004 Application or System Exploitation
Technique Context
- Review repeated exploit-looking web requests or service interactions that appear to target one endpoint until the service behavior changes or goes quiet.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((http.uri == /(?i)(admin|manager|cgi-bin|api|upload|debug|invoke|deserialize|soap|xmlrpc)/) || port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 3389 || port.dst == 5985)
What the search isolates: Candidate exploit-driven service pressure where one target receives repeated suspicious requests rather than normal use.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, port.dst, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review repeated suspicious request paths or service touches to one endpoint.
2. Compare the traffic before and after the suspected exploit burst to see whether the service behavior changes sharply.
Validation steps:
- Confirm exploit-looking requests concentrate on one endpoint and are followed by service disruption or silence.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Vulnerability scanners, QA testing, or admin troubleshooting.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when exploit-looking pressure is followed by abrupt service instability or downstream denial symptoms.
hunt_methods.title: T1499.004 Exploit Driven Service Crash Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify unexpected local mediation behavior consistent with adversary-in-the-middle activity.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface hosts that unexpectedly take on a mediation role in local traffic flows.
prerequisites: The hunt scope includes east-west traffic and local mediation protocols are visible in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can reveal hosts unexpectedly inserted into local resolution and auth-adjacent traffic paths.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Gateway Drift Mediation Hunt for T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle
Technique Context
- Review local mediation indicators such as unexpected ARP, DHCP, or DNS changes that could place one system in the middle of peer traffic.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (protocols == arp || port.dst == 53 || port.dst == 67 || port.dst == 68)
What the search isolates: Candidate local mediation traffic that can be pivoted into later SMB, HTTP, or relay-prerequisite activity.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, protocols, port.dst, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether one host appears repeatedly in gateway-like, DNS-like, or responder-like traffic it does not normally own.
2. Pivot into later SMB, HTTP, or auth activity involving the same host.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the suspected mediation host is unusual for the environment or segment.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Legitimate infrastructure, onboarding workflows, or segment changes.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when one internal host unexpectedly appears central to resolution, gateway, or auth-adjacent traffic and is followed by relay or credential abuse patterns.
hunt_methods.title: T1557 Gateway Drift Mediation Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify local responder behavior that is followed by SMB or HTTP callback traffic.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface responder-style name-resolution activity followed by auth-adjacent callbacks.
prerequisites: The hunt scope includes local name-resolution and follow-on SMB or HTTP traffic.

# Arkime LLMNR NBT-NS Responder Follow-On Hunt for T1557.001 LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay
Technique Context
- Review hosts that respond to local name-resolution traffic and then quickly receive SMB or HTTP callbacks from peers.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 5355 || port.dst == 137 || port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 80)
What the search isolates: Candidate responder behavior that becomes more suspicious when it is followed by SMB or HTTP auth-related traffic.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether one host repeatedly appears in local name-resolution traffic before peers connect back over SMB or HTTP.
2. Pivot into later relay-prerequisite or successful access behavior from the same systems.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the suspected responder is not an approved infrastructure host and later receives auth-adjacent callbacks.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Legitimate name services, imaging workflows, or management platforms.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when local name-resolution traffic is followed by unusual SMB or HTTP callbacks to the same host.
hunt_methods.title: T1557.001 LLMNR NBT-NS Responder Follow-On Hunt

# Arkime Evil Twin Portal Redirect Hunt for T1557.004 Evil Twin

Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((http.uri == /(?i)(portal|captive|guest|hotspot|wpad|wifi|wireless|login)/) || (host.http == /(?i)(portal|guest|wifi|wireless)/))
What the search isolates: Candidate captive-portal or rogue-wireless mediation traffic that may reflect an evil-twin-style lure.

- Confirm one external destination is repeatedly used for similarly sized outbound transfers.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Backups, integration jobs, or sanctioned sync platforms.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a rare external collector receives repeated automated-looking transfers after suspicious prep activity.

# Arkime T1048 External Non-Web Protocol Egress Hunt for T1048 Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol
Technique Context
- Review mid-to-large outbound transfers over non-web or host-role-mismatched protocols, then validate against business transfer baselines.
Role baseline: <EXPECTED_HOST_ROLE>
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_EXTERNAL_SCOPE>) && bytes >= <MID_TRANSFER_BYTES> && (protocols == ftp || protocols == ssh || protocols == smb || protocols == nfs || port.dst == 21 || port.dst == 22 || port.dst == 25 || port.dst == 587 || port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 873 || port.dst == 1194 || port.dst == 51820)
What the search isolates: External transfer sessions where protocol, port, destination, and host role should be reviewed as an alternate-protocol egress path.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, protocols, port.dst, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, dns.host, host.http, ja3
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source, protocol, and destination to find rare egress paths.
2. Compare the protocol against the source host role and approved partner or backup workflows.
3. Pivot into earlier collection, staging, archive movement, and later repeated transfer sessions.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the transfer is outbound, unusual for the host, and not an approved business or admin workflow.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Backups, SFTP, partner file exchange, VPNs, storage appliances, and sanctioned remote administration.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when rare external transfer paths follow suspicious staging or recur outside approved workflows.
hunt_methods.title: T1048 External Non-Web Protocol Egress Hunt

hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Redirect Chain To Retrieval Hunt for T1189 Drive-by Compromise
Technique Context
- Review browser-like traffic that hits redirect-heavy or lure-like content and then quickly retrieves a larger object or shifts to a new destination.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && http.method == GET && http.user-agent == /(?i)(mozilla|chrome|edge|safari|firefox)/ && (http.uri == /(?i)(redirect|click|go|download|update|install)/ || bytes >= <PAYLOAD_RETRIEVAL_BYTES>)
What the search isolates: Candidate drive-by chains where the lure, redirect, and retrieval are closer together than ordinary browsing behavior.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, http.user-agent, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether browser traffic hits redirect-like paths and then quickly retrieves a large object or new destination.
2. Pivot into later C2, DNS, or remote-access traffic from the same host.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the same browser workflow moves from lure or redirect content into retrieval or new infrastructure.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Software downloads, legitimate redirectors, or web advertising.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when redirect-like browser traffic quickly leads to retrieval and later operator-style network behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1189 Redirect Chain To Retrieval Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify tiny trigger sessions that precede sustained access or C2 behavior.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface traffic-signaling candidates that can be validated through follow-on session transitions.
prerequisites: The hunt scope includes enough adjacent traffic to review what follows a small signal.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime excels at pivoting from a tiny signal into the next session that gives it meaning.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Signal Then Session Transition Hunt for T1205 Traffic Signaling
Technique Context
- Start from tiny signals and then validate whether the same source or destination transitions into sustained communication shortly afterward.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && bytes <= <TINY_SIGNAL_BYTES> && packets <= <LOW_PACKET_THRESHOLD> && (port.dst >= 1024 || port.dst == 80 || port.dst == 443)
What the search isolates: Low-volume trigger candidates that must be expanded into adjacent sessions to prove signaling behavior.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Sort by firstSeen and group by source, destination, and port to find repeated tiny signals.
2. Pivot forward from the same source and destination into the next minutes or hours for sustained sessions, callbacks, or protocol changes.
3. Treat the tiny signal as a lead only when it is adjacent to a meaningful access or C2 transition.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the signal is patterned and directly precedes a larger or more interactive session.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Health checks, scanners, connection tests, keepalives, and failed handshakes.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a patterned tiny signal reliably precedes new service access, tasking, or callback behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1205 Signal Then Session Transition Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify remote-service abuse that shifts from setup traffic into sustained access.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface remote-service transitions from exploit-like setup into sustained access.

hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Exploit-to-Interactive Session Hunt for T1210 Exploitation of Remote Services
Technique Context
- Review service exposure traffic that evolves from short exploit-like attempts into an obviously interactive or persistent session.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 22 || port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 3389 || port.dst == 5985 || port.dst == 5986)
What the search isolates: Candidate remote-service abuse where the same source and target move from brief setup traffic into a sustained session.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Review the source-target timeline instead of single sessions and note where the session profile changes.
2. Pivot into later tool transfer, tunneling, or outbound behavior from the target after the transition.
Validation steps:
- Confirm one source-target pair transitions from short setup traffic into an interactive or persistent session.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Legitimate retries, admin access, or monitoring checks.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when the remote-service transition is rare for the target and leads to immediate post-access activity.
hunt_methods.title: T1210 Exploit-to-Interactive Session Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify role-inappropriate external web uploads.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface external web uploads that stand out because the host role should not perform them.
prerequisites: Relevant outbound web-service traffic is visible in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because directionality and host-role mismatch make large web uploads much more meaningful in Arkime.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime External Web Upload Role Mismatch Hunt for T1567 Exfiltration Over Web Service
Technique Context
- Review large outbound web uploads where the host role should not be pushing meaningful data to external web services.
Role baseline: <EXPECTED_HOST_ROLE>
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_EXTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (protocols == http || protocols == tls) && bytes >= <MID_TRANSFER_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Large external web-service sessions that are suspicious because of the host role and destination, not just the transfer size.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Start with the host role and ask whether the endpoint should upload to external web services at all.
2. Pivot into specific repository, cloud-storage, or collaboration patterns if the destination family matches one of them.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the external web upload is rare for the host role and not an approved workflow.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- SaaS sync, API integrations, or browser-driven uploads.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a role-inappropriate host uploads to rare external web services after suspicious staging or access activity.
hunt_methods.title: T1567 External Web Upload Role Mismatch Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify suspicious push-like communication to external code repositories.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface repository push behavior that is unusual for the host role and data volume.
prerequisites: HTTP method and host metadata are retained for repository traffic in scope.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can isolate repository push-style traffic and pair it with role mismatch and transfer size.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.

# Arkime Repository Push Delta Hunt for T1567.001 Exfiltration to Code Repository
Technique Context
- Review external repository traffic where the source host pushes materially more data or uses repository APIs it does not normally touch.
Role baseline: <EXPECTED_HOST_ROLE>
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_EXTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (host.http == /(?i)(github|gitlab|bitbucket|azuredevops)/) && (http.method == POST || http.method == PUT || bytes >= <MID_TRANSFER_BYTES>)
What the search isolates: Candidate repository exfiltration where push-style behavior and destination family matter more than a simple domain IOC.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, http.method, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the host role normally performs repository pushes or API uploads to the destination family.
2. Pivot into staging, archive movement, or unusual repo object paths before escalating.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source performs push-like or high-volume repository communication that is rare for the host role.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Developer workflows, CI runners, or sanctioned automation.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a non-developer or otherwise unusual host pushes material data into external repository infrastructure.
hunt_methods.title: T1567.001 Repository Push Delta Hunt

hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Cloud Sync Burst Hunt for T1567.002 Exfiltration to Cloud Storage
Technique Context
- Review uploads to cloud-storage destinations where the source host rarely uses the service family or the volume arrives in suspicious bursts.
Role baseline: <EXPECTED_HOST_ROLE>
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_EXTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (host.http == /(?i)(dropbox|onedrive|sharepoint|box|blob|s3|storage|gdrive|googleapis)/) && bytes >= <MID_TRANSFER_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Candidate cloud-storage exfiltration where bursty upload volume and destination family matter more than one domain hit.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the host role should upload to the cloud-storage family in question.
2. Pivot into staging, archive transfer, or remote-access activity immediately before the burst.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the upload volume or burstiness is unusual for the host and storage destination.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Legitimate cloud backup, sync, or user file sharing.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a host that should not use cloud storage pushes significant outbound volume after suspicious prep activity.

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify DNS lookups followed by rotating infrastructure use.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface DNS-driven destination rotation rather than one stable resolution path.

hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Resolution-to-Rotation Hunt for T1568 Dynamic Resolution
Technique Context
- Review domains that are followed by communication to changing infrastructure instead of a stable destination set.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && protocols == dns
What the search isolates: DNS lookups that can be pivoted into follow-on sessions to reveal destination rotation over time.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, dns.host, ip.dst, firstSeen, lastSeen, bytes, packets
Session-review workflow:
1. Start with the domain and review whether the same source later talks to changing infrastructure after the lookup.
2. Pivot into C2, upload, or tunneling behavior tied to the rotated destination set.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the same lookup is followed by destination rotation rather than one stable service path.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- CDNs, cloud load balancing, and modern web platforms.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when rotating destinations are tied to rare domains and stable operator-like behavior from the same host.
hunt_methods.title: T1568 Resolution-to-Rotation Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify domain-driven destination churn consistent with fast-flux behavior.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface fast-flux-like destination expansion tied to one domain or infrastructure family.

hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Flux Set Expansion Hunt for T1568.001 Fast Flux DNS
Technique Context
- Review domains that appear to fan out into unusually broad or fast-changing destination sets for one host or host group.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && protocols == dns && bytes <= <DNS_SESSION_BYTES>
What the search isolates: DNS activity that can be regrouped into fast-changing infrastructure sets rather than a normal fixed service path.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, dns.host, ip.dst, firstSeen, lastSeen, packets, bytes
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by dns.host and review how many destinations or infrastructure families are touched after the lookups.
2. Pivot into later web or TLS sessions to determine whether the rotation acts like rendezvous infrastructure.
Validation steps:
- Confirm one domain appears to steer traffic into a broad or rapidly changing set of destinations.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- CDNs, resilient cloud services, or large distributed platforms.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when fast-changing destination sets are tied to rare domains and operator-like follow-on behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1568.001 Flux Set Expansion Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify repeated rare domain patterns consistent with DGA-style retry behavior.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface repeated structurally similar domain retries that may indicate DGA behavior.
prerequisites: DNS parser fields are retained and the scope includes the host's retry behavior over time.

hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime DGA Retry Drift Hunt for T1568.002 Domain Generation Algorithms
Technique Context
- Review hosts generating repeated long or odd-looking domain patterns that drift from one name to another while preserving similar structure.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && protocols == dns && dns.host == /([a-z0-9-]{12,}\.){2,}/
What the search isolates: Candidate algorithmic domain behavior where one host repeatedly tries structurally similar names instead of normal business domains.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, dns.host, ip.dst, firstSeen, lastSeen, packets, bytes
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and inspect whether the host cycles through similarly structured domains.
2. Pivot into any successful follow-on communication that emerges after the retry pattern.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the host repeatedly uses similar-looking rare domains rather than ordinary service names.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- CDN-generated names, telemetry systems, or auto-generated SaaS subdomains.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when structurally similar rare domains are tied to later successful control or transfer sessions.
hunt_methods.title: T1568.002 DGA Retry Drift Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify stable reuse of unusual protocol-port combinations.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface repeated destination reuse over unusual protocol-port pairings.
prerequisites: Protocol parser fields are retained for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because destination reuse is often what turns an odd port into a stable malicious channel.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Unusual Port Destination Reuse Hunt for T1571 Non-Standard Port
Technique Context
- Focus on the same rare destination being reused on the same unusual port instead of treating one odd port in isolation.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((protocols == http && port.dst != 80 && port.dst != 443 && port.dst != 8080 && port.dst != 8443) || (protocols == tls && port.dst != 443 && port.dst != 8443) || (protocols == dns && port.dst != 53))
What the search isolates: Protocol-port mismatches that become more suspicious when one destination or infrastructure family is reused over time.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, protocols, port.dst, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, ja3
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by destination and port to find stable reuse of the same unusual pairing.
2. Pivot into earlier and later sessions to see whether the pairing is part of a persistent control or exfil path.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the unusual port pairing recurs to the same destination family and is not an approved app pattern.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Custom enterprise services, proxies, or alternate listeners.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a rare destination repeatedly uses the same protocol-port mismatch in a stable operational cadence.
hunt_methods.title: T1571 Unusual Port Destination Reuse Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify encrypted channels that grow from small check-ins into meaningful transfer.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface encrypted paths that grow from small check-ins into larger operational traffic.

hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show one encrypted path progressing from beacon-like use into larger operational traffic.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Encrypted Channel Growth Hunt for T1573 Encrypted Channel
Technique Context
- Review encrypted destinations that begin as small repeated sessions and later grow into materially larger traffic.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (protocols == tls || port.dst == 22 || port.dst == 1194 || port.dst == 51820 || port.dst == 4500)
What the search isolates: Candidate encrypted channels where progression matters more than any single session size.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, protocols, port.dst, ja3, cert.subject.cn, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by destination and review whether the same encrypted path grows from check-ins into larger transfers.
2. Pivot into staging, tunneling, or exfiltration behavior before concluding the growth is suspicious.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the same encrypted path is reused across both low-volume and higher-volume phases.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Normal SaaS, sync, or secure admin traffic.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a rare encrypted path clearly progresses from small repeated sessions into material transfer or operator support activity.
hunt_methods.title: T1573 Encrypted Channel Growth Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify DCOM/RPC remote-service use that is rare for the source host or adjacent to suspicious lateral movement.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface unusual RPC/DCOM remote-service sessions that may represent lateral movement.
prerequisites: DCE/RPC or RPC endpoint-mapper traffic is visible for the scoped internal segment.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can identify RPC/DCOM session paths and pivot them into surrounding SMB, WinRM, RDP, and transfer behavior.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime DCOM RPC Remote Service Hunt for T1021.003 Distributed Component Object Model
Technique Context
- Review internal DCE/RPC and endpoint-mapper sessions that may represent DCOM-based remote execution or lateral movement.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 135 || protocols == dcerpc) && packets > <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS>
What the search isolates: Candidate RPC/DCOM remote-service sessions that need validation against approved admin, management, and application workflows.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the source normally initiates RPC/DCOM traffic to the destination.
2. Pivot into adjacent SMB, WinRM, RDP, and high-port RPC sessions from the same source.
3. Compare timing against prior discovery, credential abuse, or lateral transfer behavior.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the RPC/DCOM path is rare for the host role or follows suspicious discovery/staging.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Systems management, endpoint agents, domain administration, backup agents, and Windows service control workflows.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when rare RPC/DCOM traffic appears from a workstation or unmanaged source and is paired with staging, SMB transfer, or other remote-service activity.
hunt_methods.title: T1021.003 DCOM RPC Remote Service Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify CDN or cloud-fronted channels that may proxy C2 traffic through trusted infrastructure.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface CDN or cloud-fronted web sessions that may conceal proxied command and control.
prerequisites: HTTP hostnames, TLS certificate metadata, JA3, or DNS hostnames are retained for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Supporting pivot because Arkime can surface frontable infrastructure and metadata mismatch leads, but true domain-fronting confirmation often requires packet detail or TLS/HTTP host comparison.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Domain Fronting Candidate Hunt for T1090.004 Domain Fronting
Technique Context
- Review web/TLS sessions that use CDN or cloud-fronted infrastructure where hostnames, certificate values, JA3, and destination reuse may indicate fronted C2.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (protocols == http || protocols == http2 || protocols == tls) && bytes <= <SMALL_BEACON_BYTES> && (host.http == /(?i)(cloudfront\.net|azureedge\.net|cloudflare|fastly|akamai|appspot\.com|workers\.dev|pages\.dev|cdn)/ || cert.subject.cn == /(?i)(cloudfront|azureedge|cloudflare|fastly|akamai|appspot|cdn)/)
What the search isolates: Low-volume sessions to CDN or cloud-fronted surfaces that require review for host/certificate mismatch, repeated tasking, or suspicious path structure.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, host.http, dns.host, http.uri, http.user-agent, ja3, cert.subject.cn, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source, CDN/fronting provider, JA3, and certificate values to identify repeated small channels.
2. Inspect HTTP host, URI structure, certificate common name, and packet detail for mismatch or fronted routing indicators.
3. Pivot into adjacent payload retrieval, exfiltration, or fallback channels from the same source.
Validation steps:
- Confirm repeated low-volume traffic to frontable infrastructure with suspicious metadata or host/certificate mismatch.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Normal CDN-backed websites, SaaS APIs, browser traffic, update infrastructure, and cloud applications.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when CDN/fronted sessions are rare for the host, recur like tasking, and lead to staging or egress behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1090.004 Domain Fronting Candidate Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify exfiltration candidates to paste, gist, note, and text-hosting services.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface uploads or larger transfers to text storage services that can receive staged exfiltration data.
prerequisites: HTTP hostnames or DNS lookups for public text storage services are retained in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can isolate text-storage service use, HTTP methods, transfer size, source rarity, and surrounding staging sessions.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Text Storage Site Exfil Hunt for T1567.003 Exfiltration to Text Storage Sites
Technique Context
- Review outbound uploads or larger transfers to paste, gist, note, and text-hosting services that can receive staged data through normal web traffic.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_EXTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (protocols == http || protocols == http2 || protocols == tls) && (host.http == /(?i)(pastebin|rentry|hastebin|ghostbin|paste\.rs|paste\.ee|controlc|gist\.github|githubusercontent|notion|telegra\.ph)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(pastebin|rentry|hastebin|ghostbin|paste\.rs|paste\.ee|controlc|gist\.github|githubusercontent|notion|telegra\.ph)/) && ((http.method == POST || http.method == PUT) || bytes >= <MID_TRANSFER_BYTES>)
What the search isolates: Web sessions to text storage services that may carry uploaded notes, encoded blobs, credentials, or staged exfiltration data.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, dns.host, http.uri, http.method, http.user-agent, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the source host or user normally posts to text storage, gist, or note-sharing services.
2. Pivot backward into staging, archive, clipboard, browser, or tasking activity from the same host.
3. Pivot across the same service account/path or destination to find other affected hosts.
Validation steps:
- Confirm POST/PUT behavior, larger uploads, rare source-service relationship, or suspicious encoded path/content metadata.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Developer gists, documentation, support notes, browser use, and normal collaboration workflows.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when text-storage uploads are rare for the host, follow staging or collection, or recur with suspicious encoded paths.
hunt_methods.title: T1567.003 Text Storage Site Exfil Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify exfiltration candidates over webhooks and chat/collaboration API receivers.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface outbound webhook deliveries that may carry staged exfiltration data through collaboration or automation services.
prerequisites: HTTP hostnames, DNS lookups, or URI metadata for webhook services are retained in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can identify webhook service use, POST-like behavior, transfer size, source rarity, and surrounding staging sessions.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Webhook Exfil Hunt for T1567.004 Exfiltration Over Webhook
Technique Context
- Review outbound POST-like traffic to webhook endpoints and chat/collaboration APIs that can receive staged data or alerts from attacker tooling.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_EXTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (protocols == http || protocols == http2 || protocols == tls) && (host.http == /(?i)(hooks\.slack\.com|discord\.com|discordapp\.com|api\.telegram\.org|webhook\.site|maker\.ifttt\.com|teams\.microsoft\.com|graph\.microsoft\.com|chat\.googleapis\.com)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(hooks\.slack\.com|discord\.com|discordapp\.com|api\.telegram\.org|webhook\.site|maker\.ifttt\.com|teams\.microsoft\.com|graph\.microsoft\.com|chat\.googleapis\.com)/ || http.uri == /(?i)(webhook|api\/webhooks|bot[0-9A-Za-z_:-]+|sendDocument|sendMessage)/) && ((http.method == POST || http.method == PUT) || bytes >= <MID_TRANSFER_BYTES>)
What the search isolates: Sessions to webhook-style receivers where outbound data may be delivered through allowed collaboration or automation services.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, dns.host, http.uri, http.method, http.user-agent, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Determine whether the source normally sends automation or chat webhook traffic.
2. Review URI families for webhook identifiers, bot endpoints, or API paths, avoiding exposure of secrets in notes.
3. Pivot backward into staging or collection and forward into repeated webhook delivery sessions.
Validation steps:
- Confirm unusual POST/PUT behavior to a webhook receiver, repeated deliveries, or transfer after staging/collection.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- CI/CD notifications, monitoring alerts, SOAR playbooks, developer automation, and collaboration integrations.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when webhook traffic is new for the host, carries larger payloads, or follows suspicious staging and collection behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1567.004 Webhook Exfil Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify unusual access to VPN, bastion, remote desktop web, and gateway services.
