prerequisites: HTTP hostnames, DNS lookups, or TLS certificate metadata for remote access gateways are retained in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can identify remote-service gateway access and correlate it with source rarity and surrounding activity.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime VPN And Gateway Hostname Hunt for T1133 External Remote Services
Technique Context
- Review access to VPN, remote desktop web, bastion, Citrix, and gateway hostnames from sources that do not normally use external remote services.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_EXTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (host.http == /(?i)(vpn|ssl-vpn|globalprotect|fortinet|fortigate|pulse|citrix|netscaler|rdweb|remote|bastion|jump|guacamole)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(vpn|ssl-vpn|globalprotect|fortinet|fortigate|pulse|citrix|netscaler|rdweb|remote|bastion|jump|guacamole)/)
What the search isolates: Remote-access gateway sessions that should be compared against user, host, time-of-day, and approved access baselines.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, dns.host, port.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, ja3, cert.subject.cn, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and remote-service hostname to find unusual or newly introduced access.
2. Pivot into adjacent authentication, cloud admin, staging, or exfiltration sessions from the same host.
3. Review certificate and JA3 reuse to find related gateway infrastructure.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source host or user is not expected to use the remote-service gateway.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Normal VPN, contractor access, helpdesk, vendor support, and administrator workflows.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when remote gateway access is new, rare, off-hours, or adjacent to suspicious payload, remote access, or egress behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1133 VPN And Gateway Hostname Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify network paths that may coerce hosts to authenticate to attacker-controlled infrastructure.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface forced-authentication candidates involving WPAD/proxy discovery and SMB callbacks.
prerequisites: Internal HTTP, DNS, and SMB session metadata are visible in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show WPAD/proxy requests, SMB callback paths, and convergence on suspicious internal destinations.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime WPAD Forced Authentication Hunt for T1187 Forced Authentication
Technique Context
- Review internal WPAD, proxy auto-config, and SMB follow-on traffic that can coerce authentication to attacker-controlled infrastructure.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && ((http.uri == /(?i)(wpad\.dat|proxy\.pac|autodiscover|file:|\\)/) || (dns.host == /(?i)(^wpad\.|wpad$)/) || (port.dst == 445 && packets <= <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS>))
What the search isolates: Forced-authentication leads where web/proxy discovery or SMB callbacks may expose credentials for relay or capture.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, dns.host, http.uri, port.dst, protocols, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by destination to identify unexpected WPAD/proxy or SMB callback collectors.
2. Pivot from WPAD/proxy requests into SMB or HTTP follow-on sessions to the same destination.
3. Compare destinations against legitimate proxy infrastructure and domain services.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the destination is not an approved proxy, file server, or domain service and that multiple clients are coerced.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Legitimate WPAD, proxy auto-discovery, file shares, printer services, and normal SMB authentication.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when WPAD/proxy or SMB callbacks converge on an unmanaged host or precede relay-style access attempts.
hunt_methods.title: T1187 WPAD Forced Authentication Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify mail-protocol tasking or polling from hosts that should not directly communicate with external mail services.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface unusual direct external mail-protocol use that could carry C2 tasking.
prerequisites: Mail-protocol session metadata or port-based session metadata is visible in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can isolate direct external mail-protocol paths and compare them to host role and cadence.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime External Mail Tasking Anomaly Hunt for T1071.003 Mail Protocols
Technique Context
- Review mail-protocol sessions from hosts that are not expected to speak directly to external SMTP, IMAP, or POP services.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_EXTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 25 || port.dst == 465 || port.dst == 587 || port.dst == 993 || port.dst == 995 || port.dst == 110 || port.dst == 143) && bytes <= <SMALL_BEACON_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Low-volume external mail-protocol sessions that may carry tasking, mailbox polling, or implant communications.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, dns.host, cert.subject.cn
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the source host should connect directly to external mail services instead of corporate mail relays.
2. Group by destination and timing to identify polling or tasking cadence.
3. Pivot into adjacent payload retrieval, credential access, or exfiltration sessions from the same source.
Validation steps:
- Confirm direct external mail access is unusual for the source host and recurs like tasking or polling.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Mail clients, mobile sync, legacy applications, printers, scanners, and approved SMTP integrations.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when non-mail hosts repeatedly use external mail protocols with stable timing or suspicious adjacent activity.
hunt_methods.title: T1071.003 External Mail Tasking Anomaly Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify non-application protocol control traffic that stands out from diagnostics or approved tunnels.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface repeated non-application protocol sessions that may act as control channels.
prerequisites: ICMP or GRE sessions are visible in Arkime for the scoped network path.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can isolate ICMP/GRE paths and evaluate repetition, byte stability, and surrounding-session context.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime ICMP Low-Volume Control Hunt for T1095 Non-Application Layer Protocol
Technique Context
- Review repeated ICMP or GRE sessions that are small, stable, and reused like a control channel instead of normal diagnostics.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (protocols == icmp || protocols == gre) && bytes <= <SMALL_BEACON_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Non-application protocol sessions that can carry control traffic or tunnel signaling with little application metadata.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and destination to separate repeated control-like channels from one-off diagnostics.
2. Compare timing, byte stability, and destination rarity against approved monitoring or VPN/tunnel use.
3. Pivot into adjacent encrypted, proxy, or data-transfer sessions from the same source.
Validation steps:
- Confirm repeated ICMP/GRE use to rare infrastructure and no approved diagnostic, tunnel, or monitoring reason.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Ping monitoring, traceroute, VPN tunnels, network devices, and diagnostics.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when non-application protocol traffic recurs to rare destinations and is adjacent to tasking, tunneling, or transfer behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1095 ICMP Low-Volume Control Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify internal host enumeration through short reachability and name-discovery sessions.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface host-discovery behavior that maps reachable internal systems before lateral movement.
prerequisites: Arkime sees east-west discovery traffic and session metadata for the scoped internal segment.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show one-to-many internal discovery patterns and their transition into follow-on access.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Internal Remote System Discovery Hunt for T1018 Remote System Discovery
Technique Context
- Review internal host-discovery patterns such as ping-like reachability checks, NetBIOS/SMB discovery, DNS lookups, and short sessions to many peers.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && packets <= <LOW_PACKET_THRESHOLD> && bytes <= <SCAN_BYTES_THRESHOLD> && (protocols == icmp || port.dst == 53 || port.dst == 137 || port.dst == 138 || port.dst == 139 || port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 135)
What the search isolates: Short internal discovery conversations that can reveal a source attempting to map reachable systems rather than using normal peer relationships.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, protocols, port.dst, packets, bytes, dns.host, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by ip.src and destination count to identify sources touching many internal systems in a compressed window.
2. Separate approved scanners, monitoring systems, and domain infrastructure from user workstations or newly compromised hosts.
3. Pivot into later SMB, RDP, WinRM, SSH, or transfer sessions from the same source to see whether discovery led to access.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source is enumerating a broad or unusual internal peer set and not only resolving normal business dependencies.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Asset inventory, vulnerability scanning, monitoring probes, login scripts, and domain discovery by legitimate management tools.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a workstation or unmanaged source performs broad internal discovery and then moves into remote services, staging, or outbound tasking.
hunt_methods.title: T1018 Internal Remote System Discovery Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify internal network share enumeration and follow-on access.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface share-discovery behavior that can precede collection or lateral tool transfer.
prerequisites: SMB, NetBIOS, or directory-service session metadata is visible for the scoped segment.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show internal SMB and directory fan-out plus the later transition into high-byte share access.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Network Share Discovery Hunt for T1135 Network Share Discovery
Technique Context
- Review SMB, NetBIOS, and directory-service conversations that look like share discovery across many internal hosts.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 139 || port.dst == 137 || port.dst == 389 || port.dst == 636) && packets <= <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS> && bytes <= <SMALL_BEACON_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Candidate network-share enumeration where a source queries many hosts or directory services with short, low-payload sessions.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by ip.src, then by ip.dst and port.dst to find broad SMB or directory-service discovery.
2. Pivot into later high-byte SMB sessions from the same source to determine whether discovered shares were accessed or copied from.
3. Compare the source against file servers, backup systems, admin workstations, and vulnerability scanners.
Validation steps:
- Confirm repeated discovery-style sessions to multiple internal hosts before any large file or tool transfer.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Login scripts, inventory tools, file-indexing agents, backup discovery, and administrative share mapping.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when non-admin endpoints enumerate shares and then access unusual file servers or stage data.
hunt_methods.title: T1135 Network Share Discovery Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify suspicious collection from internal network shares.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface high-volume internal file-share access that may represent collection from shared drives.
prerequisites: Internal file-service traffic is visible in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show file-service path, volume, timing, and surrounding discovery or exfiltration context.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Network Shared Drive Collection Hunt for T1039 Data from Network Shared Drive
Technique Context
- Review large internal SMB, NFS, or file-service sessions where a source pulls data from network shares outside its normal role.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 139 || port.dst == 2049 || port.dst == 111) && bytes >= <MID_TRANSFER_BYTES>
What the search isolates: High-volume access to network file services that can reflect collection from shared drives before staging or exfiltration.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and file-service destination to find unusual large reads or repeated share access.
2. Pivot into earlier share-discovery sessions and later staging or outbound transfer from the same source.
3. Compare against normal backup, file-sync, and software distribution patterns for that subnet and host role.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source does not normally pull this volume from the destination and the access aligns with suspicious discovery or staging.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Backup, endpoint migration, large project file access, software deployment, and user-driven file copy operations.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when unusual shared-drive access is followed by archive staging, cloud upload, C2 egress, or remote-service movement.
hunt_methods.title: T1039 Network Shared Drive Collection Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify centralized internal staging before outbound transfer.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface remote staging behavior where data is consolidated internally before exfiltration.
prerequisites: Internal transfer paths and later outbound sessions from candidate staging hosts are visible in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can reveal convergence of large internal transfers and the later egress path from the staging host.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Remote Data Staging Hunt for T1074.002 Remote Data Staging
Technique Context
- Review internal transfers that consolidate data onto a central host, file service, web staging point, or temporary relay before exfiltration.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && bytes >= <LARGE_TRANSFER_BYTES> && (port.dst == 22 || port.dst == 80 || port.dst == 443 || port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 873 || port.dst == 2049 || port.dst == 8080 || port.dst == 8443)
What the search isolates: Large internal consolidation flows that may represent remote staging prior to external transfer.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, host.http, http.uri, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by staging destination to identify one internal host receiving data from many peers or receiving unusually large transfers.
2. Pivot into later outbound sessions from the staging host to cloud storage, web services, proxy relays, or uncommon external infrastructure.
3. Review earlier collection behavior from the same sources, especially SMB/NFS reads and database or repository downloads.
Validation steps:
- Confirm data converges on a host that is not an expected backup, file server, package cache, or migration target.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Backups, software distribution, file migrations, build artifacts, replication, and data warehouse ingestion.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when internal consolidation is followed by external egress, proxying, or tasking from the staging destination.
hunt_methods.title: T1074.002 Remote Data Staging Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify remote email collection through mail protocols and mail-service APIs.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface high-volume remote mailbox collection paths that deserve identity and endpoint validation.
prerequisites: Mail service traffic, HTTP hostnames, or DNS lookups are retained for the scoped traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show mailbox service access volume, service endpoints, and adjacent staging or egress.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Remote Email Collection Hunt for T1114.002 Remote Email Collection
Technique Context
- Review large or repeated mailbox-access sessions over IMAP, POP3, Exchange, Gmail, Outlook, or Graph endpoints from hosts that do not normally collect mail at scale.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((port.dst == 143 || port.dst == 993 || port.dst == 110 || port.dst == 995) || host.http == /(?i)(outlook\.office365\.com|graph\.microsoft\.com|mail\.google\.com|imap\.gmail\.com|exchange|ews|owa)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(outlook\.office365\.com|graph\.microsoft\.com|mail\.google\.com|imap\.gmail\.com|exchange|ews|owa)/) && bytes >= <MID_TRANSFER_BYTES>
What the search isolates: High-volume remote mail access that may represent mailbox collection rather than normal interactive mail use.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, host.http, dns.host, http.uri, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source, destination, and mail service to identify repeated or high-volume mailbox access.
2. Compare the source against expected mail clients, eDiscovery systems, journaling servers, and security tooling.
3. Pivot into later cloud upload, archive transfer, or C2 sessions from the same source.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the access volume, timing, or source host is abnormal for mail usage and aligns with suspicious identity or endpoint context.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Mobile mail sync, desktop mail cache rebuilds, eDiscovery exports, journaling, mail migration, and backup jobs.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when rare or high-volume remote mail access is followed by staging or egress from the same host.
hunt_methods.title: T1114.002 Remote Email Collection Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify bulk collection from information repositories.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface suspicious bulk access to information repositories.
prerequisites: HTTP hostnames or DNS lookups for repository platforms are retained.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can identify repository destinations, access volume, and follow-on staging or egress.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Information Repository Bulk Access Hunt for T1213 Data from Information Repositories
Technique Context
- Review bulk access to collaboration, documentation, code, messaging, and repository services from hosts that do not normally mine those sources.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (host.http == /(?i)(confluence|jira|sharepoint|github|gitlab|bitbucket|dev\.azure\.com|slack|teams|box|dropbox|notion|atlassian)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(confluence|jira|sharepoint|github|gitlab|bitbucket|dev\.azure\.com|slack|teams|box|dropbox|notion|atlassian)/) && bytes >= <MID_TRANSFER_BYTES>
What the search isolates: High-volume access to information repositories that may reflect collection of documentation, source code, tickets, or sensitive project data.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, dns.host, http.uri, http.user-agent, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and repository family to identify unusual bulk access or many repository destinations.
2. Review user-agent and URI patterns for scripted access, exports, downloads, archive retrieval, or API scraping.
3. Pivot into later archive staging, cloud upload, or C2 egress from the same source.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source, user segment, and repository family do not align with normal engineering, knowledge-management, or business workflows.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Normal engineering activity, document sync, browser cache rebuilds, CI/CD jobs, backup exports, and approved repository migrations.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when repository bulk access is rare for the host, scripted, or followed by staging or external transfer.
hunt_methods.title: T1213 Information Repository Bulk Access Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify SharePoint repository collection from unusual hosts.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface suspicious bulk collection from SharePoint or Microsoft 365 repositories.
prerequisites: HTTP hostnames or DNS lookups for Microsoft 365 services are retained.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can isolate Microsoft repository endpoints, session volume, and surrounding staging or egress.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime SharePoint Bulk Repository Access Hunt for T1213.002 Sharepoint
Technique Context
- Review large or scripted access to SharePoint and Microsoft 365 document repositories from unusual endpoints.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (host.http == /(?i)(sharepoint\.com|sharepoint|graph\.microsoft\.com|office\.com|microsoftonline\.com)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(sharepoint\.com|sharepoint|graph\.microsoft\.com|office\.com|microsoftonline\.com)/) && bytes >= <MID_TRANSFER_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Candidate SharePoint or Microsoft 365 repository collection, especially large downloads, exports, or scripted Graph access.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, dns.host, http.uri, http.user-agent, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and Microsoft repository endpoint to identify rare hosts performing bulk access.
2. Review HTTP URI and user-agent values for Graph API access, sync clients, export paths, or scripted download patterns.
3. Pivot into later archive staging, cloud upload, or non-Microsoft egress from the same source.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source normally should not perform high-volume SharePoint or Graph repository access.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- OneDrive/SharePoint sync, document migrations, eDiscovery, DLP tooling, backup, and normal user bulk downloads.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when SharePoint bulk access originates from an unusual endpoint or is followed by staging or external transfer.
hunt_methods.title: T1213.002 SharePoint Bulk Repository Access Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify code repository collection from unusual hosts.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface suspicious code repository collection or export behavior.
prerequisites: HTTP hostnames, DNS lookups, or Git port sessions are visible in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can identify code-hosting endpoints, clone/export volume, and follow-on staging or egress.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Code Repository Clone And Export Hunt for T1213.003 Code Repositories
Technique Context
- Review large or repeated access to GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, Azure DevOps, and raw code-content endpoints from unusual hosts.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (host.http == /(?i)(github\.com|githubusercontent\.com|gitlab|bitbucket|dev\.azure\.com|visualstudio\.com)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(github\.com|githubusercontent\.com|gitlab|bitbucket|dev\.azure\.com|visualstudio\.com)/ || port.dst == 9418) && bytes >= <MID_TRANSFER_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Candidate repository cloning, archive export, raw source retrieval, or scripted source-code collection.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, dns.host, http.uri, http.user-agent, port.dst, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and code-hosting provider to identify unusual clone/export volume.
2. Inspect URI and user-agent values for archive downloads, API calls, raw content, or automated clients.
3. Pivot into later compression, staging, cloud upload, or C2 sessions from the same source.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the host, user segment, and time window do not align with normal developer, CI/CD, or build activity.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Developer activity, CI/CD pipelines, dependency fetches, package mirrors, and approved code migrations.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when non-developer endpoints or rare automation hosts perform large code-repository pulls and later move data externally.
hunt_methods.title: T1213.003 Code Repository Clone And Export Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify bulk database repository collection from unusual sources.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface suspicious high-volume access to database repositories.
prerequisites: Database traffic to the relevant segments is visible in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show database destination, volume, timing, and later transfer behavior even without query contents.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Database Repository Bulk Access Hunt for T1213.006 Databases
Technique Context
- Review large sessions to database services from hosts that do not normally query or export those repositories.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 1433 || port.dst == 1521 || port.dst == 3306 || port.dst == 5432 || port.dst == 6379 || port.dst == 9200 || port.dst == 9300 || port.dst == 27017 || port.dst == 9042) && bytes >= <MID_TRANSFER_BYTES>
What the search isolates: High-volume database access that may reflect repository collection, table export, index scraping, or dump activity.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and database destination to find unusual database access paths or unexpected clients.
2. Compare the source against application servers, ETL systems, admin workstations, and backup infrastructure.
3. Pivot into later file staging, cloud upload, or external transfer from the same source or database host.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the database path, volume, source host, or timing is not expected for the application or data pipeline.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- ETL jobs, reporting, backups, replication, analytics queries, maintenance windows, and application batch jobs.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when unusual database bulk access comes from non-application hosts or is followed by staging and egress.
hunt_methods.title: T1213.006 Database Repository Bulk Access Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify suspicious retrieval from cloud storage services.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface large cloud-storage object access that may represent collection from cloud storage.
prerequisites: HTTP hostnames or DNS lookups for cloud storage services are retained.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can identify cloud storage destinations, transfer volume, and adjacent staging or egress.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Cloud Storage Data Access Hunt for T1530 Data from Cloud Storage
Technique Context
- Review large access to cloud object or file-storage services from hosts that do not normally retrieve or synchronize large cloud datasets.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (host.http == /(?i)(s3\.amazonaws\.com|\.s3[.-]|blob\.core\.windows\.net|file\.core\.windows\.net|storage\.googleapis\.com|storage\.cloud\.google\.com|digitaloceanspaces\.com|backblazeb2\.com)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(s3\.amazonaws\.com|\.s3[.-]|blob\.core\.windows\.net|file\.core\.windows\.net|storage\.googleapis\.com|storage\.cloud\.google\.com|digitaloceanspaces\.com|backblazeb2\.com)/) && bytes >= <LARGE_TRANSFER_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Large cloud-storage access that may represent collection from cloud storage objects or file shares.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, dns.host, http.uri, http.user-agent, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and storage provider to identify rare or high-volume cloud storage access.
2. Review URI, host, and user-agent for object downloads, bucket paths, sync clients, or scripted retrieval.
3. Pivot into identity, cloud audit, and endpoint context to determine whether the source is authorized to retrieve the objects.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source host and user role do not normally perform this cloud-storage retrieval or sync operation.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Backup restores, cloud sync clients, data science workloads, build artifacts, software updates, and approved storage migrations.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when large cloud-storage retrieval is rare for the source and followed by staging, C2, or secondary egress.
hunt_methods.title: T1530 Cloud Storage Data Access Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify cloud storage object enumeration from unusual sources.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface cloud storage listing behavior that can precede cloud object collection.
prerequisites: HTTP URI, hostnames, or DNS lookups for cloud storage services are retained.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can show storage API hosts, listing-style URIs, repetition, and later object retrieval.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Cloud Storage Object Discovery Hunt for T1619 Cloud Storage Object Discovery
Technique Context
- Review listing-style access to cloud storage APIs where a source enumerates buckets, containers, prefixes, or object metadata.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (host.http == /(?i)(s3\.amazonaws\.com|\.s3[.-]|blob\.core\.windows\.net|storage\.googleapis\.com|storage\.cloud\.google\.com)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(s3\.amazonaws\.com|\.s3[.-]|blob\.core\.windows\.net|storage\.googleapis\.com|storage\.cloud\.google\.com)/) && (http.uri == /(?i)(list-type=2|comp=list|prefix=|delimiter=|\/buckets|\/objects|marker=|continuation-token=)/ || bytes <= <SMALL_BEACON_BYTES>)
What the search isolates: Cloud-storage listing or enumeration behavior that may precede object collection or exfiltration.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, dns.host, http.uri, http.user-agent, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and storage account or bucket host to identify repeated listing-style requests.
2. Review whether listing sessions are followed by high-byte object retrieval from the same storage family.
3. Pivot into cloud audit and identity logs to validate the principal and API action.
Validation steps:
- Confirm repeated list/enumeration behavior from a source that does not normally administer or index storage objects.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Backup tools, sync clients, data cataloging, lifecycle jobs, cloud inventory, and approved administrative scripts.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when unusual cloud-storage enumeration is followed by object retrieval or external transfer.
hunt_methods.title: T1619 Cloud Storage Object Discovery Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify suspicious Kerberos service-ticket request bursts from unusual sources.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface Kerberos request bursts that may correspond to Kerberoasting and require KDC validation.
prerequisites: Kerberos traffic to domain controllers is visible in Arkime; KDC logs are available for validation.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Supporting pivot because Arkime can show Kerberos volume and timing, but KDC logs are needed to confirm Kerberoasting semantics.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Kerberoasting Network Pattern Hunt for T1558.003 Kerberoasting
Technique Context
- Review concentrated Kerberos service-ticket activity from a source to domain controllers, especially when followed by SMB, LDAP, or remote-service movement.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 88 || port.dst == 464) && packets <= <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS> && bytes >= <DNS_SESSION_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Kerberos-heavy sessions that may contain bursts of service-ticket requests worth validating with domain-controller logs.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by ip.src and domain-controller destination to identify unusual Kerberos request concentration.
2. Pivot into LDAP account or SPN discovery, SMB access, and later remote-service sessions from the same source.
3. Validate with KDC telemetry for TGS-REQ volume, encryption type, service principal diversity, and requesting account.
Validation steps:
- Confirm with domain-controller logs before treating Arkime Kerberos volume as Kerberoasting evidence.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Normal logon bursts, application service access, domain joins, patch cycles, and service account operations.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when Kerberos bursts are rare for the source and coincide with account discovery, SPN enumeration, or lateral movement.
hunt_methods.title: T1558.003 Kerberoasting Network Pattern Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify suspicious Kerberos authentication bursts from unusual sources.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface Kerberos authentication bursts that may correspond to AS-REP roasting and require KDC validation.

hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime AS-REP Roasting Network Pattern Hunt for T1558.004 AS-REP Roasting
Technique Context
- Review bursts of Kerberos AS-REQ/AS-REP-style traffic to domain controllers that may reflect enumeration of accounts without preauthentication.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && port.dst == 88 && packets <= <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS> && bytes >= <DNS_SESSION_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Concentrated Kerberos authentication exchanges that may align to AS-REP roasting attempts when validated with KDC logs.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and domain-controller destination to find compressed Kerberos bursts from unusual endpoints.
2. Pivot into LDAP account enumeration or later remote-service use from the same source.
3. Validate with domain-controller events for preauthentication-disabled account targeting and unusual request patterns.
Validation steps:
- Confirm with KDC logs; Arkime alone cannot prove the preauthentication flag or returned ticket details.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Normal user logons, boot storms, domain joins, SSO behavior, and application authentication bursts.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when Kerberos bursts occur from rare sources and KDC logs show AS-REP roasting indicators.
hunt_methods.title: T1558.004 AS-REP Roasting Network Pattern Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify rare internal RPC/DCOM sessions that may support WMI execution.

hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime WMI RPC Lateral Activity Hunt for T1047 Windows Management Instrumentation
Technique Context
- Review internal RPC/DCOM paths that can support WMI-based remote execution or management after discovery or credential access.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 135 || port.dst >= 49152) && packets > <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS>
What the search isolates: Candidate WMI/DCOM-style remote management sessions requiring correlation with endpoint or Windows event telemetry.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and destination to find rare RPC/DCOM management paths.
2. Pivot into earlier discovery, credential access, SMB transfer, or WinRM/RDP sessions from the same source.
3. Validate with endpoint logs for WMI process creation, remote command execution, or suspicious consumer/provider use.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source is not an expected management server and the RPC/DCOM activity aligns with endpoint evidence.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- SCCM, monitoring, backup agents, admin tools, vulnerability scanners, and normal Windows management traffic.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when rare RPC/DCOM paths appear after discovery or tool transfer and endpoint telemetry confirms WMI execution.

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify hosts using mining pools or bandwidth-proxy infrastructure.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface resource-hijacking traffic such as cryptomining or bandwidth proxy abuse.
prerequisites: DNS, HTTP, TLS, and session metadata are retained for outbound traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can directly show persistent connections to resource-abuse services and supporting payload-retrieval context.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Resource Hijacking Network Service Hunt for T1496 Resource Hijacking
Technique Context
- Review mining-pool, Stratum, bandwidth-proxy, and resource-abuse infrastructure use from endpoints or servers that should not participate in those services.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((port.dst == 3333 || port.dst == 4444 || port.dst == 5555 || port.dst == 7777 || port.dst == 14444 || port.dst == 18081) || host.http == /(?i)(stratum|xmr|monero|minexmr|supportxmr|nanopool|nicehash|2miners|ethermine|unmineable|proxyrack|iproyal|honeygain)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(stratum|xmr|monero|minexmr|supportxmr|nanopool|nicehash|2miners|ethermine|unmineable|proxyrack|iproyal|honeygain)/) && bytes >= <PROXY_SESSION_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Candidate resource-hijacking traffic such as cryptocurrency mining pools or bandwidth proxy networks.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, host.http, dns.host, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, ja3
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and destination family to identify hosts repeatedly using mining or bandwidth-sharing infrastructure.
2. Pivot into payload retrieval, container/cloud API access, and persistence-related traffic from the same source.
3. Compare against approved lab, research, or developer use before escalation.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the infrastructure family, destination reuse, and host role do not support legitimate mining, testing, or proxy use.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Security research, blockchain development, sanctioned mining labs, or user-installed bandwidth-sharing applications.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when unapproved hosts maintain persistent sessions to mining, stratum, or bandwidth-proxy infrastructure.
hunt_methods.title: T1496 Resource Hijacking Network Service Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify service-specific exhaustion floods.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface high-packet service-focused traffic that may exhaust a network service.
prerequisites: Arkime sees traffic to the service under investigation.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can directly show packet volume, target service, source distribution, and timing.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Service Exhaustion Flood Hunt for T1499.002 Service Exhaustion Flood
Technique Context
- Review high-packet floods against specific network services where traffic concentrates on one service port or host.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && packets >= <FLOOD_PACKET_THRESHOLD> && (port.dst == 21 || port.dst == 22 || port.dst == 25 || port.dst == 53 || port.dst == 80 || port.dst == 443 || port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 3389 || port.dst == 5985 || port.dst == 5986)
What the search isolates: High-packet service-focused traffic that can exhaust a target service rather than broadly saturating the network.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by ip.dst and port.dst to identify service-specific concentration.
2. Review source diversity, timing, and protocol mix to distinguish single-source abuse from distributed floods.
3. Pivot into target availability telemetry, server logs, and load-balancer/CDN records.
Validation steps:
- Confirm a service-specific traffic spike coincides with degraded service, elevated errors, or resource exhaustion.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Load tests, vulnerability scans, backup bursts, monitoring storms, retries after outages, and misconfigured clients.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when high-packet traffic concentrates on a service and coincides with availability degradation or repeated failure responses.
hunt_methods.title: T1499.002 Service Exhaustion Flood Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify application exhaustion through repeated high-cost HTTP workflows.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface application-layer request floods against expensive functions.
prerequisites: HTTP parser fields are retained for application traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can identify repeated costly URIs, source concentration, and packet volume at application layer.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Application Exhaustion Flood Hunt for T1499.003 Application Exhaustion Flood
Technique Context
- Review repeated HTTP requests to expensive application functions such as search, export, report generation, GraphQL, or download endpoints.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (protocols == http || protocols == http2) && packets >= <FLOOD_PACKET_THRESHOLD> && http.uri == /(?i)(search|export|report|generate|download|graphql|api\/.*(query|search|report|export)|render|preview)/
What the search isolates: HTTP sessions that may stress expensive application workflows instead of simply flooding a port.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, http.method, http.user-agent, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by host.http and http.uri to identify repeated expensive endpoints.
2. Compare user-agent, method, source count, and timing against known load tests or scraping jobs.
3. Pivot into application logs, WAF/CDN telemetry, and service health metrics.
Validation steps:
- Confirm repeated invocation of expensive endpoints coincides with elevated application errors, latency, or resource pressure.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Load testing, legitimate reporting jobs, indexing, crawlers, data exports, and user-driven bulk searches.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when repeated expensive application requests are unapproved and align with service degradation or abuse from rare sources.
hunt_methods.title: T1499.003 Application Exhaustion Flood Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify direct cloud VM access through cloud-native services.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface direct cloud VM connection paths from unexpected endpoints.

hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can identify cloud-native VM access endpoints and connect them to surrounding cloud activity.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Direct Cloud VM Connection Hunt for T1021.008 Direct Cloud VM Connections
Technique Context
- Review cloud-native VM access paths such as AWS SSM Session Manager, EC2 Instance Connect, Azure Serial Console, and bastion-style cloud access from unusual hosts.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (host.http == /(?i)(ssmmessages|ec2messages|ssm\.[a-z0-9-]+\.amazonaws\.com|ec2-instance-connect|serialconsole|bastion\.azure\.com|azurebastion|compute\.googleapis\.com)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(ssmmessages|ec2messages|ssm\.[a-z0-9-]+\.amazonaws\.com|ec2-instance-connect|serialconsole|bastion\.azure\.com|azurebastion|compute\.googleapis\.com)/) && packets > <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS>
What the search isolates: Interactive cloud-native VM access sessions that may bypass traditional SSH/RDP monitoring paths.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, dns.host, http.uri, ja3, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and cloud access service to identify unusual endpoints initiating direct VM sessions.
2. Pivot into cloud admin-plane access, storage access, and later VM egress from the same source or cloud environment.
3. Validate with cloud audit logs for SSM, serial console, bastion, or instance-connect events.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the cloud-native VM access is not an approved admin workflow and matches cloud audit evidence.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Cloud administration, break-glass access, DevOps troubleshooting, bastion workflows, and managed operations.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when direct cloud VM access originates from unusual endpoints or is adjacent to suspicious cloud discovery, staging, or egress.
hunt_methods.title: T1021.008 Direct Cloud VM Connection Hunt

# Arkime Cloud Service Discovery Hunt for T1526 Cloud Service Discovery
Technique Context
- Review access to cloud service inventory, identity, resource-management, and dashboard/API endpoints from endpoints that do not normally perform cloud discovery.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (host.http == /(?i)(management\.azure\.com|graph\.microsoft\.com|portal\.azure\.com|console\.aws\.amazon\.com|iam\.amazonaws\.com|sts\.amazonaws\.com|organizations\.amazonaws\.com|cloudresourcemanager\.googleapis\.com|serviceusage\.googleapis\.com|admin\.google\.com)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(management\.azure\.com|graph\.microsoft\.com|portal\.azure\.com|console\.aws\.amazon\.com|iam\.amazonaws\.com|sts\.amazonaws\.com|organizations\.amazonaws\.com|cloudresourcemanager\.googleapis\.com|serviceusage\.googleapis\.com|admin\.google\.com)/) && bytes <= <SMALL_BEACON_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Cloud service discovery and inventory access that may precede cloud resource abuse or data collection.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, dns.host, http.uri, http.user-agent, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and cloud provider endpoint to identify unusual inventory or admin-plane browsing.
2. Pivot into later cloud storage, VM access, code repository, or SaaS data access from the same source.
3. Validate with cloud audit logs for enumeration APIs, list actions, and principal identity.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source host and identity should not perform cloud service discovery or admin-plane enumeration.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Cloud administrators, DevOps tooling, CI/CD, posture-management tools, inventory systems, and browser-based admin work.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when cloud discovery originates from unusual endpoints and is followed by direct VM access, storage access, or data transfer.

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify cloud infrastructure enumeration from unusual sources.

# Arkime Cloud Infrastructure Discovery Hunt for T1580 Cloud Infrastructure Discovery
Technique Context
- Review access to compute, network, container, database, and load-balancer cloud APIs that may enumerate cloud infrastructure assets.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (host.http == /(?i)(ec2\.[a-z0-9-]+\.amazonaws\.com|elasticloadbalancing\.[a-z0-9-]+\.amazonaws\.com|autoscaling\.[a-z0-9-]+\.amazonaws\.com|rds\.[a-z0-9-]+\.amazonaws\.com|eks\.[a-z0-9-]+\.amazonaws\.com|management\.azure\.com|compute\.googleapis\.com|container\.googleapis\.com|cloudresourcemanager\.googleapis\.com)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(ec2\.[a-z0-9-]+\.amazonaws\.com|elasticloadbalancing\.[a-z0-9-]+\.amazonaws\.com|autoscaling\.[a-z0-9-]+\.amazonaws\.com|rds\.[a-z0-9-]+\.amazonaws\.com|eks\.[a-z0-9-]+\.amazonaws\.com|management\.azure\.com|compute\.googleapis\.com|container\.googleapis\.com|cloudresourcemanager\.googleapis\.com)/) && bytes <= <SMALL_BEACON_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Cloud infrastructure enumeration through provider APIs and consoles.

Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and cloud API family to identify unusual enumeration of compute, load balancer, database, or container resources.
2. Pivot into later direct cloud VM access, cloud storage access, or large transfers from the same source.
3. Validate with cloud audit logs for list/describe/enumerate API actions and the principal used.
Validation steps:

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify intermediate staging hosts receiving unusual data volume before later transfer or egress.

hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Data Staging Relay Hunt for T1074 Data Staged
Technique Context
- Look for internal systems accumulating large transfers before later relay, compression, cloud upload, or external egress.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && bytes >= <MID_TRANSFER_BYTES> && (port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 22 || port.dst == 80 || port.dst == 443 || port.dst == 8080 || port.dst == 8443)
What the search isolates: Candidate staging traffic where data moves to an intermediate internal host rather than directly to its final destination.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, host.http, http.uri, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by destination to find internal hosts receiving large transfers from unusual sources.
2. Pivot forward from the staging candidate into outbound cloud, web, FTP, SSH, SMB, or proxy sessions.
3. Pivot backward into discovery, share access, mailbox access, or repository access that may explain what was collected.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the host is not an expected file server, backup target, deployment share, or data-processing node.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Backup jobs, software deployment, file servers, package mirrors, and scheduled ETL flows.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when an unusual internal staging path is followed by remote-service use, archive retrieval, proxying, or external data movement.

# Arkime Bulk Mail Access Collection Hunt for T1114 Email Collection
Technique Context
- Review unusually large mailbox access over IMAP, POP, EWS, OWA, Gmail, or Graph-like mail endpoints.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && bytes >= <MID_TRANSFER_BYTES> && ((port.dst == 143 || port.dst == 993 || port.dst == 110 || port.dst == 995) || host.http == /(?i)(outlook|office365|office365\.com|graph\.microsoft\.com|outlook\.office|owa|ews|gmail|googleapis|mail)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(outlook|office365|graph\.microsoft\.com|outlook\.office|owa|ews|gmail|googleapis|mail)/)

Session-review workflow:

hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Directory Account Enumeration Hunt for T1087 Account Discovery
Technique Context
- Review directory-service access from hosts that do not normally enumerate accounts or identity objects.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (((<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 389 || port.dst == 636 || port.dst == 3268 || port.dst == 3269 || port.dst == 88 || port.dst == 464) && packets > <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS>) || (host.http == /(?i)(graph\.microsoft\.com|login\.microsoftonline\.com|admin\.google\.com)/ && http.uri == /(?i)(users|directory|members|servicePrincipals|groups)/))
What the search isolates: Candidate account-enumeration paths over LDAP, Global Catalog, Kerberos-adjacent traffic, or cloud directory APIs.

# Arkime Permission Group Enumeration Hunt for T1069 Permission Groups Discovery
