Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (((<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 389 || port.dst == 636 || port.dst == 3268 || port.dst == 3269) && packets > <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS>) || (host.http == /(?i)(graph\.microsoft\.com|admin\.google\.com|console\.aws\.amazon\.com|portal\.azure\.com)/ && http.uri == /(?i)(groups|members|roles|directoryRoles|iam|policies)/))
What the search isolates: Candidate group and role enumeration over LDAP, Global Catalog, or cloud identity APIs.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, host.http, http.uri, protocols, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and identity service to find endpoints with unusual group or role lookup behavior.
2. Pivot into later remote-service, cloud-admin, mailbox, repository, or storage access.
3. Compare against expected identity governance, security tooling, and admin workflows.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source host or user segment does not normally perform group or role enumeration.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- IAM governance, admin consoles, SSO tooling, EDR inventory, and compliance scans.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when group enumeration appears from a suspicious host and is followed by privileged cloud, domain, or remote-service access.
hunt_methods.title: T1069 Permission Group Enumeration Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify non-admin hosts performing unusual domain group discovery.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface domain group enumeration candidates over LDAP, GC, SMB, or RPC paths.
prerequisites: Traffic to domain controllers and directory-adjacent services is visible in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Supporting pivot because Arkime can surface suspicious network evidence, but host, identity, or application telemetry is needed to confirm the ATT&CK behavior.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Domain Group LDAP Enumeration Hunt for T1069.002 Domain Groups
Technique Context
- Look for non-admin hosts performing sustained LDAP or Global Catalog access that may enumerate domain groups and memberships.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 389 || port.dst == 636 || port.dst == 3268 || port.dst == 3269 || port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 135) && packets > <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS>
What the search isolates: Directory and SAMR/RPC-adjacent sessions that can support domain group discovery.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and destination domain controller or member server.
2. Pivot into later SMB admin-share, RDP, WinRM, Kerberos, or cloud admin sessions.
3. Suppress approved admin workstations, domain controllers, and identity management platforms.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source is not expected to query group membership at this volume or time.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Logon processing, GPO processing, AD tooling, EDR inventory, and normal admin scripts.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when unusual group discovery is followed by lateral movement, privilege abuse, or credential-access pivots.
hunt_methods.title: T1069.002 Domain Group LDAP Enumeration Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify hosts performing directory topology discovery that may precede cross-domain movement.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface domain trust and topology discovery candidates through directory-service traffic.
prerequisites: Domain controller and cross-domain directory traffic is visible in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Supporting pivot because Arkime can surface suspicious network evidence, but host, identity, or application telemetry is needed to confirm the ATT&CK behavior.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Domain Trust Discovery Hunt for T1482 Domain Trust Discovery
Technique Context
- Review LDAP, Global Catalog, SMB, and RPC traffic to domain controllers that may precede cross-domain or trust-aware movement.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 389 || port.dst == 636 || port.dst == 3268 || port.dst == 3269 || port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 135 || port.dst == 139) && packets > <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS>
What the search isolates: Directory and RPC sessions where a suspicious source may be querying trust relationships or domain topology.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Compare the source to known admin, identity, and monitoring systems that normally query domain topology.
2. Pivot into later sessions to other domains, privileged services, or cross-segment admin paths.
3. Review timing against account discovery, group discovery, Kerberos requests, and lateral transfers.
Validation steps:
- Confirm a non-admin or newly suspicious host is accessing directory services beyond normal logon behavior.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Domain joins, GPO processing, identity tools, AD health checks, and admin scripts.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when trust-discovery-like directory access is followed by remote service attempts into unusual domains or segments.
hunt_methods.title: T1482 Domain Trust Discovery Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify suspicious domain policy discovery preceding credential attacks.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface password-policy discovery candidates over directory and RPC paths.
prerequisites: Domain controller and RPC traffic is visible in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Supporting pivot because Arkime can surface suspicious network evidence, but host, identity, or application telemetry is needed to confirm the ATT&CK behavior.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Password Policy Discovery Hunt for T1201 Password Policy Discovery
Technique Context
- Review LDAP, SAMR/RPC, and domain-controller sessions from hosts that may be querying password policy before brute force or credential abuse.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 389 || port.dst == 636 || port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 135 || port.dst == 139 || port.dst == 3268 || port.dst == 3269) && packets > <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS>
What the search isolates: Directory and RPC access patterns that can support password policy discovery.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source to identify non-admin hosts querying domain services unusually.
2. Pivot into nearby brute-force, password spraying, Kerberos, SMB, RDP, or VPN access attempts.
3. Suppress approved identity audit tools, domain controllers, and administrator jump boxes.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source does not normally perform domain policy or SAMR-like queries.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Normal logon, domain joins, AD management tools, security audit tools, and EDR inventory.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when password-policy discovery candidates appear before authentication fan-out or credential-access activity.
hunt_methods.title: T1201 Password Policy Discovery Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify unusual Group Policy discovery through domain-controller SMB access.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface suspicious SMB access to domain policy distribution paths.
prerequisites: SMB traffic to domain controllers is visible in Arkime; SMB file-path fields, if retained, make this hunt stronger.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Supporting pivot because Arkime can surface suspicious network evidence, but host, identity, or application telemetry is needed to confirm the ATT&CK behavior.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime SYSVOL Group Policy Discovery Hunt for T1615 Group Policy Discovery
Technique Context
- Group Policy information is commonly exposed through predictable SYSVOL and policy paths; Arkime can surface suspicious SMB access to domain-controller file shares.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && port.dst == 445 && bytes >= <TINY_SIGNAL_BYTES>
What the search isolates: SMB sessions to domain infrastructure that may include SYSVOL, NETLOGON, or policy object discovery.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and destination domain controller to identify unusual SMB access to domain infrastructure.
2. Pivot into LDAP, Kerberos, WinRM, SMB transfer, or remote-service activity from the same source.
3. Suppress normal logon/GPO processing by looking for unusual source hosts, timing, volume, and follow-on behavior.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source and volume are unusual relative to ordinary workstation GPO processing.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Normal domain logon, GPO refresh, software deployment, and login scripts.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when unusual SYSVOL-like SMB activity occurs from a suspicious host and is followed by privilege or lateral movement behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1615 SYSVOL Group Policy Discovery Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify metadata-service access that may expose cloud credentials.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface access to cloud instance metadata endpoints and credential-bearing paths.
prerequisites: Traffic from cloud workloads to metadata endpoints is visible to Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can expose the relevant network path, protocol, destination reuse, timing, and byte/packet profile for analyst validation.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Cloud Metadata Credential Access Hunt for T1552.005 Cloud Instance Metadata API
Technique Context
- Cloud metadata services commonly expose instance identity and temporary credential material to local workloads.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (ip.dst == 169.254.169.254 || host.http == /(?i)(metadata\.google\.internal)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(metadata\.google\.internal)/ || http.uri == /(?i)(latest\/meta-data|latest\/user-data|iam\/security-credentials|metadata\/identity|instance\/service-accounts|computeMetadata)/)
What the search isolates: Direct or SSRF-like access to cloud metadata endpoints and credential paths.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, dns.host, http.uri, http.user-agent, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the source is a cloud workload that normally queries metadata and whether the path reaches credential material.
2. Pivot into prior public-facing web requests from external clients if SSRF is suspected.
3. Pivot forward into cloud API, storage, container API, or outbound C2 sessions using the same source.
Validation steps:
- Confirm access to credential-relevant metadata paths, unusual user agents, or bursts from workloads that do not normally query metadata.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Cloud agents, bootstrapping scripts, monitoring agents, autoscaling, and workload identity libraries.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when metadata credential access is unusual, follows inbound web requests, or is followed by cloud API activity from the same host.
hunt_methods.title: T1552.005 Cloud Metadata Credential Access Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify unapproved access to container APIs that may expose secrets or credentials.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface suspicious access to Docker, Kubernetes, and container API credential paths.
prerequisites: Container API ports, HTTP paths, or DNS names are visible in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can expose the relevant network path, protocol, destination reuse, timing, and byte/packet profile for analyst validation.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Container API Credential Access Hunt for T1552.007 Container API
Technique Context
- Docker and Kubernetes APIs can expose logs, secrets, service-account tokens, and workload metadata when abused.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && ((port.dst == 2375 || port.dst == 2376 || port.dst == 10250 || port.dst == 10255 || port.dst == 6443 || port.dst == 8001) || host.http == /(?i)(kubernetes|docker|k8s|openshift)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(kubernetes|docker|k8s|openshift)/ || http.uri == /(?i)(api\/v1\/namespaces|secrets|serviceaccounts|pods|logs|exec|containers)/)
What the search isolates: Candidate direct access to container control-plane APIs and credential-bearing API paths.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, host.http, dns.host, http.uri, http.method, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Separate expected control-plane clients from new or unusual API consumers.
2. Review URI paths for secrets, logs, service accounts, pod exec, and namespace enumeration.
3. Pivot into cloud metadata access, image registry access, mining pools, or external tool retrieval from the same source.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source is not an approved cluster component, administrator, or CI/CD runner.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Kubernetes nodes, controllers, CI/CD, image scanners, service meshes, and cluster administration.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when unapproved hosts touch container APIs, especially credential paths, and then access cloud APIs or external infrastructure.
hunt_methods.title: T1552.007 Container API Credential Access Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify suspicious certificate enrollment paths that may support authentication certificate abuse.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface unusual access to certificate enrollment and CA infrastructure.
prerequisites: AD CS web enrollment, CA, RPC, or PKI-related traffic is visible in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Supporting pivot because Arkime can surface suspicious network evidence, but host, identity, or application telemetry is needed to confirm the ATT&CK behavior.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime AD CS Certificate Enrollment Hunt for T1649 Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates
Technique Context
- Abuse of AD CS and certificate enrollment can create authentication material that survives password resets and enables privileged access.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (http.uri == /(?i)(certsrv|certfnsh|certnew|certcarc|certenroll|certcli|xcep|xenroll)/ || host.http == /(?i)(adcs|certsrv|pki|certificate|certauth|certenroll)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(adcs|certsrv|pki|certificate|certauth|certenroll)/)
What the search isolates: Certificate enrollment and CA-adjacent sessions that may support certificate theft or forging workflows.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, host.http, dns.host, http.uri, http.method, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Identify certificate authority and enrollment endpoints, then group by unusual source hosts.
2. Pivot into LDAP, Kerberos, SMB, WinRM, RDP, or cloud identity activity after certificate enrollment.
3. Compare to expected auto-enrollment, domain join, machine certificate renewal, and admin workflows.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source should not be requesting, renewing, or interacting with certificate enrollment endpoints.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Auto-enrollment, domain joins, machine renewal, VPN certificates, and PKI administration.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when unusual certificate enrollment activity appears near privilege discovery, remote access, or suspicious Kerberos usage.
hunt_methods.title: T1649 AD CS Certificate Enrollment Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify unusual remote-service access that may involve alternate authentication material.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface network paths that may represent use of alternate authentication material once correlated with identity telemetry.
prerequisites: Remote service traffic is visible in Arkime and identity telemetry is available for confirmation.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Supporting pivot because Arkime can surface suspicious network evidence, but host, identity, or application telemetry is needed to confirm the ATT&CK behavior.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Alternate Authentication Material Remote Access Hunt for T1550 Use Alternate Authentication Material
Technique Context
- Arkime cannot see the credential material, but it can surface unusual remote-service access patterns that should be correlated with authentication telemetry.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 3389 || port.dst == 5985 || port.dst == 5986 || port.dst == 22 || port.dst == 88 || port.dst == 464) && packets > <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS>
What the search isolates: Remote-service and Kerberos-adjacent sessions where alternate authentication material may be in use.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and remote-service protocol to identify new or rare access paths.
2. Correlate with domain, Kerberos, identity, and endpoint telemetry for pass-the-hash, pass-the-ticket, or token use.
3. Pivot into earlier credential access and later lateral transfer or egress.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the remote access path is rare, not an approved admin route, and aligns with suspicious authentication events.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Normal administration, automation, service accounts, patching, and jump-host activity.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when rare remote access appears from a suspicious host and authentication telemetry indicates token, hash, ticket, or certificate abuse.
hunt_methods.title: T1550 Alternate Authentication Material Remote Access Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify SMB and RPC movement patterns that may correspond to pass-the-hash activity.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface SMB/RPC/RDP paths that may indicate pass-the-hash lateral movement when validated with auth logs.
prerequisites: Internal SMB/RPC/RDP traffic is visible in Arkime and Windows authentication telemetry is available.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Supporting pivot because Arkime can surface suspicious network evidence, but host, identity, or application telemetry is needed to confirm the ATT&CK behavior.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime SMB Pass-the-Hash Candidate Hunt for T1550.002 Pass the Hash
Technique Context
- Pass-the-hash often manifests as SMB/RPC/RDP lateral movement paths, but Arkime must be correlated with Windows authentication telemetry for proof.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 135 || port.dst == 139 || port.dst == 3389) && packets > <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS>
What the search isolates: Candidate SMB/RPC/RDP access patterns where stolen NTLM material may have enabled movement.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source to find fan-out to many hosts over SMB/RPC/RDP.
2. Pivot into preceding credential access indicators and following admin-share transfers or service-control traffic.
3. Validate with Windows logon type, NTLM, account, and endpoint telemetry.
Validation steps:
- Confirm unusual NTLM-based access from a host that should not administer the destinations.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Admin scripts, software deployment, vulnerability scanners, backups, and endpoint management.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when SMB/RPC/RDP fan-out from a suspicious host aligns with NTLM authentication anomalies or lateral tool transfer.
hunt_methods.title: T1550.002 SMB Pass-the-Hash Candidate Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify unusual Kerberos-adjacent service paths that may correspond to pass-the-ticket activity.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface Kerberos-to-service sequences that may support pass-the-ticket validation.
prerequisites: Kerberos and remote-service traffic are visible in Arkime and Kerberos logs are available for confirmation.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Supporting pivot because Arkime can surface suspicious network evidence, but host, identity, or application telemetry is needed to confirm the ATT&CK behavior.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Kerberos Pass-the-Ticket Candidate Hunt for T1550.003 Pass the Ticket
Technique Context
- Pass-the-ticket is proven in Kerberos and endpoint logs, but Arkime can identify unusual Kerberos-to-service access sequences.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 88 || port.dst == 464 || port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 389 || port.dst == 3389 || port.dst == 5985 || port.dst == 5986) && packets > <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS>
What the search isolates: Kerberos-adjacent service access that may represent ticket-based movement when correlated with authentication events.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and sequence Kerberos traffic against later SMB, LDAP, WinRM, or RDP access.
2. Compare the source to expected admin and service-account paths.
3. Validate with Kerberos ticket, account, and endpoint telemetry.
Validation steps:
- Confirm suspicious Kerberos ticket use or service access from an endpoint that does not normally reach those services.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Normal domain logon, service accounts, admin scripts, and enterprise management tooling.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when unusual Kerberos-adjacent movement aligns with abnormal ticket events or privileged service access.
hunt_methods.title: T1550.003 Kerberos Pass-the-Ticket Candidate Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify network-visible write patterns to applications that may reflect unauthorized data manipulation.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface write-heavy application traffic that could represent data manipulation when validated with app logs.
prerequisites: HTTP method, host, and URI metadata are visible for the scoped application traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Supporting pivot because Arkime can surface suspicious network evidence, but host, identity, or application telemetry is needed to confirm the ATT&CK behavior.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Network Data Manipulation Candidate Hunt for T1565 Data Manipulation
Technique Context
- Network metadata can highlight write-heavy sessions to business systems, but application or endpoint telemetry is required to prove data manipulation.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (http.method == POST || http.method == PUT || http.method == PATCH || http.method == DELETE || http.method == PROPFIND) && bytes >= <HTTP_INFLATED_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Write-capable HTTP sessions to applications where data manipulation may be occurring.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, http.method, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source, application, method, and URI family to find unusual write-heavy behavior.
2. Pivot into authentication, admin access, repository, database, or cloud-storage sessions from the same source.
3. Validate application-layer changes using logs, database audit events, or integrity monitoring.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the writes are unauthorized, unexpected for the host role, or aligned to suspicious login and admin activity.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Normal API traffic, content management, CI/CD, admin workflows, and application batch jobs.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when write-heavy application traffic is rare for the source and application logs show suspicious modification or deletion.
hunt_methods.title: T1565 Network Data Manipulation Candidate Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify cleartext write-capable data flows that could be manipulated in transit.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface cleartext transmitted-data paths that may be susceptible to manipulation.
prerequisites: Cleartext protocol metadata and surrounding network paths are visible in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Supporting pivot because Arkime can surface suspicious network evidence, but host, identity, or application telemetry is needed to confirm the ATT&CK behavior.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Cleartext Transmitted Data Manipulation Hunt for T1565.002 Transmitted Data Manipulation
Technique Context
- Transmitted data manipulation is most reviewable when sensitive workflows still use cleartext or downgrade-prone protocols.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (protocols == http || port.dst == 80 || port.dst == 25 || port.dst == 110 || port.dst == 143 || port.dst == 21) && (http.method == POST || http.method == PUT || http.method == PATCH || http.method == DELETE || bytes >= <HTTP_INFLATED_BYTES>)
What the search isolates: Cleartext or weakly protected sessions where transmitted data may be altered or abused.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, host.http, http.uri, http.method, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Identify sensitive applications or workflows using cleartext paths.
2. Pivot into AiTM, proxy, gateway, ARP, DNS, or LLMNR/NBNS anomalies near the same time.
3. Validate with application integrity checks, server logs, endpoint telemetry, or packet review.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the traffic is sensitive, write-capable, and inconsistent with approved application behavior.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Legacy applications, internal APIs, test environments, lab systems, and non-sensitive telemetry.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when cleartext write traffic is sensitive and coincides with AiTM positioning, gateway drift, or unexpected content changes.
hunt_methods.title: T1565.002 Cleartext Transmitted Data Manipulation Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify injected or suspicious update-like content delivery through network traffic.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface browser and update-like delivery paths that may reflect malicious content injection.
prerequisites: HTTP user-agent, URI, and DNS metadata are available for web traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can expose the relevant network path, protocol, destination reuse, timing, and byte/packet profile for analyst validation.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Content Injection Delivery Hunt for T1659 Content Injection
Technique Context
- Content injection can appear as browser traffic that receives payload-like content from unexpected network paths or altered update/download flows.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && http.user-agent == /(?i)(mozilla|chrome|safari|edge|firefox)/ && http.method == GET && (http.uri == /(?i)(update|download|installer|setup|windowsupdate|flash|chrome|edge|office|\.exe$|\.msi$|\.js$|\.hta$|\.dll$|\.ps1$)/ || bytes >= <PAYLOAD_RETRIEVAL_BYTES>)
What the search isolates: Browser-driven downloads and update-like payload retrievals that may be injected into otherwise legitimate traffic.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, http.user-agent, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Compare the destination, hostname, and path against expected vendor update infrastructure.
2. Pivot into DNS, gateway, proxy, and AiTM signals near the same time window.
3. Pivot forward into C2, staging, or remote-service activity from the same endpoint.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the payload-like content comes from a rare or mismatched destination, especially after DNS or HTTP anomalies.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Legitimate software updates, browser downloads, package managers, and internal software portals.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when an update-like download path resolves to rare infrastructure or coincides with AiTM and post-delivery communications.
hunt_methods.title: T1659 Content Injection Delivery Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify inbound mail session-count spikes that can distract users or precede social-engineering escalation.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface mail-related sessions that can be grouped into inbound email-flood patterns.
prerequisites: Mail server sessions are visible in Arkime and target mail infrastructure is in scope.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can expose the relevant network path, protocol, destination reuse, timing, and byte/packet profile for analyst validation.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Inbound Mail Flood Hunt for T1667 Email Bombing
Technique Context
- Email bombing can appear as a sudden spike of inbound mail delivery sessions or repeated connections to mail infrastructure.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 25 || port.dst == 465 || port.dst == 587 || port.dst == 993 || port.dst == 995) && bytes >= <TINY_SIGNAL_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Mail-related sessions to group by destination, source, and time window to identify floods that are session-count-driven rather than large per-session transfers.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by destination mail server and then by source, time bucket, and session count to identify floods, distributed senders, or repeated delivery attempts.
2. Pivot into remote support, voice-social-engineering, helpdesk-themed domains, or unusual authentication activity around the same target window.
3. Compare against normal campaign mail, marketing systems, and known bulk senders.
Validation steps:
- Confirm an abnormal spike in mail sessions or message delivery to a limited destination or user population.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Marketing campaigns, mailing lists, alerts, bulk notifications, and mail-server retry storms.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a mail flood targets specific users and coincides with remote-access lures, MFA fatigue, or suspicious helpdesk contact.
hunt_methods.title: T1667 Inbound Mail Flood Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify suspicious write-capable activity against public web content paths.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface web write activity that could precede external defacement.
prerequisites: HTTP method and URI metadata are visible for public web infrastructure.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Supporting pivot because Arkime can surface suspicious network evidence, but host, identity, or application telemetry is needed to confirm the ATT&CK behavior.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime External Web Defacement Candidate Hunt for T1491.002 External Defacement
Technique Context
- External defacement often requires write-capable access to public web infrastructure before public-facing content changes.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (http.method == POST || http.method == PUT || http.method == PATCH || http.method == DELETE || http.method == PROPFIND) && http.uri == /(?i)(wp-admin|administrator|admin|cms|upload|filemanager|webdav|themes|templates|index\.php|index\.html|home|default\.aspx)/
What the search isolates: Write-capable HTTP sessions to public website administration, CMS, upload, or WebDAV paths.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, http.method, http.user-agent, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Separate known administrators, CI/CD, and CMS maintenance from rare external or internal sources.
2. Pivot into prior exploit-surface probing, credential attacks, or web-shell staging against the same site.
3. Validate with web server logs, content integrity monitoring, and change-management records.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the write-capable session is unauthorized or outside expected maintenance workflows.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Normal CMS publishing, web administration, CI/CD deployments, and WebDAV-based content management.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when suspicious write-capable web sessions are followed by altered public content or defacement indicators.
hunt_methods.title: T1491.002 External Web Defacement Candidate Hunt

# Arkime WebDAV Write Abuse Hunt for T1190 Exploit Public-Facing Application
Technique Context
- WebDAV and write-capable HTTP methods can be abused for upload, staging, or web-shell placement on exposed applications.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (http.method == PUT || http.method == PROPFIND || http.method == MKCOL || http.method == COPY || http.method == MOVE || http.method == DELETE)
What the search isolates: Write-capable HTTP/WebDAV traffic against exposed services that may indicate exploitation or staging.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, http.method, http.user-agent, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by host and method to isolate unexpected WebDAV or write methods.
2. Pivot into adjacent GET/POST sessions for shell access, payload retrieval, or callbacks.
3. Validate against application logs and approved WebDAV/content-management workflows.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the target should not receive write-capable methods from the source.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- WebDAV publishing, CMS operations, CI/CD, and admin maintenance.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when unexpected write methods are followed by suspicious paths, new outbound sessions, or payload retrieval.
hunt_methods.title: T1190 WebDAV Write Abuse Hunt

hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Fake Update Payload Retrieval Hunt for T1189 Drive-by Compromise
Technique Context
- Fake update and browser-driven payload flows often use familiar product terms with executable, script, archive, or installer-like objects.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && http.user-agent == /(?i)(mozilla|chrome|safari|edge|firefox)/ && http.method == GET && http.uri == /(?i)(chrome|edge|browser|flash|adobe|teams|zoom|office|windows|update|installer|setup).*(\.exe|\.msi|\.zip|\.iso|\.img|\.js|\.hta|\.ps1|\.vbs)/
What the search isolates: Browser downloads that look like fake updates, installers, or staged payloads.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, http.user-agent, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review the destination reputation and whether the product/update path matches the vendor.
2. Pivot into prior landing-page traffic and later C2 or remote access from the same endpoint.
3. Compare with software distribution and browser update baselines.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the download came from a rare or mismatched destination and is followed by suspicious network activity.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Legitimate software updates, helpdesk downloads, and admin-driven installers.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when a fake-update-looking download is from rare infrastructure and precedes post-delivery communications.
hunt_methods.title: T1189 Fake Update Payload Retrieval Hunt

# Arkime RMM First-Contact Burst Hunt for T1219 Remote Access Tools
Technique Context
- New RMM installation or abuse often creates initial check-ins to vendor infrastructure from hosts that have no prior support-tool baseline.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && bytes <= <SMALL_BEACON_BYTES> && (host.http == /(?i)(atera|syncro|screenconnect|connectwise|anydesk|teamviewer|splashtop|rustdesk|simple-help|simplehelp|level\.io|tacticalrmm|ninjaone|datto|meshcentral)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(atera|syncro|screenconnect|connectwise|anydesk|teamviewer|splashtop|rustdesk|simple-help|simplehelp|level\.io|tacticalrmm|ninjaone|datto|meshcentral)/)
What the search isolates: Small initial remote-management check-ins that may be the first sign of RMM abuse.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, dns.host, ja3, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Identify hosts with first-seen or rare RMM vendor traffic.
2. Pivot into surrounding payload retrieval, discovery, credential access, or cloud storage sessions.
3. Compare against approved helpdesk and managed service provider tooling.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the RMM vendor is not approved or the host has no prior baseline for that tool.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Legitimate remote support, MSP onboarding, or helpdesk troubleshooting.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when first-contact RMM traffic appears after phishing, exploit delivery, or suspicious user activity.
hunt_methods.title: T1219 RMM First-Contact Burst Hunt

# Arkime Archive And Script Retrieval Hunt for T1105 Ingress Tool Transfer
Technique Context
- Tool transfer often appears as retrieval of archives, installers, scripts, or binaries from rare external infrastructure.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && http.method == GET && (http.uri == /(?i)\.(zip|rar|7z|tar|gz|iso|img|exe|dll|msi|ps1|vbs|js|hta|jar|bat|cmd)$/ || bytes >= <PAYLOAD_RETRIEVAL_BYTES>)
What the search isolates: Outbound payload retrieval that can stage tools into the environment.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, http.user-agent, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Review whether the host normally retrieves tools or archives from the destination.
2. Pivot into execution-adjacent network activity: C2, remote services, cloud storage, or staging.
3. Compare destination against package mirrors, software portals, and approved repositories.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the file type, host, and destination are rare or suspicious for the source host.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Software installation, admin downloads, package managers, and developer workflows.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when rare payload retrieval is followed by C2, discovery, remote service, or exfiltration behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1105 Archive And Script Retrieval Hunt

# Arkime WebSocket Command Channel Hunt for T1071.001 Web Protocols
Technique Context
- WebSocket upgrades can sustain interactive web-based C2 while blending into browser or application traffic.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (http.uri == /(?i)(websocket|socket|ws|wss|signalr|sockjs|socket\.io)/ || http.user-agent == /(?i)(websocket|socket\.io|python|go-http-client|curl|powershell)/) && bytes <= <SMALL_BEACON_BYTES>
What the search isolates: WebSocket-like control sessions, especially low-volume repeated channels.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, http.user-agent, bytes, packets, ja3, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and host to find repeated low-volume WebSocket channels.
2. Compare against approved chat, monitoring, SaaS, and application frameworks.
3. Pivot into payload retrieval, DNS, TLS JA3, and later exfiltration from the same source.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the destination or user-agent is rare and the channel repeats like tasking rather than normal application use.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- SaaS apps, chat platforms, dashboards, dev tools, and application telemetry.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when rare WebSocket-like traffic is stable, repeated, and adjacent to payload transfer or staging.
hunt_methods.title: T1071.001 WebSocket Command Channel Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify DNS-based tasking or payload channels using session byte and repetition patterns.

# Arkime DNS TXT-Style Payload Hunt for T1071.004 DNS
Technique Context
- DNS C2 often creates repeated inflated DNS sessions, long encoded labels, or destination reuse that does not match normal resolver behavior.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && protocols == dns && bytes >= <DNS_SESSION_BYTES> && packets <= <LOW_PACKET_THRESHOLD>
What the search isolates: Small DNS conversations with higher-than-normal byte counts that can represent DNS tasking or payload fragments.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, dns.host, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and queried domain family to identify repeated inflated DNS exchanges.
2. Review label length, randomness, and destination resolver behavior.
3. Pivot into later HTTP, TLS, or exfiltration sessions to the same infrastructure.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the domain family is rare, labels look encoded, and the source repeats the pattern over time.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- EDR telemetry, CDN/service discovery, enterprise software, and DNS-based load balancing.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when inflated DNS recurs to rare domains and is adjacent to payload transfer or other C2 behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1071.004 DNS TXT-Style Payload Hunt

# Arkime CDN Fronting Reuse Hunt for T1090.004 Domain Fronting
Technique Context
- Domain fronting candidates often involve repeated HTTPS sessions to large CDN or cloud front doors from hosts that do not normally use those services.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && (host.http == /(?i)(cloudfront\.net|azureedge\.net|frontdoor\.azure\.com|trafficmanager\.net|workers\.dev|pages\.dev|fastly\.net|appspot\.com|herokuapp\.com)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(cloudfront\.net|azureedge\.net|frontdoor\.azure\.com|trafficmanager\.net|workers\.dev|pages\.dev|fastly\.net|appspot\.com|herokuapp\.com)/) && bytes <= <SMALL_BEACON_BYTES>
What the search isolates: Low-volume repeated cloud/CDN front-door sessions that may mask backend C2 infrastructure.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, dns.host, ja3, cert.subject.cn, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source, fronting domain, JA3, and destination to find stable low-volume reuse.
2. Compare against expected SaaS/CDN use for the host role.
3. Pivot into payload retrieval, tasking, proxying, or exfiltration around the same time window.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the front-door service is rare for the source and repeats in a beacon-like pattern.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- SaaS apps, CDNs, serverless apps, package hosting, and legitimate cloud services.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when rare CDN-fronted traffic recurs with suspicious JA3 or surrounding C2/exfil behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1090.004 CDN Fronting Reuse Hunt

# Arkime Cloud Drive Bulk Upload Hunt for T1567.002 Exfiltration to Cloud Storage
Technique Context
- Cloud-drive exfiltration often appears as large outbound sessions to storage providers from hosts that do not normally upload bulk data.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && bytes >= <LARGE_TRANSFER_BYTES> && (host.http == /(?i)(drive\.google\.com|docs\.google\.com|dropbox|box\.com|onedrive|sharepoint|blob\.core\.windows\.net|s3\.amazonaws\.com|storage\.googleapis\.com|mega\.nz|pcloud|mediafire)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(drive\.google\.com|docs\.google\.com|dropbox|box\.com|onedrive|sharepoint|blob\.core\.windows\.net|s3\.amazonaws\.com|storage\.googleapis\.com|mega\.nz|pcloud|mediafire)/)
What the search isolates: Large outbound cloud-storage sessions that may represent data upload.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, dns.host, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, ja3
Session-review workflow:
1. Separate sanctioned cloud-sync clients and approved storage workflows from unusual sources.
2. Pivot backward into staging, compression, repository access, SMB collection, or mailbox access.
3. Pivot forward into repeated uploads or new storage destinations.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the host does not normally upload at this volume to the cloud provider.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Cloud sync, backup, collaboration, file sharing, and software distribution.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when bulk cloud upload follows collection/staging or originates from a host with no cloud-storage baseline.
hunt_methods.title: T1567.002 Cloud Drive Bulk Upload Hunt

prerequisites: Internal RPC, SMB, and high-port Windows traffic is visible in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can expose the relevant network path, protocol, destination reuse, timing, and byte/packet profile for analyst validation.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime DCOM RPC Follow-On Hunt for T1021.003 Distributed Component Object Model
Technique Context
- DCOM lateral movement typically sits near RPC endpoint mapper traffic and follow-on SMB, service, or admin sessions.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 135 || port.dst == 445 || port.dst >= 49152) && packets > <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS>
What the search isolates: RPC/DCOM-adjacent sessions that may represent remote COM activation or lateral administration.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and destination to identify rare RPC/DCOM paths.
2. Pivot into nearby SMB transfer, WinRM, RDP, Kerberos, and outbound sessions from the target.
3. Suppress approved management servers and endpoint tooling.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source does not normally use RPC/DCOM to the destination.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Windows administration, WMI/DCOM management, endpoint tools, and software deployment.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when rare RPC/DCOM traffic follows discovery and is paired with transfer or outbound tasking.
hunt_methods.title: T1021.003 DCOM RPC Follow-On Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify suspicious WMI-adjacent management sessions.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface WMI/RPC management paths that may support remote execution when validated with host telemetry.
prerequisites: Internal RPC, SMB, WinRM, and high-port Windows traffic is visible in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Supporting pivot because Arkime can surface suspicious network evidence, but host, identity, or application telemetry is needed to confirm the ATT&CK behavior.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime WMI RPC Pivot Follow-On Hunt for T1047 Windows Management Instrumentation
Technique Context
- WMI remote execution is network-adjacent to RPC endpoint mapper, dynamic high ports, SMB, and later outbound traffic from the target.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 135 || port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 5985 || port.dst == 5986 || port.dst >= 49152) && packets > <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS>
What the search isolates: WMI/RPC/WinRM-adjacent traffic worth validating with endpoint and Windows event telemetry.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and target to identify rare management paths.
2. Pivot into prior discovery and later payload transfer or outbound target communications.
3. Validate with WMI provider/process telemetry and Windows events.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source is not an approved management server and the target shows WMI activity.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Systems management, monitoring, configuration management, EDR, and admin tooling.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when rare WMI/RPC paths occur after discovery and before suspicious target outbound activity.
hunt_methods.title: T1047 WMI RPC Pivot Follow-On Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify forced-authentication patterns around LLMNR/NBNS and SMB relay.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface local name-resolution and SMB relay prerequisites.
prerequisites: Internal broadcast/name-resolution and SMB traffic is visible in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can expose the relevant network path, protocol, destination reuse, timing, and byte/packet profile for analyst validation.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime LLMNR NBNS Forced Auth Burst Hunt for T1557.001 LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay
Technique Context
- Name-resolution poisoning and relay often create local name-resolution noise followed by SMB or HTTP authentication attempts to an unusual host.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_INTERNAL_SCOPE>) && (port.dst == 137 || port.dst == 138 || port.dst == 139 || port.dst == 445 || port.dst == 5355) && packets <= <LOW_PACKET_THRESHOLD>
What the search isolates: LLMNR/NBNS/SMB-adjacent sessions that may indicate forced authentication or relay setup.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by destination to identify hosts receiving name-resolution or SMB traffic from many peers.
2. Pivot into HTTP/SMB sessions to the same destination and later lateral movement from that host.
3. Compare against printers, file servers, domain controllers, and name-resolution infrastructure.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the destination is not expected to receive many name-resolution or authentication attempts.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Printers, file shares, discovery protocols, legacy name resolution, and misconfigured clients.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when an unusual host receives local name-resolution traffic and then initiates SMB/RDP/WinRM movement.
hunt_methods.title: T1557.001 LLMNR NBNS Forced Auth Burst Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify cryptomining resource hijacking through pool and Stratum traffic.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface mining pool and Stratum-like network traffic.
prerequisites: Outbound DNS, HTTP, and TCP sessions from servers or cloud workloads are visible.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can expose the relevant network path, protocol, destination reuse, timing, and byte/packet profile for analyst validation.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime Mining Pool Stratum Fallback Hunt for T1496 Resource Hijacking
Technique Context
- Cryptomining often uses mining pool hostnames, Stratum ports, or persistent outbound sessions from servers and cloud workloads.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && ((port.dst == 3333 || port.dst == 4444 || port.dst == 5555 || port.dst == 7777 || port.dst == 14444) || host.http == /(?i)(stratum|mining|pool|xmr|monero|nanopool|supportxmr|minexmr|nicehash)/ || dns.host == /(?i)(stratum|mining|pool|xmr|monero|nanopool|supportxmr|minexmr|nicehash)/)
What the search isolates: Mining pool and Stratum-like sessions that can indicate resource hijacking.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, host.http, dns.host, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Identify hosts with direct mining-pool or Stratum-like traffic.
2. Pivot into prior exploit delivery, container API access, cloud metadata access, or payload retrieval.
3. Compare to any approved mining, research, or lab systems.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the source should not mine cryptocurrency and the destination aligns with mining infrastructure.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Research labs, developer testing, blockchain services, and false-positive pool strings.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when mining-pool traffic appears from servers, containers, or cloud workloads after exploit or payload retrieval.
hunt_methods.title: T1496 Mining Pool Stratum Fallback Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify application exhaustion through high-volume expensive endpoint requests.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface application-layer request floods against expensive endpoints.
prerequisites: HTTP URI and method metadata are visible for application traffic.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can expose the relevant network path, protocol, destination reuse, timing, and byte/packet profile for analyst validation.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime HTTP API Exhaustion Hunt for T1499.003 Application Exhaustion Flood
Technique Context
- Application exhaustion may appear as repeated high-rate requests to expensive endpoints rather than simple packet floods.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (http.method == GET || http.method == POST) && packets >= <FLOOD_PACKET_THRESHOLD> && http.uri == /(?i)(search|login|auth|report|export|api|graphql|query|download|generate)/
What the search isolates: High-volume request patterns against expensive or sensitive application endpoints.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, host.http, http.uri, http.method, packets, bytes, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source, URI family, and destination application to identify concentrated endpoint abuse.
2. Compare to load testing, monitoring, and known user workflows.
3. Pivot into source diversity, user-agent reuse, and upstream proxy or CDN paths.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the request volume is abnormal for the endpoint and affects application capacity or availability.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Load tests, monitoring, batch jobs, scraping, and legitimate reporting/export workflows.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when high-rate expensive endpoint use comes from unusual sources or causes service degradation.
hunt_methods.title: T1499.003 HTTP API Exhaustion Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify masqueraded application channels through protocol-port drift.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface protocol-port mismatches that may indicate protocol impersonation.
prerequisites: HTTP/TLS parser classification and port metadata are available.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can expose the relevant network path, protocol, destination reuse, timing, and byte/packet profile for analyst validation.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime TLS Nonstandard Port Impersonation Hunt for T1001.003 Protocol Impersonation
Technique Context
- Protocol impersonation candidates include TLS or HTTP-like traffic on unexpected service ports and small repeated sessions that do not fit the advertised service.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && ((protocols == tls && port.dst != 443 && port.dst != 8443) || (protocols == http && port.dst != 80 && port.dst != 8080 && port.dst != 443 && port.dst != 8443)) && bytes <= <SMALL_BEACON_BYTES>
What the search isolates: HTTP/TLS parser hits on nonstandard ports that may reflect masqueraded application channels.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, port.dst, protocols, host.http, dns.host, ja3, cert.subject.cn, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen
Session-review workflow:
1. Group by source and destination to find repeated nonstandard-port application channels.
2. Compare port/protocol combinations to approved proxies, appliances, and developer services.
3. Pivot into payload retrieval, fallback C2, and exfiltration behavior.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the protocol-port pairing is rare for the host and recurs to stable infrastructure.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- Proxies, admin panels, dev services, appliances, and custom enterprise apps.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when nonstandard-port HTTP/TLS is rare, repeated, and adjacent to other C2 or transfer behavior.
hunt_methods.title: T1001.003 TLS Nonstandard Port Impersonation Hunt

hunt_methods.behavior_focus: Identify SSH tunneling and relay behavior through duration, volume, and reuse.
hunt_methods.expectation: Surface SSH sessions that may act as protocol tunnels or relays.
prerequisites: Outbound SSH sessions are visible in Arkime.
hunt_methods.strength_reason: Primary hunt because Arkime can expose the relevant network path, protocol, destination reuse, timing, and byte/packet profile for analyst validation.
hunt_methods.surface_details: Use Arkime session metadata, parser fields, and surrounding-session pivots to isolate network behavior.
hunt_methods.template:
# Arkime SSH Tunnel Relay Hunt for T1572 Protocol Tunneling
Technique Context
- SSH tunnels can create long-lived or high-byte sessions that front later access or egress through a single external relay.
Search expression: (<ARKIME_SESSION_SCOPE>) && (<ARKIME_OUTBOUND_SCOPE>) && port.dst == 22 && (bytes >= <MID_TRANSFER_BYTES> || packets > <INTERACTIVE_SESSION_PACKETS>)
What the search isolates: SSH sessions that may carry tunneled application traffic rather than normal shell administration.
Facets to pivot on: ip.src, ip.dst, bytes, packets, firstSeen, lastSeen, communityId
Session-review workflow:
1. Compare the SSH destination and duration against approved administration and DevOps paths.
2. Pivot into nearby sessions from the same source that stop or shift after the tunnel starts.
3. Review whether the same external SSH destination is reused by multiple endpoints.
Validation steps:
- Confirm the SSH path is rare for the host and has volume or duration inconsistent with normal admin use.
Likely benign explanations / noise:
- DevOps administration, Git over SSH, backup jobs, and legitimate port forwarding.
Escalation guidance: Escalate when rare SSH sessions front other suspicious traffic or follow payload retrieval and discovery.
hunt_methods.title: T1572 SSH Tunnel Relay Hunt
